Kuch type karo...
# Python for CBSE Classes XI & XII import pandas as pd import numpy as np def learn_python(student): topics = ['variables', 'loops', 'functions', 'OOP', 'pandas', 'numpy', 'data_structures'] for topic in topics: student.study(topic) student.practice(topic) return student.succeed() class Student: def __init__(self, name, grade): self.name = name self.grade = grade def succeed(self): print(f"{self.name} scored 100!") # Start your journey today s = Student("You", "XII") learn_python(s) # Output: You scored 100!

🎯 Book a Free Demo Class

Your Computer Science Mentor —
Mr. Deepak Kannaujaiya

HOD & PGT Computer Science · M.Tech (CSE) · B.Ed · CBSE Appointed Subject Evaluator

"Computer Science is not difficult — it just needs the right guidance. My goal is to break down every concept so clearly that students not only understand it, but excel in their board examinations."

Mr. Deepak Kannaujaiya is a passionate PGT Computer Science educator with 5 years of teaching experience across CBSE-affiliated schools. Currently serving as HOD of Computer Science at Udaya International School, he has previously taught at Raj Global Academy and GR Academy. He holds an M.Tech in Computer Science & Engineering from Dr. APJ Abdul Kalam University, Lucknow, and a B.Ed from Siddharth University.

Recognised by CBSE as a Subject Evaluator (AISSCE) for Computer Science with Python (083) and Artificial Intelligence (417) in 2022 and 2024, and appointed as IT Head for SAFAL Examinations in 2024–25 and 2025–26. He has also published 4 research papers at IEEE Conferences. Through CS with Deepak Sir, he brings this expertise directly to students online — making quality CBSE preparation accessible to all.

5+ Years Experience
2000+ Happy Students
100% Board Results
4 IEEE Papers
🔧 Areas of Expertise
🐍 Python Programming 💻 Computer Science (083) 📊 Informatics Practices 🤖 Artificial Intelligence (417) 🗄️ MySQL & Database Management ☕ Java & C++ 🌐 HTML, CSS & Web Basics 🔒 Cybersecurity & Networking 📝 CBSE Board Exam Preparation
🏆
CBSE Appointed Subject Evaluator — CS & AI Officially appointed by CBSE to evaluate AISSCE answer sheets for Computer Science (083) and Artificial Intelligence (417) in 2022 and 2024.
📄
4 Research Papers Published at IEEE Conference Contributed original research to IEEE-level academic conferences, demonstrating deep subject expertise beyond the classroom.
🎯
100% Board Results — Every Session Students consistently score top marks in CBSE board exams; trained students for National & International Coding Competitions.
🏫
HOD & IT Head — Udaya International School Currently heading the Computer Science department and managing school IT infrastructure, smart classrooms, and digital learning tools.

📋 Full Profile

Resume Highlights — Mr. Deepak Kannaujaiya

Click on each section below to explore in detail 👇

🎯 Career Objective

▼

Passionate and dedicated PGT Computer Science teacher with 5 years of experience in teaching computer science concepts, programming, and emerging technologies. Skilled in curriculum design, classroom management, and fostering a technology-driven learning environment — committed to contributing expertise to an institution that values innovation and academic excellence.

💼 Teaching Experience 5 Years

▼
HOD, Computer Science — Udaya International School, SKN(April 2024 – Present)
  • Taught Computer Science to secondary & senior secondary students
  • Managed school IT infrastructure, software, and computer labs
  • Conducted coding clubs, robotics projects & extracurricular IT activities
PGT Computer Science & IT Teacher — Raj Global Academy, SKN(Aug 2023 – Apr 2024)
  • Taught CS to Class 11 & 12 as per CBSE curriculum
  • Conducted hands-on programming sessions — Python, Java, C++, Database Management
  • Organized workshops on AI, Cybersecurity & IoT
  • Mentored students for Olympiads & IT Fests
  • Managed Smart Classroom tech, LMS & digital learning tools
PGT Computer Science Teacher — GR Academy, SKN(Sep 2021 – Jul 2023)
  • Maintained records of assessments, attendance, planning & syllabus
  • Taught Python programming, networking, graphics, HTML & IT management

🎓 Academic Background

▼
  • M.Tech (CSE) — BIT GKP, Dr. APJ Abdul Kalam University, Lucknow (2023) — 75.45%
  • B.Tech (IT) — BIT GKP, Dr. APJ Abdul Kalam University, Lucknow (2021) — 75.47%
  • B.Ed — Siddharth University, Kapilvastu, Siddharth Nagar (2025) — 72%
  • Intermediate — HRIC Khalilabad, Sant Kabir Nagar (2017) — 67%
  • SSC (High School) — HRIC Khalilabad, Sant Kabir Nagar (2015) — 71%

📜 Certifications & Other Roles

▼
✅ STET Paper 1 & 2 Qualified 💻 CCC Certified 📘 'O' Level 📊 MS Office (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) & Tally Prime 2.0 ⌨️ Typing Speed — 35 WPM 🐍 Python · ☕ Java · 💠 C++ · 🔒 Cybersecurity
  • CBSE Appointed Subject Evaluator (AISSCE) — Computer Science with Python (083) & Artificial Intelligence (417), 2022 & 2024
  • CBSE Appointed IT Head for SAFAL Exams — 2024-25 & 2025-26

▼
  • Strong desire to redefine teaching standards — fluent teaching in English & Hindi
  • Skilled in Hardware, Graphic Design, Video Editing, Website & ERP Management
  • Experienced in organizing child development activities — games, field trips, storytelling

☕ IT Class 12
Unit 1: Communication Skills-IV Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-IV Unit 3: ICT Skills-IV Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-IV Unit 5: Green Skills-IV Unit 1: Database – RDBMS Unit 2: Web Based Applications Unit 3: Java Programming Unit 4: Work Integrated Learning

🗄️ IT Class XII (802) — Unit 1: Database Concepts & RDBMS (MySQL)

Koi result nahi mila. Dusra keyword try karein.
🗄️
CBSE Class XII · IT Code 802 · Part B · Unit 1

Database Concepts & RDBMS Tool (MySQL)

Comprehensive notes covering Database fundamentals, RDBMS concepts, Data Models, Keys, Relationships, SQL commands (DDL, DML, DCL, TCL) and MySQL queries — as per CBSE syllabus.

📚 8 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💡 Examples Included 🗃️ MySQL Queries

Topic 1 Introduction to Database

▼
📖 Database (DB) A Database is an organised collection of related data stored in a structured format so that it can be easily accessed, managed, and updated. It eliminates redundancy and ensures data integrity.
📖 Database Management System (DBMS) A DBMS is a software system that enables users to define, create, maintain, and control access to a database. Examples: MySQL, Oracle, MS Access, PostgreSQL, SQLite.
⭐ Why DBMS? — Advantages over File System
  • Reduced Data Redundancy — Same data not stored multiple times.
  • Data Consistency — Change once, reflected everywhere.
  • Data Integrity — Rules ensure only valid data is stored.
  • Data Security — Access control prevents unauthorised use.
  • Data Sharing — Multiple users can access simultaneously.
  • Backup & Recovery — Automatic backup mechanisms.
  • Data Independence — App programs independent of data storage.

Disadvantages of Traditional File System

  • Data Redundancy and Inconsistency
  • Difficulty in accessing data
  • Data isolation — data scattered in multiple files
  • Integrity problems
  • Atomicity problems
  • Concurrent access anomalies
  • Security problems

Topic 2 Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)

▼
📖 RDBMS An RDBMS is a type of DBMS that stores data in the form of tables (relations). Tables are linked using keys. It is based on E.F. Codd's relational model.

Popular RDBMS: MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, SQLite.

Basic Terminology

TermMeaningExample
Table / RelationCollection of data organised in rows and columnsSTUDENT table
Row / Tuple / RecordA single horizontal entry in a tableOne student's complete data
Column / Attribute / FieldA vertical entry representing one propertyName, Age, Marks
DegreeTotal number of columns in a tableIf 4 columns → Degree = 4
CardinalityTotal number of rows in a tableIf 5 rows → Cardinality = 5
DomainSet of valid values for an attributeAge domain: 1–120
SchemaStructure/design of the database (table names, columns)STUDENT(RollNo, Name, Age)
InstanceActual data stored in the database at a particular timeCurrent rows in STUDENT
📊 Example Table — STUDENT
RollNoNameAgeCityMarks
101Aman Sharma17Delhi89
102Priya Singh16Mumbai92
103Rohan Gupta17Delhi78
104Neha Verma16Lucknow95

Degree = 5 (columns) | Cardinality = 4 (rows)

Topic 3 Keys in RDBMS

▼
⭐ Keys — Most Important for Exam!
Keys are used to uniquely identify rows in a table and to establish relationships between tables.
🔑 1. Candidate Key An attribute (or set of attributes) that can uniquely identify each row in a table. A table can have multiple candidate keys.
Properties: Unique + Not Null.
Example: In STUDENT table — RollNo and Email both can be candidate keys.
🔑 2. Primary Key (PK) The candidate key chosen by the designer to uniquely identify each row. A table has only one primary key.
Properties: Unique + Not Null.
Example: RollNo is chosen as Primary Key in STUDENT table.
🔑 3. Alternate Key All candidate keys except the primary key are called Alternate Keys.
Example: If RollNo is PK, then Email becomes the Alternate Key.
🔑 4. Foreign Key (FK) An attribute in one table that is the Primary Key of another table. It creates a link between two tables.
Example: In MARKS table, RollNo refers to RollNo in STUDENT table — so RollNo in MARKS is a Foreign Key.
Referential Integrity: FK value must exist in the referenced table or be NULL.
🔑 5. Composite Key A key made of two or more attributes that together uniquely identify a row.
Example: (StudentID + SubjectCode) together can be a composite key in EXAM table.
🔑 6. Super Key Any set of attributes that can uniquely identify a row. Every Primary Key is a Super Key but vice-versa is not always true.
Example: {RollNo}, {RollNo, Name}, {RollNo, Age} — all are super keys.
📌 Key Summary Table
KeyUnique?Not Null?Count per Table
Candidate Key✅ Yes✅ YesCan be many
Primary Key✅ Yes✅ YesOnly ONE
Alternate Key✅ Yes✅ YesCandidates minus PK
Foreign Key❌ Not required❌ Can be NULLCan be many
Composite Key✅ Together✅ YesCan be many
Super Key✅ YesMay/may notMany
📊 Example — Two Linked Tables

STUDENT

RollNo (PK)NameCity
101AmanDelhi
102PriyaMumbai

MARKS

SrNo (PK)RollNo (FK→STUDENT)SubjectMarks
1101IT89
2101Maths92
3102IT78

RollNo in MARKS is a Foreign Key referencing RollNo (PK) of STUDENT table.

Topic 4 Data Models & ER Diagram

▼

Types of Data Models

🗂️ Hierarchical Model

Data is organised in a tree structure (parent-child). One parent, multiple children. Example: File system.

  • Fast access
  • Rigid structure
  • Complex many-to-many relations
🔗 Network Model

Data is organised as a graph. Records can have multiple parent and child records. Overcomes hierarchical limitations.

  • Flexible
  • Complex to design
  • Hard to modify
📋 Relational Model

Data stored in tables (relations). Tables linked via keys. Based on E.F. Codd's 12 rules. Most popular today.

  • Simple tabular format
  • Easy to query (SQL)
  • MySQL, Oracle use this
🔷 Object-Oriented Model

Data stored as objects like in OOP. Objects have attributes and methods. Used in complex applications.

  • Handles complex data
  • Reusability
  • Less common for CBSE

Entity-Relationship (ER) Model

📖 ER Diagram An Entity-Relationship Diagram is a visual representation of entities (objects) and the relationships between them. It is used in database design.

ER Diagram Components

SymbolRepresentsExample
RectangleEntity (real-world object)STUDENT, TEACHER, BOOK
Ellipse / OvalAttribute (property of entity)Name, Age, RollNo
Double EllipseMulti-valued AttributePhone (many numbers)
Dashed EllipseDerived AttributeAge (derived from DOB)
Underlined AttributePrimary Key AttributeRollNo
DiamondRelationship between entitiesSTUDIES, TEACHES
Double RectangleWeak Entity (no independent PK)DEPENDENT of EMPLOYEE
LinesLinks entities, attributes, and relationships—

Types of Relationships (Cardinality)

TypeMeaningExample
One-to-One (1:1)One entity relates to exactly one entity in anotherPerson ↔ Aadhar Card
One-to-Many (1:M)One entity relates to many entities in anotherOne Teacher → Many Students
Many-to-One (M:1)Many entities relate to one entityMany Students → One School
Many-to-Many (M:N)Many entities relate to many entitiesStudents ↔ Subjects

Topic 5 SQL — Structured Query Language

▼
📖 SQL (Structured Query Language) SQL is a standard language used to communicate with a relational database. It is used to create, read, update, and delete data in databases. SQL is not case-sensitive.

Types of SQL Commands

CategoryFull FormCommandsUse
DDLData Definition LanguageCREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE, RENAMEDefine/modify structure
DMLData Manipulation LanguageINSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECTManipulate data in tables
DQLData Query LanguageSELECTRetrieve/query data
DCLData Control LanguageGRANT, REVOKEControl access/permissions
TCLTransaction Control LanguageCOMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINTManage transactions

MySQL Data Types

CategoryData TypeDescriptionExample
NumericINTInteger numbersAge INT
FLOATFloating point (single precision)Price FLOAT
DECIMAL(p,s)Exact numeric with precisionSalary DECIMAL(10,2)
BIGINTLarge integersPopulation BIGINT
StringCHAR(n)Fixed length stringGender CHAR(1)
VARCHAR(n)Variable length stringName VARCHAR(50)
TEXTLarge text dataDescription TEXT
ENUMSet of allowed valuesGrade ENUM('A','B','C')
Date/TimeDATEDate (YYYY-MM-DD)DOB DATE
TIMETime (HH:MM:SS)InTime TIME
DATETIMEDate and time combinedCreatedAt DATETIME
⭐ CHAR vs VARCHAR — Important Difference
  • CHAR(n): Always stores exactly n characters. If input is shorter, pads with spaces. Faster but wastes space.
  • VARCHAR(n): Stores only the actual characters entered (up to n). Saves space. Slightly slower.
  • Example: CHAR(10) for "Ram" stores "Ram " (7 spaces added). VARCHAR(10) stores "Ram" only.

Topic 6 DDL Commands — CREATE, ALTER, DROP

▼

CREATE Command

Used to create a new database or table.
-- Create a Database CREATE DATABASE school; -- Use/Select a Database USE school; -- Create a Table CREATE TABLE STUDENT ( RollNo INT PRIMARY KEY, Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, Age INT, City VARCHAR(30), Marks DECIMAL(5,2), Gender CHAR(1), DOB DATE );

Constraints in CREATE TABLE

ConstraintMeaningExample
PRIMARY KEYUnique + Not Null identifierRollNo INT PRIMARY KEY
NOT NULLColumn cannot be emptyName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
UNIQUEAll values must be differentEmail VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE
DEFAULTSets a default value if none providedCity VARCHAR(30) DEFAULT 'Delhi'
CHECKValidates data before insertingAge INT CHECK(Age >= 5)
FOREIGN KEYReferences PK of another tableFOREIGN KEY(RollNo) REFERENCES STUDENT(RollNo)
-- Table with Foreign Key example CREATE TABLE MARKS ( SrNo INT PRIMARY KEY, RollNo INT, Subject VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL, Score INT CHECK(Score BETWEEN 0 AND 100), FOREIGN KEY(RollNo) REFERENCES STUDENT(RollNo) );

ALTER Command

Used to modify an existing table — add/remove/modify columns or constraints.
-- Add a new column ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD Email VARCHAR(100); -- Modify/change a column's data type ALTER TABLE STUDENT MODIFY Age TINYINT; -- Rename a column ALTER TABLE STUDENT RENAME COLUMN City TO Address; -- Drop (remove) a column ALTER TABLE STUDENT DROP COLUMN Gender; -- Add a constraint ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD CONSTRAINT chk_age CHECK(Age > 0);

DROP and TRUNCATE

CommandEffectCan be Rolled Back?
DROP TABLEDeletes the entire table (structure + data)❌ No
TRUNCATE TABLEDeletes all rows but keeps table structure❌ No (DDL)
DELETE (DML)Deletes specific or all rows✅ Yes (with ROLLBACK)
DROP TABLE STUDENT; -- Remove table completely DROP DATABASE school; -- Remove entire database TRUNCATE TABLE STUDENT; -- Delete all rows, keep structure

Topic 7 DML Commands — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT

▼

INSERT Command

-- Insert all columns (order must match table structure) INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (101, 'Aman Sharma', 17, 'Delhi', 89.5, 'M', '2007-05-14'); -- Insert specific columns INSERT INTO STUDENT (RollNo, Name, Marks) VALUES (102, 'Priya', 92); -- Insert multiple rows INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (103, 'Rohan', 17, 'Delhi', 78, 'M', '2007-01-20'), (104, 'Neha', 16, 'Lucknow', 95, 'F', '2008-03-11');

SELECT Command (Most Important)

-- Select all columns SELECT * FROM STUDENT; -- Select specific columns SELECT Name, Marks FROM STUDENT; -- WHERE clause — filter rows SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE City = 'Delhi'; SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE Marks > 80; SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE Age BETWEEN 15 AND 18; SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE Name LIKE 'A%'; -- starts with A SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE Name LIKE '%a'; -- ends with a SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE Name LIKE '_o%'; -- 2nd char is o SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE City IN ('Delhi', 'Mumbai'); SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE Age IS NULL; -- ORDER BY — sort results SELECT * FROM STUDENT ORDER BY Marks DESC; -- highest first SELECT * FROM STUDENT ORDER BY Name ASC; -- alphabetical -- DISTINCT — remove duplicates SELECT DISTINCT City FROM STUDENT; -- LIMIT — retrieve limited rows SELECT * FROM STUDENT LIMIT 3;

Aggregate Functions with GROUP BY

FunctionPurposeExample
COUNT()Count number of rowsSELECT COUNT(*) FROM STUDENT;
SUM()Sum of values in a columnSELECT SUM(Marks) FROM STUDENT;
AVG()Average of valuesSELECT AVG(Marks) FROM STUDENT;
MAX()Maximum valueSELECT MAX(Marks) FROM STUDENT;
MIN()Minimum valueSELECT MIN(Age) FROM STUDENT;
-- GROUP BY — group rows by a column SELECT City, COUNT(*) AS Total FROM STUDENT GROUP BY City; SELECT City, AVG(Marks) FROM STUDENT GROUP BY City; -- HAVING — filter after GROUP BY (like WHERE for groups) SELECT City, COUNT(*) FROM STUDENT GROUP BY City HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
⭐ WHERE vs HAVING — Important!
  • WHERE filters rows before grouping. Cannot use aggregate functions.
  • HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY. Can use aggregate functions.
  • Example: WHERE Marks > 50 → filters individual rows. HAVING AVG(Marks) > 50 → filters groups.

UPDATE Command

-- Update specific rows UPDATE STUDENT SET Marks = 95 WHERE RollNo = 101; -- Update multiple columns UPDATE STUDENT SET City = 'Chennai', Age = 18 WHERE RollNo = 102; -- Update all rows (no WHERE — be careful!) UPDATE STUDENT SET Marks = Marks + 5;

DELETE Command

-- Delete specific rows DELETE FROM STUDENT WHERE RollNo = 103; -- Delete rows matching condition DELETE FROM STUDENT WHERE Marks < 33; -- Delete all rows (keeps table structure) DELETE FROM STUDENT;

Topic 8 SQL Joins & Normalization

▼

SQL Joins

📖 JOIN A JOIN is used to retrieve data from two or more tables based on a related column (usually a Foreign Key – Primary Key pair).
Join TypeReturns
INNER JOIN (JOIN)Only matching rows from BOTH tables
LEFT JOINAll rows from left table + matching from right (NULL if no match)
RIGHT JOINAll rows from right table + matching from left (NULL if no match)
CROSS JOINCartesian product — every row of left × every row of right
-- INNER JOIN — common rows only SELECT S.Name, S.City, M.Subject, M.Score FROM STUDENT S INNER JOIN MARKS M ON S.RollNo = M.RollNo; -- LEFT JOIN — all students, even without marks SELECT S.Name, M.Score FROM STUDENT S LEFT JOIN MARKS M ON S.RollNo = M.RollNo; -- Cartesian Product (old syntax) SELECT * FROM STUDENT, MARKS;

Normalization

📖 Normalization The process of organising a database to reduce data redundancy and improve data integrity by dividing large tables into smaller related tables.
Normal FormCondition
1NF (First Normal Form)Each column must have atomic (indivisible) values. No repeating groups.
2NF (Second Normal Form)Must be in 1NF + no partial dependency (every non-key attribute fully depends on the whole primary key).
3NF (Third Normal Form)Must be in 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key attributes should not depend on other non-key attributes).

Important MySQL Commands for Reference

-- Show all databases SHOW DATABASES; -- Show all tables in current database SHOW TABLES; -- See structure/schema of a table DESCRIBE STUDENT; DESC STUDENT; -- See the CREATE statement used for a table SHOW CREATE TABLE STUDENT; -- Column alias (rename in output) SELECT Name AS StudentName, Marks AS Score FROM STUDENT; -- String functions SELECT UPPER(Name), LOWER(City), LENGTH(Name) FROM STUDENT; SELECT SUBSTRING(Name, 1, 3) FROM STUDENT; SELECT CONCAT(Name, ' - ', City) FROM STUDENT; -- Math / Numeric functions SELECT ROUND(89.67, 1); -- 89.7 SELECT MOD(10, 3); -- 1 SELECT POWER(2, 8); -- 256 -- Date functions SELECT NOW(); -- current date & time SELECT CURDATE(); -- today's date SELECT YEAR(DOB) FROM STUDENT; -- extract year from date

Transaction Control Language (TCL)

📖 Transaction A Transaction is a sequence of one or more SQL statements that are executed as a single unit. Either all succeed (COMMIT) or all fail (ROLLBACK) — this is called Atomicity.
START TRANSACTION; -- Begin transaction UPDATE STUDENT SET Marks = 90 WHERE RollNo = 101; DELETE FROM STUDENT WHERE RollNo = 999; SAVEPOINT sp1; -- Create a save point ROLLBACK TO sp1; -- Undo changes after sp1 ROLLBACK; -- Undo ALL changes in transaction COMMIT; -- Save all changes permanently
📌 ACID Properties — Important for Exam!
PropertyMeaning
A — AtomicityAll operations in a transaction complete or none do (all-or-nothing).
C — ConsistencyDatabase moves from one valid state to another; constraints always met.
I — IsolationConcurrent transactions do not interfere with each other.
D — DurabilityOnce committed, changes are permanent even after system failure.

⚡ Quick Revision — Key Points for Exam

🔑 Keys
  • PK = Unique + Not Null, only ONE per table
  • FK references PK of another table
  • Candidate Key = all possible PKs
📋 SQL Types
  • DDL: CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE
  • DML: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT
  • DCL: GRANT, REVOKE
  • TCL: COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT
📊 Aggregate
  • COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN
  • GROUP BY groups rows
  • HAVING filters groups (≠ WHERE)
🔗 Join Types
  • INNER JOIN: matching rows only
  • LEFT JOIN: all left + matching right
  • CROSS JOIN: cartesian product
🏗️ Normalization
  • 1NF: Atomic values
  • 2NF: No partial dependency
  • 3NF: No transitive dependency
💾 ACID
  • Atomicity: all-or-nothing
  • Consistency: valid state always
  • Isolation: concurrent safety
  • Durability: committed = permanent
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 9
Unit 1: Communication Skills-I Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-I Unit 3: ICT Skills-I Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-I Unit 5: Green Skills-I

💬 IT Class IX (402) — Unit 1: Communication Skills – I

Koi result nahi mila. Dusra keyword try karein.
💬
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 1

Communication Skills – I

Complete notes covering Introduction to Communication, Verbal & Non-Verbal Communication, Writing Skills, Pronunciation, Greetings, and Asking Questions — as per CBSE Employability Skills syllabus.

📚 7 Sessions ✅ Full Syllabus 💡 Examples Included 🔤 7Cs of Communication

Session 1 Introduction to Communication

▼
📖 What is Communication? The word 'Communication' comes from the Latin word commūnicāre, meaning 'to share'. Communication is the exchange of information through signs, signals, speech, writing, or other forms of media.
⭐ Importance of Communication
Communication skills are needed to:
  • Inform — Give facts or information to someone.
  • Influence — Change or persuade someone in an indirect but important way.
  • Express Feelings — Talking about your feelings is a healthy way to express them.

Elements of Communication

Communication is a two-way exchange of information — giving and receiving.

ElementDescription
SenderThe person who starts the communication process by sending a message.
MessageThe information, idea, or feeling the sender wants to convey.
ChannelThe path/medium used (phone, face-to-face, writing, email, etc.).
ReceiverThe person who receives and understands the message.

Perspectives in Communication

📖 Perspectives Perspectives are ideas, views, or fixed ways of thinking that sometimes affect our communication. For example, if your teacher is usually strict, even when they are being friendly, you may think they are scolding you.
⭐ Factors Affecting Perspectives in Communication
  • Language — Incorrect words, unfamiliar language, or lack of detail can act as a barrier.
  • Visual Perception — The brain's ability to make sense of what we see. E.g., partially drawn pictures can be misunderstood.
  • Past Experience — Earlier experiences can stop us from understanding clearly. E.g., "This shopkeeper cheated me last time."
  • Prejudice — Fixed ideas like "No one in my class likes me" may stop a student from communicating openly.
  • Feelings — Emotions like lack of interest or distrust affect communication. E.g., "I am not feeling well."
  • Environment — Noise or disturbance in surroundings can make communication difficult.
  • Personal Factors — Your own feelings, habits, and ways of thinking.
  • Culture — Signs mean different things in different cultures. E.g., showing a thumb means 'good job' for some but may be insulting to others.

7Cs — Principles of Effective Communication

Effective communication can happen if we follow the 7C principles of professional communication:

CPrincipleMeaning
1️⃣ClearBe clear about what you want to say.
2️⃣ConciseUse simple words and say only what is needed.
3️⃣ConcreteUse exact words and specific phrases.
4️⃣CorrectUse correct spellings, language, and grammar.
5️⃣CoherentYour words should make sense and relate to the main topic.
6️⃣CompleteYour message should have all the needed information.
7️⃣CourteousBe respectful, friendly, and honest.

Session 2 Verbal Communication

▼
📖 Verbal Communication Verbal communication is the sharing of information by using words. It is the most commonly used method of communication. Using the right words is very important — wrong words cause confusion.

Types of Verbal Communication

TypeDescriptionExamples
Oral / Spoken Communication Exchanging information using spoken words Face-to-face conversation, phone calls, classroom teaching, business discussions, public speeches
Written Communication Conveying information through written words Letters, notes, emails, SMS, books, newspapers
⭐ Advantages & Disadvantages of Verbal Communication

✅ Advantages:

  • Easy and quick to use.
  • Easier to exchange ideas back and forth.
  • You can change your communication based on the other person's reply.

❌ Disadvantages:

  • Cultural differences between sender and receiver can cause misunderstandings.
  • If the right words are not used, meanings become confusing and difficult to understand.
🎤 Public Speaking Speaking in front of a large group is known as public speaking. The most difficult part is fear and nervousness. You can use the 3Ps method to overcome your fears.
⭐ 3Ps of Public Speaking
  • Prepare
    • Think about your topic.
    • Think about what your listeners need to know.
    • Think about the best way to help listeners understand.
    • Write what you plan to say.
  • Practice
    • Practice by yourself first.
    • Talk in front of a mirror.
    • Talk in front of family and friends and ask for feedback.
    • Speak clearly, loudly, and at the right speed.
  • Perform
    • Take a few deep breaths if feeling nervous.
    • Think about what you have prepared and start speaking confidently.

Session 3 Non-Verbal Communication

▼
📖 Non-Verbal Communication Non-verbal communication is the message we send to others without using any words. We communicate through signals, expressions, gestures, body postures, and body language.
⭐ Importance of Non-Verbal Communication
Research shows that most of our daily communication is done through:
  • Body movements (face, arms, movements, etc.) — ~55%
  • Voice control (tone, pauses, volume) — ~38%
  • Words (actual content spoken) — only ~7%
This shows non-verbal communication is more powerful than verbal!

Types of Non-Verbal Communication

TypeDescriptionExample
Facial ExpressionShows the feelings of a person through face movementsSmiling, frowning, raised eyebrows
PostureBody positions showing confidence and feelingsStanding straight = confidence; slouching = boredom
Gestures / Body LanguageMovements of hands or head to express an ideaWaving goodbye, nodding yes
TouchCommunication through physical contactShaking hands, patting on the back
Space (Proxemics)Maintaining proper distance while talkingStanding closer with close friends
Eye ContactLooking at the person while talking shows interestLooking away can make the other person feel ignored
ParalanguageTone, speed, and volume of voice that changes the meaningSaying "Really?" with surprise vs. sarcasm
🖼️ Visual Communication Visual communication involves sending and understanding messages only through images or pictures.

Advantages:
  • Easy to understand by everyone.
  • Can convey the message to a large audience quickly.

Examples: Traffic signs, maps, charts, graphs, emojis, logos, diagrams.

Session 4 Writing Skills: Parts of Speech

▼
📖 Sentence vs Phrase
  • Sentence — A group of words that communicates a complete thought. Begins with a capital letter and ends with a full stop, question mark, or exclamation mark. Example: Raju goes to school.
  • Phrase — A group of words that does NOT make complete sense. Example: goes to school
⭐ MINTS — Capitalisation Rules
LetterRuleExample
MMonths, Days, HolidaysJanuary, Monday, Diwali
IThe word "I" is always capitalisedI am a student.
NNames of people and placesRahul, Delhi, India
TTitles and headingsMr. Sharma, Dr. Gupta
SStart of every sentenceThe boy is playing.

Punctuation Marks

NameSignUse
Full Stop.End of a statement sentence
Comma,Short pause in a sentence; separating items in a list
Question Mark?End of a question sentence
Exclamation Mark!Shows strong feelings — surprise, anger, joy
Apostrophe'Shows possession or omission (e.g., Rahul's, don't)
Inverted Commas" "Encloses direct speech or quoted text
Colon:Introduces a list or explanation

Basic Parts of Speech

Part of SpeechDefinitionExample
NounName of a person, place, thing, or ideaRahul, Delhi, book, love
PronounWord used in place of a nounhe, she, it, they, I, we
VerbAction or state of beingrun, eat, is, are, was
AdjectiveDescribes a noun or pronountall, red, happy, big
AdverbDescribes a verb, adjective, or another adverbquickly, very, well, slowly
PrepositionShows the relationship between noun and other wordsin, on, at, under, beside
ConjunctionJoins words, phrases, or clausesand, but, or, because, so
InterjectionExpresses sudden emotionOh! Wow! Hurray! Ouch!

Session 5 Writing Skills: Sentences

▼
📖 Subject, Verb and Object
  • Subject — The person or thing that performs the action. Example: Raju eats a mango.
  • Verb — The action word. Example: Raju eats a mango.
  • Object — The person or thing on which the action is performed. Example: Raju eats a mango.

Types of Objects

TypeDescriptionExample
Direct ObjectDirectly receives the action of the verbPriya gave a book.
Indirect ObjectTells to whom or for whom the action is donePriya gave Rahul a book.

Types of Sentences (Based on Structure)

TypeDescriptionExample
Simple SentenceHas one subject and one verb (one independent clause)Raju goes to school.
Compound SentenceTwo or more simple sentences joined by a conjunctionRaju goes to school and he studies hard.
Complex SentenceOne main clause + one or more dependent clausesAlthough it rained, Raju went to school.

Types of Sentences (Based on Purpose)

TypeDescriptionEnd MarkExample
DeclarativeMakes a statement.She reads a book.
InterrogativeAsks a question?Does she read a book?
ImperativeGives a command or request. or !Read the book now.
ExclamatoryExpresses strong feeling!What a great book!
📖 Paragraph A paragraph is a group of related sentences that deal with a single main idea. A good paragraph has:
  • Topic Sentence — States the main idea of the paragraph.
  • Supporting Sentences — Give details and examples to explain the main idea.
  • Concluding Sentence — Summarises or wraps up the paragraph.

Session 6 Pronunciation Basics

▼
📖 Pronunciation Pronunciation is the way we speak a word — the sounds we make and the stress we place on certain syllables. Correct pronunciation helps others understand us clearly.
📖 Phonetics Phonetics is the study of the sounds of speech. It helps us understand how to correctly pronounce words. Each sound in a language is represented by a phonetic symbol.
⭐ Key Pronunciation Tips
  • Speak clearly and at a moderate speed — not too fast, not too slow.
  • Pay attention to stress in words. E.g., PREsent (noun) vs preSENT (verb).
  • Words can have silent letters. E.g., the 'k' in 'knife' is silent.
  • Practice with a dictionary that shows phonetic symbols.
  • Listen carefully to how native or fluent speakers pronounce words.
📊 Common Pronunciation Mistakes (Indian English)
WordWrong PronunciationCorrect Pronunciation
KnifeK-nifeNife (silent 'k')
WednesdayWed-nes-dayWenz-day
ReceiptRe-see-ptRi-seet (silent 'p')
DoubtDoubt (with 'b' sound)Dout (silent 'b')

Session 7 Greetings and Introductions

▼
📖 Greeting A greeting is a word or phrase used to welcome or acknowledge someone when you meet them or communicate with them. It is the first step in any communication.

Types of Greetings

TypeExamplesWhen to Use
Formal GreetingsGood morning, Good afternoon, Good evening, How do you do?With teachers, elders, strangers, in professional settings
Informal GreetingsHi, Hello, Hey, What's up?With friends, classmates, family of similar age
Written GreetingsDear Sir/Madam, To Whom It May Concern, Hi [Name]In letters, emails, messages
Cultural GreetingsNamaste, Sat Sri Akal, SalaamBased on cultural or regional context
⭐ Self-Introduction Tips
When introducing yourself, always include:
  • Your name
  • Your class / school
  • Your hometown / city
  • Your interests / hobbies (optional but good)

Example: "Good morning! My name is Rahul Sharma. I am a student of Class IX at ABC School in Lucknow. I enjoy reading books and playing cricket."

Session 9 & 10 Asking Questions

▼
📖 Asking Questions Asking questions is an important communication skill. It helps us gather information, clarify doubts, and show interest in what the other person is saying.

Types of Questions

TypeDescriptionExample
Open Questions Cannot be answered with just Yes or No. They invite a detailed response and start with Wh- words. What do you think about this? / How did you do that?
Closed Questions Can be answered with a simple Yes or No or a one-word answer. Did you do your homework? / Are you coming?
Clarifying Questions Asked when you do not understand something and want to make it clear. Could you please explain that again? / What do you mean by that?
Probing Questions Used to dig deeper into a topic or find more details. Why do you think that? / Can you give an example?
Rhetorical Questions Asked for effect — not expecting an actual answer. Isn't the sky beautiful today?
⭐ Wh- Question Words
Question WordUsed to Ask AboutExample
WhatThings / InformationWhat is your name?
WhoPersonWho is your teacher?
WherePlaceWhere do you live?
WhenTimeWhen is your exam?
WhyReasonWhy were you absent?
HowManner / MethodHow do you spell that?
WhichChoice between optionsWhich subject do you like?
WhoseOwnershipWhose book is this?
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 9
Unit 1: Communication Skills-I Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-I Unit 3: ICT Skills-I Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-I Unit 5: Green Skills-I

🧠 IT Class IX (402) — Unit 2: Self-Management Skills – I

🧠
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 2

Self-Management Skills – I

Complete notes covering Introduction to Self-Management, Strength & Weakness Analysis, Self-Confidence, Positive Thinking, Personal Hygiene, and Grooming — as per CBSE Employability Skills syllabus.

📚 6 Sessions ✅ Full Syllabus 💡 Examples Included 😊 SMILE Technique

Session 1 Introduction to Self-Management

▼
📖 What is Self-Management? Self-management, also referred to as 'self-control' or 'self-regulation', is the ability to regulate one's emotions, thoughts, and behaviour effectively in different situations.
⭐ Self-Management can help in:
  • Developing good habits
  • Overcoming bad habits
  • Reaching your goals
  • Overcoming challenges and difficulties

Self-Management Skills

SkillMeaning
Self-awarenessKnowing yourself — your values, likes, dislikes, strengths and weaknesses.
Self-controlAbility to control your behaviour and maintain discipline.
Self-confidenceBelieving in yourself that you can do any task given to you and not being scared of taking risks.
Self-motivationDoing tasks on your own without any external motivation.
Positive thinkingExpressing certainty or affirmation even in tough situations.
Problem solvingUnderstanding a problem and finding a solution using a step-by-step method.
Team workWorking together with people to accomplish shared goals.
Time managementAchieving tasks on time and according to a plan.
Goal settingPlanning concrete goals to be accomplished within a set timeframe.
Personal hygiene and groomingKeeping oneself clean, healthy and smart.

Session 2 Strength and Weakness Analysis

▼
📖 Knowing Yourself Strengths and weaknesses help us analyse ourselves — knowing what makes us happy, finding areas of improvement, and understanding our opinions.
TermMeaningHow to Identify
Strength Things we do well and are good at. Everyone has some strengths. Think about what you do well, what you are always good at, and what others appreciate about you.
Weakness Also called 'areas of improvement' — things we do not do well. Everyone has some weaknesses too. Point out areas where you struggle, look at feedback from others, and accept your weaknesses as areas of improvement.
⭐ Difference between Interests and Abilities
  • Interest — Things that we enjoy doing. Activities we want to learn, activities that make us happy, or things we would do even if no one asked us to.
  • Ability — An acquired or natural capacity that enables an individual to perform a particular job or task with considerable proficiency.

Session 3 Self-Confidence

▼
📖 What is Self-Confidence? Self-confidence is a sense of trusting one's own abilities and self. People who are confident believe that they can do anything given to them in any situation.

Qualities of Self-Confident People

QualityMeaning
Self-beliefThey believe in themselves and feel they can achieve anything.
Hard WorkThey work hard to achieve their goals.
Positive AttitudeThey maintain a positive attitude towards their goals.
CommitmentThey are committed to achieving their goals in life.
⭐ Three Steps to Build Self-Confidence
  1. Know yourself — Identify your strengths and weaknesses.
  2. Set small goals — Achieve small targets first to build confidence.
  3. Take action — Act on your plans and learn from mistakes.
⚠️ Factors That Decrease Self-Confidence
  1. Thinking we cannot do a particular task.
  2. Constantly thinking about past mistakes and feeling bad instead of learning from them.
  3. Expecting success on the first attempt and giving up when it doesn't happen.
  4. Being surrounded by people with a negative attitude.

Session 4 Positive Thinking

▼
📖 What is Positive Thinking? Positive-thinking people have a positive attitude towards life and its challenges. A positive-thinking person looks at the good in things and works towards improving them rather than worrying or looking for the bad.
⭐ Benefits of Positive Thinking
  • Helps in overcoming challenges
  • Makes you an energetic individual
  • Helps you perform better at work
  • Makes you and people around you happy

SMILE — How to Stay Positive

LetterStep
SStart your day in a positive way
MManage time to relax
IImagine the best in any situation
LLearn to take feedback in a positive way
EExpress gratitude

Session 5 Personal Hygiene

▼
📖 What is Personal Hygiene? Personal Hygiene is the habit or practice of keeping ourselves clean. Cleanliness helps us maintain our health and well-being.
⭐ Why is Personal Hygiene Important?
  • Helps us stay healthy
  • Creates a good image of ourselves
  • Avoids feeling ashamed in public due to bad breath, body odour, etc.

Three Steps to Personal Hygiene

StepWhat to Do
1. Cleanliness of the BodyBathe daily, wash hands regularly, keep nails clean and trimmed, brush teeth twice a day.
2. Cleanliness of ClothesWear clean, washed, and ironed clothes every day. Change inner wear daily.
3. Cleanliness of SurroundingsKeep your desk, room, and workplace clean and organised.

Session 6 Grooming

▼
📖 What is Grooming? Grooming is the process of making yourself look neat, tidy, and smart. Dressing and grooming are important because they help us make a good impression, look smart, and feel confident.

Guidelines for Dressing and Grooming

  • Clothes should be clean, neat, and ironed.
  • Shoes should be clean and polished.
  • Change socks every day and always wear clean socks.
  • Hair should be washed regularly to keep clean.
  • Simple hairstyles and well-combed hair give a smart look.
  • Face looks clean if you shave regularly.
  • Moustache should be neatly trimmed.
⭐ Remember — First Impression is the Last Impression!
The way you dress and present yourself forms the first impression in people's minds. Always be clean, neat, and well-groomed in school, interviews, and professional settings.
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 9
Unit 1: Communication Skills-I Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-I Unit 3: ICT Skills-I Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-I Unit 5: Green Skills-I

💻 IT Class IX (402) — Unit 3: ICT Skills – I

💻
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 3

ICT Skills – I

Complete notes covering Introduction to ICT, Computer Hardware, Input/Output Devices, Storage Devices, Operating System, and Internet Basics — as per CBSE Employability Skills syllabus.

📚 5 Sessions ✅ Full Syllabus 🖥️ Hardware & Software

Session 1 Introduction to ICT

▼
📖 What is ICT? ICT stands for Information and Communication Technology. It refers to all technologies used to handle telecommunications, broadcast media, intelligent building management systems, audiovisual processing, transmission systems, and network-based control and monitoring functions.
⭐ Uses of ICT in Daily Life
  • Education — Online classes, e-books, digital libraries.
  • Communication — Email, video calls, social media.
  • Business — Online shopping, banking, digital payments.
  • Healthcare — Online consultations, patient records.
  • Entertainment — Streaming movies, music, online games.
📖 What is a Computer? A computer is an electronic device that accepts data as input, processes it, and gives output. It works on the IPO (Input → Process → Output) cycle.

Session 2 Computer Hardware — Input & Output Devices

▼
📖 Hardware vs Software
  • Hardware — Physical parts of a computer that can be seen and touched. Example: keyboard, monitor, CPU.
  • Software — Set of instructions that tell the computer what to do. Cannot be touched. Example: Windows, MS Office.

Input Devices

Input devices are used to enter data into the computer.

DeviceUse
KeyboardTyping text and commands
MousePointing and clicking on screen
ScannerConverting printed documents to digital form
MicrophoneInputting audio/voice
WebcamInputting video/images
JoystickPlaying video games
TouchscreenTouch-based input

Output Devices

Output devices are used to display or produce the result of processed data.

DeviceUse
MonitorDisplaying visual output on screen
PrinterPrinting documents and images on paper
SpeakersProducing sound output
ProjectorDisplaying on a large screen
HeadphonesPersonal audio output

Session 3 Storage Devices & Memory

▼
📖 Types of Memory
  • Primary Memory (RAM & ROM) — Directly accessed by CPU. Fast but limited.
    • RAM (Random Access Memory) — Temporary memory. Data is lost when power is off. Used to run programs.
    • ROM (Read Only Memory) — Permanent memory. Data is not lost when power is off. Stores boot instructions.
  • Secondary Memory — External storage. Used to permanently store data.

Common Storage Devices

DeviceDescription
Hard Disk (HDD)Large storage inside the computer. Stores OS, programs, and files.
SSD (Solid State Drive)Faster than HDD. No moving parts. Used in modern laptops.
Pen Drive / USB DrivePortable storage device connected via USB port.
CD / DVDOptical disk used to store music, movies, and data.
Memory CardUsed in mobile phones and cameras.
Cloud StorageOnline storage — data saved on internet servers. E.g., Google Drive.

Session 4 Operating System

▼
📖 What is an Operating System? An Operating System (OS) is a software that manages computer hardware and software resources and provides common services for computer programs. It acts as an interface between the user and the computer hardware.

Functions of an Operating System

  • Memory Management — Controls how memory is used by programs.
  • Process Management — Manages running of programs and tasks.
  • File Management — Organises files and folders on the disk.
  • Device Management — Controls input and output devices.
  • User Interface — Provides GUI or command-line interface to the user.

Examples of Operating Systems

OSUsed In
WindowsDesktop and laptop computers (Microsoft)
macOSApple Mac computers
LinuxServers, programmers, and open-source users
AndroidMobile phones and tablets
iOSApple iPhones and iPads

Session 5 Internet Basics

▼
📖 What is the Internet? The Internet is a global network of interconnected computers that communicate using a standard protocol (TCP/IP). It allows users to share information and communicate across the world.

Important Internet Terms

TermMeaning
WWW (World Wide Web)A system of interlinked web pages and websites accessible via the internet.
WebsiteA collection of web pages under one domain name. E.g., www.google.com
Web BrowserSoftware used to access websites. E.g., Chrome, Firefox, Edge.
URLUniform Resource Locator — the address of a web page.
Search EngineTool to search for information online. E.g., Google, Bing.
EmailElectronic Mail — sending/receiving messages over the internet.
DownloadGetting data from the internet to your computer.
UploadSending data from your computer to the internet.
⭐ Internet Safety Tips
  • Never share your password with anyone.
  • Do not click on unknown links or emails.
  • Use strong passwords with letters, numbers and symbols.
  • Do not share personal information online with strangers.
  • Always log out after using shared computers.
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 9
Unit 1: Communication Skills-I Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-I Unit 3: ICT Skills-I Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-I Unit 5: Green Skills-I

🚀 IT Class IX (402) — Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills – I

🚀
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 4

Entrepreneurial Skills – I

Complete notes covering Introduction to Entrepreneurship, Qualities of an Entrepreneur, Types of Business, Functions of an Entrepreneur, and Business Ideas — as per CBSE Employability Skills syllabus.

📚 4 Sessions ✅ Full Syllabus 💡 Business Ideas

Session 1 Introduction to Entrepreneurship

▼
📖 What is Entrepreneurship? Entrepreneurship is the process of designing, launching, and running a new business. It involves taking financial risks in the hope of making a profit.
📖 Who is an Entrepreneur? An Entrepreneur is a person who sets up a business, taking on financial risks in order to earn profit. They identify a need in the market and create a product or service to meet that need.
⭐ Why is Entrepreneurship Important?
  • Creates employment for others.
  • Drives economic growth of the country.
  • Encourages innovation and new ideas.
  • Fulfils the needs of society.
  • Provides independence and self-reliance.

Session 2 Qualities of an Entrepreneur

▼
QualityExplanation
InitiativeTakes action on their own without waiting to be told.
Risk-TakingWilling to take calculated risks to start and grow a business.
Creativity & InnovationComes up with new ideas and finds better ways to do things.
Decision-MakingMakes important business decisions confidently and wisely.
LeadershipGuides and motivates their team to achieve business goals.
Hard WorkWorks with dedication and perseverance to achieve goals.
Communication SkillsCommunicates ideas clearly to customers, employees, and investors.
Problem SolvingIdentifies challenges and finds practical solutions.

Session 3 Types of Business & Functions of an Entrepreneur

▼

Types of Business Activities

TypeDescriptionExample
ManufacturingMaking or producing goods from raw materials.Making furniture, clothes, biscuits
TradingBuying and selling goods without changing them.Grocery shop, electronics store
ServiceProviding services to meet people's needs.Hospital, school, salon, transport

Functions of an Entrepreneur

  • Idea Generation — Coming up with a unique business idea.
  • Planning — Making a detailed plan for the business.
  • Resource Management — Arranging money, manpower, and materials.
  • Risk-Taking — Taking financial and business risks.
  • Decision Making — Taking important decisions for the business.
  • Innovation — Constantly improving products and processes.

Session 4 Business Ideas & Opportunity

▼
📖 What is a Business Idea? A business idea is a concept that can be used for financial gain — usually centred on a product or service that can be offered for money.
⭐ Sources of Business Ideas
  • Hobbies and Interests — Turn what you love into a business. E.g., cooking, photography.
  • Problems in Daily Life — Find a problem and create a solution. E.g., home delivery apps.
  • Skills and Knowledge — Use your expertise. E.g., tutoring, tailoring.
  • Trends and Technology — Keep up with new trends. E.g., social media management.
  • Existing Businesses — Improve on what already exists.
💡 Examples of Student Business Ideas
  • Selling handmade crafts or greeting cards
  • Tutoring younger students
  • Starting a YouTube channel or blog
  • Selling homemade food items
  • Offering computer repair or help services
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 9
Unit 1: Communication Skills-I Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-I Unit 3: ICT Skills-I Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-I Unit 5: Green Skills-I

🌱 IT Class IX (402) — Unit 5: Green Skills – I

🌍
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 5

Green Skills – I

Complete notes covering Environment & Ecology, Climate Change, Sustainable Development, Waste Management, and Energy Conservation — as per CBSE Employability Skills syllabus.

📚 4 Sessions ✅ Full Syllabus 🌍 Save the Planet

Session 1 Introduction to Environment & Ecology

▼
📖 What is Environment? The environment refers to the surroundings in which we live — including all living and non-living things around us. It includes air, water, soil, plants, animals, and people.
📖 What is an Ecosystem? An ecosystem is a community of living organisms (plants, animals, microbes) interacting with each other and with the non-living components (air, water, soil) of their environment.

Components of Environment

ComponentDescriptionExamples
Biotic (Living)All living organisms in the environment.Plants, animals, bacteria, fungi
Abiotic (Non-living)All non-living elements that affect living organisms.Air, water, soil, sunlight, temperature

Session 2 Climate Change & Global Warming

▼
📖 What is Climate Change? Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global temperatures and weather patterns. While some changes are natural, human activities since the 1800s have been the main cause.
📖 Greenhouse Effect & Global Warming The Greenhouse Effect is the trapping of the sun's heat in the Earth's atmosphere by gases like CO₂ and methane — causing the planet to warm up. Excessive warming is called Global Warming.
⭐ Causes of Climate Change
  • Burning of fossil fuels (coal, oil, petrol) — releases CO₂
  • Deforestation — reduces CO₂ absorption by trees
  • Industrial activities — release harmful gases
  • Agriculture — livestock and farming release methane
  • Vehicles — release carbon emissions
⚠️ Effects of Climate Change
  • Rising sea levels due to melting glaciers
  • Extreme weather events (floods, droughts, cyclones)
  • Loss of biodiversity and extinction of species
  • Crop failures and food insecurity
  • Spread of diseases

Session 3 Sustainable Development

▼
📖 What is Sustainable Development? Sustainable Development is development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.
⭐ The 3 Pillars of Sustainable Development
PillarMeaning
🌍 EnvironmentProtecting natural resources, reducing pollution, conserving biodiversity.
💰 EconomyUsing resources efficiently and promoting fair trade and employment.
👥 SocietyEnsuring equal access to education, healthcare, and basic needs.

The 3R Principle

RMeaningExample
ReduceUse less resources and produce less waste.Turn off lights when not in use.
ReuseUse items more than once instead of throwing them away.Reuse glass bottles and bags.
RecycleConvert waste into new usable materials.Recycle paper, plastic, and metal.

Session 4 Waste Management & Energy Conservation

▼

Types of Waste

TypeDescriptionExample
BiodegradableWaste that decomposes naturally in the environment.Food waste, vegetable peels, paper
Non-BiodegradableWaste that does not decompose easily and causes pollution.Plastic, glass, metal cans, e-waste
⭐ Ways to Conserve Energy
  • Switch off lights, fans, and appliances when not in use.
  • Use LED bulbs instead of regular bulbs.
  • Use solar energy for heating water and generating electricity.
  • Walk, cycle, or use public transport instead of private vehicles.
  • Use energy-efficient appliances (5-star rating).
  • Avoid wasting water — fix leaking taps immediately.
🌱 Green Habits You Can Start Today
  • Carry a cloth bag instead of using plastic bags.
  • Plant trees and take care of plants in your school/home.
  • Separate dry and wet waste at home.
  • Say NO to single-use plastic.
  • Save paper — use both sides before recycling.
  • Spread awareness about environmental protection.

📝 MCQ Quiz — Practice Test

📚 Subject aur Chapter choose karein

Question 1 of 10
⏱️ 30s
🎉

Excellent!

0%
Score
0
✅ Correct
0
❌ Wrong
0
