Well done! Keep it up!

📚 Chapters
📋 IT Class 10
Unit 1: Communication Skills-II Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-II Unit 3: ICT Skills-II Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-II Unit 5: Green Skills-II

💬 IT Class X (402) — Unit 1: Communication Skills – II

💬
CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 1

Communication Skills – II

Methods of communication, verbal & non-verbal, communication cycle, feedback, barriers, writing skills (parts of speech, sentences, active/passive voice).

📚 7 Sessions ✅ Full Syllabus 🗣️ Verbal + Non-Verbal

Session 1 Methods of Communication

▼
📖 CommunicationCommunication comes from the Latin word commūnicāre meaning 'to share'. It is the process of conveying information, knowledge, feelings or ideas through speaking, writing or body language.

Three Parts of Communication

PartMeaning
TransmittingSender conveys message through a medium
ListeningReceiver understands the message
FeedbackReceiver responds back to complete the cycle

Elements of Communication

ElementDescription
SenderPerson who begins the communication
MessageInformation, ideas, or questions the sender wants to share
ChannelMedium used — spoken word, email, phone, gestures
ReceiverPerson who receives the message
FeedbackReceiver's acknowledgement and response

Methods of Communication

MethodDescription
Face-to-faceEasiest and quickest; message understood clearly
E-mailCan be sent to one or multiple people quickly
Notices/PostersHelpful when same message is sent to a large group
Business MeetingsUse text, charts, graphs for sharing information and decisions
Other MethodsSocial networks, messages, phone calls, newsletters, blogs

Session 2 Verbal Communication

▼
📖 Verbal CommunicationExchange of information using spoken or written words to convey thoughts, ideas and emotions. Speaking is one of the most effective and commonly used ways of communicating.
TypeDescriptionExamples
InterpersonalOne-on-one conversation (formal or informal)Business discussion, talking with friends
Written CommunicationInvolves writing wordsLetters, reports, SMS, social media, emails
Small GroupMore than two people involvedBoard meetings, team meetings, press conferences
Public CommunicationOne person speaks to a large audienceElection campaigns, public speeches
⭐ Advantages & Disadvantages

✅ Advantages: Easy, adaptable, immediate, clear

❌ Disadvantages: Relies on accuracy of words; no permanent record; limited non-verbal cues

🎤 Tips for Mastering Verbal Communication
  • Speak clearly and at the right speed
  • Use simple words; avoid jargon
  • Listen carefully before responding
  • Maintain eye contact
  • Be respectful and polite

Session 3 Non-Verbal Communication

▼
📖 Non-Verbal CommunicationMessages sent without using words — through body language, gestures, facial expressions, eye contact, and other signals.
TypeDescription
Facial ExpressionsShow feelings — smile, frown, raised eyebrows
PostureBody position shows confidence or disinterest
GesturesHand/head movements — waving, nodding
Eye ContactShows interest and attention
TouchHandshake, pat on back
Space (Proxemics)Distance maintained while talking
ParalanguageTone, speed, volume of voice
🖼️ Visual CommunicationSending messages using images or pictures only. Examples: traffic signs, maps, charts, logos, emojis. Advantage: universally understood, quick to convey to large audience.

Session 4 Communication Cycle & Feedback

▼
📖 FeedbackFeedback is the receiver's response to the sender's message. It completes the communication cycle and tells the sender whether the message was understood correctly.
⭐ Types of Feedback
TypeDescription
Positive FeedbackAcknowledges that the message was understood; encourages the sender
Negative FeedbackPoints out errors or misunderstandings; helps correction
No FeedbackSilence or no response; creates confusion
⭐ Qualities of Good Feedback
  • Specific and clear
  • Given at the right time
  • Constructive — helps improvement
  • Respectful in tone
  • Based on observable facts, not opinions
⭐ Importance of Feedback
  • Ensures the message was understood correctly
  • Helps improve communication skills
  • Builds trust between sender and receiver
  • Prevents misunderstandings

Session 5 Barriers to Effective Communication

▼
⭐ 7Cs of Effective Communication
CMeaning
ClearBe clear about what you want to say
ConciseUse simple words; say only what is needed
ConcreteUse exact and specific phrases
CorrectUse correct spellings, language, and grammar
CoherentWords should make sense and relate to the main topic
CompleteMessage should have all needed information
CourteousBe respectful, friendly, and honest

Barriers to Communication

BarrierDescription
Physical BarriersDistance, noise, or weak phone signal
Linguistic BarriersDifferent languages; most common barrier
Interpersonal BarriersTone, emotions, or personal biases causing misunderstanding
Organisational BarriersNot following hierarchy, rules, policies
Cultural BarriersDifferent customs leading to inconvenience
⭐ Ways to Overcome Barriers
  • Use simple and clear language
  • Listen actively and patiently
  • Ask questions to confirm understanding
  • Be culturally sensitive and respectful
  • Use appropriate channel for the message

Sessions 6–7 Writing Skills: Parts of Speech & Sentences

▼

Parts of Speech

Part of SpeechDefinitionExample
NounName of person, place, thing, or ideaRahul, Delhi, book
PronounUsed in place of a nounhe, she, they, I
VerbAction or state of beingrun, eat, is, was
AdjectiveDescribes a noun or pronountall, red, happy
AdverbDescribes a verb, adjective or another adverbquickly, very, well
PrepositionShows relationship between noun and other wordsin, on, at, under
ConjunctionJoins words, phrases, or clausesand, but, or, because
InterjectionExpresses sudden emotionOh! Wow! Hurray!

Types of Objects in a Sentence

TypeMeaningExample
Direct ObjectDirectly receives the action of the verbPriya gave a book.
Indirect ObjectTells to whom/for whom action is donePriya gave Rahul a book.

Active & Passive Voice

VoiceDescriptionExample
Active VoiceSubject does the actionRadha is reading a book.
Passive VoiceSubject receives the actionA book is being read by Radha.

Types of Sentences (by Purpose)

TypeDescriptionEnd Mark
DeclarativeGives information / statement.
InterrogativeAsks a question?
ExclamatoryExpresses strong emotion!
ImperativeCommand, request, or advice. or !
📖 ParagraphA collection of sentences around a single main idea. Structure: Topic Sentence → Supporting Sentences → Concluding Sentence.
📚 Chapters
📋 IT Class 10
Unit 1: Communication Skills-II Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-II Unit 3: ICT Skills-II Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-II Unit 5: Green Skills-II

🧠 IT Class X (402) — Unit 2: Self-Management Skills – II

🧠
CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 2

Self-Management Skills – II

Stress management, self-awareness, self-motivation, goal setting (SMART), and time management.

📚 5 Sessions ✅ Full Syllabus 🎯 SMART Goals

Session 1 Stress Management

▼
📖 StressStress is our emotional, mental, physical, and social reaction to any perceived demands or threats (called stressors). Examples: exam pressure, family loss, fear of judgment, lack of sleep.
📖 Stress ManagementMaking a plan to cope effectively with daily pressures. Goal is to maintain a healthy balance between life's demands and our ability to meet them.
⭐ Stress Management Techniques
  • Exercise — Physical activity reduces stress hormones and boosts mood.
  • Deep Breathing — Slow deep breaths calm the nervous system.
  • Time Management — Planning tasks reduces last-minute panic.
  • Talking to someone — Sharing problems with friends or counsellors helps.
  • Hobbies — Doing things you enjoy diverts your mind from stress.
  • Positive Thinking — Focus on solutions, not problems.
ConceptMeaning
Ability to Work IndependentlyCompleting tasks on your own without constant supervision; requires self-discipline and time management
Emotional Intelligence (EI)The ability to understand, manage and express your own emotions and understand others' emotions. Helps in relationships and work.

Session 2 Self-Awareness: Strength & Weakness Analysis

▼
⭐ Core Self-Management Skills
  • Self-awareness — Ask for honest feedback; reflect on your interactions.
  • Responsibility — Take ownership of your tasks.
  • Time Management — Prioritise tasks; make and follow a timetable.
  • Adaptability — Stay updated; prepare for new changes.

Finding Strengths and Weaknesses

StrengthsWeaknesses
Things you do well and are good atAreas of improvement; things you struggle with
Ask: What do others appreciate about you?Ask: Where do you receive critical feedback?
Think of tasks you always handle wellBe open to feedback; don't feel bad about weaknesses
InterestAbility
Things you enjoy doing; activities that make you happy or curiousNatural or acquired capacity to perform a task with proficiency

Session 3 Self-Motivation

▼
📖 Self-MotivationThe ability to drive yourself to take action and persevere to complete tasks without external pressure or rewards.
TypeDescription
Intrinsic MotivationComes from within — personal satisfaction, passion, curiosity
Extrinsic MotivationComes from outside — rewards, grades, praise, money
⭐ Qualities of Self-Motivated People
  • Set clear personal goals
  • Maintain a positive attitude even in challenges
  • Persevere despite setbacks
  • Take initiative without being told
  • Celebrate small successes to stay motivated
⭐ How to Build Self-Motivation
  • Find your purpose — know why you want to achieve something
  • Break large goals into smaller, manageable steps
  • Surround yourself with positive people
  • Keep a record of your achievements
  • Reward yourself for completing tasks

Session 4 Goal Setting (SMART)

▼
📖 Goal SettingThe process of identifying what you want to achieve and creating a plan to accomplish it within a set timeframe.
⭐ SMART Goals
LetterStands ForMeaning
SSpecificClear and well-defined — not vague
MMeasurableYou can track your progress
AAchievableRealistic and attainable
RRelevantMatters to you and your overall aim
TTime-boundHas a deadline or time limit
💡 Example of SMART Goal "I will score above 80% in IT exam by studying 1 hour daily for the next 30 days."
→ Specific (IT exam), Measurable (80%), Achievable (1 hour/day), Relevant (improves marks), Time-bound (30 days)

Session 5 Time Management

▼
📖 Time ManagementThe ability to plan and control how you spend your time to accomplish goals effectively and efficiently.
⭐ Four Steps for Effective Time Management
StepAction
1. Make a To-Do ListWrite down all tasks you need to complete
2. Prioritise TasksDo the most important and urgent tasks first
3. Create a ScheduleAllocate specific time slots to each task
4. Avoid ProcrastinationStart tasks on time; avoid unnecessary delays
⭐ Tips to Manage Time Better
  • Wake up early and start your day with a plan
  • Avoid multitasking — focus on one task at a time
  • Take short breaks to refresh your mind
  • Say "no" to distractions (social media, games) during study time
  • Review your day before sleeping and plan tomorrow
📚 Chapters
📋 IT Class 10
Unit 1: Communication Skills-II Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-II Unit 3: ICT Skills-II Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-II Unit 5: Green Skills-II

💻 IT Class X (402) — Unit 3: ICT Skills – II

💻
CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 3

Basic ICT Skills – II

Basic computer operations, file management, computer care & maintenance, computer security and privacy.

📚 4 Sessions 🔒 Security & Privacy

Session 1 Basic Computer Operations

▼
📖 ICTICT (Information and Communication Technology) refers to all methods, tools, and concepts related to storing, recording and sending digital information.

Hardware vs Software

HardwareSoftware
Physical parts we can see and touchPrograms we can see but cannot touch
Examples: Monitor, keyboard, CPU, mouseExamples: Windows, MS Office, Antivirus
Divided into input, output, and storage devicesDivided into system software and application software
📖 Operating SystemAn Operating System (OS) is system software that manages computer hardware and provides services for programs. Examples: Windows, macOS, Linux, Android.

Starting & Shutting Down a Computer

  • Starting: Press power button → BIOS runs → OS loads → Login screen appears → Desktop shown.
  • Login: Enter username and password to access the computer.
  • Shutting Down: Start → Power → Shut Down (saves all work and safely powers off).
DeviceKey Functions
KeyboardType text; important keys: Enter, Backspace, Delete, Shift, Caps Lock, Ctrl, Alt, Tab
MouseLeft click (select), right click (options), double click (open), scroll (navigate)

Session 2 Performing Basic File Operations

▼
📖 Files & Folders
  • File — A collection of related data with a name and extension. E.g., notes.docx, photo.jpg
  • Folder — A container to organize files. Can contain files and subfolders.
⭐ File Operations
OperationDescription
CreateMake a new file or folder
OpenView the contents of a file
Copy & PasteDuplicate a file to another location
Cut & PasteMove a file from one location to another
RenameChange the name of a file or folder
DeleteRemove file (goes to Recycle Bin first)
SearchFind a file using the search box in File Explorer

Session 3 Computer Care & Maintenance

▼
⭐ Why Computer Maintenance is Important
  • Keeps computer running efficiently and fast
  • Prevents data loss and hardware damage
  • Extends the life of the device
  • Protects against viruses and malware
⭐ Basic Maintenance Tips
TipAction
Keep Device CleanWipe screen gently; keep keyboard dust-free; avoid liquids near computer
Maintenance ScheduleRun disk cleanup and defragmentation regularly
Backup DataRegularly save important files to external drive or cloud storage
Scan for VirusesUse antivirus software and update it regularly
Increase PerformanceUninstall unused programs; keep OS updated
Remove SPAMDelete unwanted emails and programs; avoid clicking suspicious links

Session 4 Computer Security & Privacy

▼
🔒 Threats to Computers
ThreatDescription
VirusMalicious program that copies itself and damages files
MalwareAny software designed to damage or gain unauthorised access to a computer
SpywareSecretly monitors and collects your personal information
RansomwareLocks your files and demands payment to unlock them
PhishingFake emails/websites that trick you into sharing passwords or bank details
HackingUnauthorised access to a computer or network
⭐ Protecting Your Computer & Data
  • Install and keep antivirus software up-to-date
  • Use strong passwords — mix of letters, numbers, and symbols
  • Never share passwords with anyone
  • Don't click on suspicious links or download unknown files
  • Enable firewall on your computer
  • Use HTTPS websites for online shopping/banking
  • Regularly backup important data
  • Log out of accounts after use on shared computers
📚 Chapters
📋 IT Class 10
Unit 1: Communication Skills-II Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-II Unit 3: ICT Skills-II Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-II Unit 5: Green Skills-II

🚀 IT Class X (402) — Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills – II

🚀
CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 4

Entrepreneurial Skills – II

Entrepreneurship and society, qualities & functions of an entrepreneur, myths about entrepreneurship, and entrepreneurship as a career option.

📚 4 Sessions 💡 Myths Busted

Session 1 Entrepreneurship and Society

▼
📖 EntrepreneurshipThe process of developing a business plan, launching and running a business using innovation to meet customer needs and make a profit.
📖 EntrepreneurA person who is self-employed, willing to take calculated risks, and brings a new idea to start a business. Not all self-employed people are entrepreneurs — only those who innovate.
⭐ What Entrepreneurs Do
  • Identify new business opportunities
  • Take risks and make decisions
  • Organise resources (money, manpower, materials)
  • Launch and manage the business
  • Create employment for others
  • Contribute to the economy and society

Session 2 Qualities & Functions of an Entrepreneur

▼
QualityDescription
Creativity & InnovationAbility to come up with new ideas and solutions
Risk-TakingWillingness to face uncertainty and calculated risks
DeterminationPersisting through failures and setbacks
LeadershipInspiring and guiding a team toward goals
Decision-MakingMaking quick, informed decisions
CommunicationExpressing ideas clearly to customers and team
Problem SolvingFinding solutions to business challenges
Self-ConfidenceBelief in one's own vision and abilities
⭐ Functions of an Entrepreneur
  • Idea Generation — Come up with innovative business ideas
  • Planning — Develop a detailed business plan
  • Resource Management — Arrange capital, manpower, and materials
  • Risk-Taking — Bear the financial and operational risks
  • Decision Making — Take key business decisions
  • Marketing — Promote products/services to customers
  • Employment Creation — Hire people and create jobs

Session 3 Myths About Entrepreneurship

▼
⭐ Common Myths — Busted!
MythReality
You need a lot of money to start a businessMany successful businesses started with little capital; planning and creativity matter more
Only born leaders can be entrepreneursEntrepreneurial skills can be learned and developed over time
Entrepreneurs work aloneSuccessful entrepreneurs build strong teams and collaborate
You must have a completely new ideaImproving an existing idea or serving an underserved market is also entrepreneurship
Entrepreneurs are always successfulMost entrepreneurs fail multiple times before succeeding; failure is part of learning
Entrepreneurship is only for adultsYoung people can also be entrepreneurs; many famous companies were started by students

Session 4 Entrepreneurship as a Career Option

▼
⭐ Benefits of Choosing Entrepreneurship as a Career
  • Independence — Be your own boss; freedom to make decisions
  • Unlimited Earnings — Income depends on effort and success, not a fixed salary
  • Job Creation — Create opportunities for others in society
  • Personal Satisfaction — Turn your passion into a profession
  • Innovation — Contribute new ideas and solutions to society
  • Flexibility — Work on your own schedule
⭐ Challenges of Entrepreneurship
  • Financial risk — may lose investment
  • No fixed income initially
  • High stress and long working hours
  • Requires constant learning and adaptation
  • Market competition can be tough
💡 Entrepreneurship vs Wage Employment
EntrepreneurshipWage Employment
You are your own bossWork for an employer
Income based on business performanceFixed monthly salary
High risk, high rewardLower risk, stable income
Creates jobs for othersYou are the employee
📚 Chapters
📋 IT Class 10
Unit 1: Communication Skills-II Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-II Unit 3: ICT Skills-II Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-II Unit 5: Green Skills-II

🌿 IT Class X (402) — Unit 5: Green Skills – II

🌿
CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part A · Unit 5

Green Skills – II

Sustainable development, SDGs, our role in sustainable development — environment, clean energy, clean water, responsible consumption, and more.

📚 2 Sessions 🌍 17 SDGs

Session 1 Sustainable Development

▼
📖 Sustainable DevelopmentDevelopment that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. It balances economic growth, environmental care, and social well-being. (World Commission on Environment and Development, 1987)
⭐ Importance of Sustainable Development
  • Ensures resources are available for future generations
  • Reduces pollution and environmental damage
  • Promotes social equality and economic growth
  • Helps combat climate change
  • Encourages responsible use of natural resources

Three Major Problems in Sustainable Development

ProblemDescription
FoodFertile land reducing; soil nutrients depleting; population growing faster than food production
WaterOnly 3% of Earth's water is fresh; overuse and pollution making clean water scarce
EnergyHeavy reliance on fossil fuels (coal, petroleum) which are non-renewable and cause pollution
⭐ How to Conserve Sustainable Development
  • Use renewable energy sources (solar, wind)
  • Practice water conservation (rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation)
  • Reduce, Reuse, Recycle waste
  • Plant trees and prevent deforestation
  • Use public transport or carpool to reduce emissions
  • Buy local and seasonal products
⭐ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) — UN 2030 Agenda

The United Nations set 17 SDGs to achieve by 2030. Key goals:

Goal No.SDG
1No Poverty
2Zero Hunger
3Good Health and Well-being
4Quality Education
6Clean Water and Sanitation
7Affordable and Clean Energy
8Decent Work and Economic Growth
10Reduced Inequalities
11Sustainable Cities and Communities
12Responsible Consumption and Production
13Climate Action
14Life Below Water
15Life on Land

Session 2 Our Role in Sustainable Development

▼
⭐ What YOU Can Do for Each SDG
SDG AreaYour Role
Quality EducationStudy regularly, share knowledge, teach others, use digital learning tools
Clean Water & SanitationFix leaking taps, don't waste water, keep water bodies clean, use toilets
Affordable & Clean EnergySwitch off lights when not in use, use LED bulbs, support solar energy use
Decent Work & Economic GrowthDevelop skills, work honestly, support local businesses
Reduced InequalitiesTreat everyone with respect regardless of gender, caste, religion
Sustainable CitiesUse public transport, plant trees, keep streets clean, avoid noise pollution
Responsible ConsumptionBuy only what you need, reduce plastic, compost food waste, recycle
Life Below WaterDon't throw garbage in rivers/sea, avoid plastic bags, support clean water campaigns
Life on LandPlant trees, avoid deforestation, protect animals, stop burning waste
♻️ Sustainable Development Initiatives
  • Swachh Bharat Mission — Clean India Campaign by Government of India
  • National Solar Mission — Promoting solar energy use across India
  • Jal Shakti Abhiyan — Water conservation drive
  • Van Mahotsav — Annual tree planting festival in India
  • Smart Cities Mission — Developing sustainable urban areas
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

🗄️ IP Class 11 (065) — Unit 1: Chapter 1 – Introduction to Computer System

🗄️
CBSE Class XI · Informatics Practices Code 065 · Unit 1 · Chapter 1

Introduction to Computer System

Computer basics, types, components, CPU, Memory, Input/Output devices, Software, Operating System and Number System — complete notes as per CBSE syllabus.

📚 8 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💡 Diagrams Included

Topic 1 What is a Computer?

▼
💻 ComputerA computer is an electronic machine that accepts data as input, processes it according to a set of instructions (program), and produces output. It can also store data for future use.

Characteristics of a Computer

CharacteristicDescription
SpeedComputers work at extremely high speeds — millions of instructions per second (MIPS)
AccuracyGives 100% accurate results if the input and program are correct
StorageCan store large amounts of data permanently or temporarily
VersatilityCan perform a wide variety of tasks — from calculations to graphics
DiligenceNever gets tired or bored — performs the same task repeatedly with equal accuracy
No IntelligenceCannot think on its own — only follows given instructions (GIGO: Garbage In, Garbage Out)
⭐ GIGO Principle
Garbage In, Garbage Out — if incorrect data is given as input, the output will also be incorrect. The computer itself never makes mistakes.

Topic 2 Block Diagram of a Computer

▼
🔲 Block DiagramA computer system has 5 main functional units that work together to process data.
⌨️ Input Unit
→
🧠 CPU
(CU + ALU)
→
🖥️ Output Unit
💾 Memory Unit (Primary + Secondary)
UnitFunctionExample
Input UnitAccepts data and instructions from the userKeyboard, Mouse, Scanner
CPU – CUControl Unit — controls and coordinates all operations—
CPU – ALUArithmetic & Logic Unit — performs calculations and comparisons+, −, *, /, >, <
Memory UnitStores data and programs temporarily or permanentlyRAM, ROM, Hard Disk
Output UnitDisplays or presents the processed result to the userMonitor, Printer, Speaker

Topic 3 CPU – Central Processing Unit

▼
🧠 CPUThe CPU (Central Processing Unit) is the brain of the computer. It performs all processing tasks. It has three main components: Control Unit (CU), Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), and Registers.

Parts of the CPU

  • Control Unit (CU): Manages the Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle. Coordinates all hardware components and controls data flow inside the CPU.
  • Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU): Performs all arithmetic operations (+, −, *, /) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, comparisons like >, <, ==).
  • Registers: Very small, ultra-fast memory locations inside the CPU used to hold data temporarily during processing. Examples: Accumulator, Program Counter (PC), Memory Address Register (MAR), Memory Data Register (MDR).
⭐ CPU Speed
CPU speed is measured in GHz (Gigahertz). Higher GHz means a faster processor. Modern CPUs also have multiple cores (dual-core, quad-core) to handle multiple tasks simultaneously.

Topic 4 Memory

▼
💾 MemoryMemory is where the computer stores data and programs. It is broadly divided into two types: Primary Memory (main memory) and Secondary Memory (auxiliary storage).

Primary Memory

🔵 RAM – Random Access Memory
  • Volatile — data is lost when power is switched off
  • Both read and write operations are possible
  • Stores currently running programs and data
  • Fast but temporary storage
  • Types: SRAM (faster, costlier) and DRAM (slower, cheaper)
🟣 ROM – Read Only Memory
  • Non-volatile — data is retained even when power is off
  • Data can only be read, not written by the user
  • Stores firmware / BIOS (boot instructions)
  • Types: PROM, EPROM, EEPROM

Cache Memory

Cache memory is a small, extremely fast memory located between the CPU and RAM. It stores frequently used data so the CPU does not have to repeatedly access the slower RAM. It has three levels: L1 (fastest, inside CPU), L2, and L3 (larger but slower).

Secondary Memory

TypeExamplesFeatures
MagneticHard Disk Drive (HDD), Magnetic TapeLarge capacity, relatively slow, affordable
OpticalCD, DVD, Blu-ray DiscPortable, can be read-only or writable
Flash / Solid StateSSD, Pen Drive (USB Flash), Memory CardFast, durable, no moving parts

Memory Hierarchy (Speed: Fastest → Slowest)

⚡ Registers (Fastest, Smallest)
↓
🏃 Cache Memory (L1, L2, L3)
↓
💡 RAM – Primary Memory
↓
💾 Secondary Memory (Slowest, Largest)
⭐ Memory Units
1 Bit → 1 Byte (8 bits) → 1 KB (1024 B) → 1 MB (1024 KB) → 1 GB (1024 MB) → 1 TB (1024 GB) → 1 PB (1024 TB)

Topic 5 Input & Output Devices

▼
⌨️ Input Devices
  • Keyboard – text and command input
  • Mouse – pointing and clicking device
  • Scanner – digitises documents and images
  • Microphone – audio input
  • Webcam – video input
  • OMR – Optical Mark Reader (MCQ answer sheets)
  • OCR – Optical Character Recognition
  • Barcode Reader – reads barcodes on products
  • Touchscreen – input via touch
  • Joystick – used mainly in gaming
🖥️ Output Devices
  • Monitor (VDU) – visual display; types: CRT, LCD, LED
  • Printer – Impact (Dot Matrix) vs Non-Impact (Laser, Inkjet)
  • Plotter – large-format drawings and engineering diagrams
  • Speaker – audio output
  • Projector – projects display on a large screen
  • Headphones – personal audio output
📝 Quick Tip
Input devices send data to the computer. Output devices receive processed results from the computer. Some devices (e.g. touchscreen, modem) are both input and output — called I/O devices.

Topic 6 Software

▼
💿 SoftwareSoftware is a collection of programs and instructions that tell the computer what to do. Without software, hardware is useless. It is broadly classified into System Software and Application Software.
TypeSub-typeExamples
System SoftwareOperating SystemWindows, Linux, macOS, Android, iOS
Language TranslatorsCompiler, Interpreter, Assembler
Utility SoftwareAntivirus, Disk Defragmenter, Backup Tools, File Manager
Application SoftwareGeneral PurposeMS Word, Excel, Photoshop, VLC Media Player
Specific PurposePayroll System, Railway Reservation, Hospital Management

Language Translators

⚙️ Compiler

Translates the entire source code into machine code at once before execution. Faster execution. Errors are shown all at once after compilation. Example: C, C++.

🐍 Interpreter

Translates and executes source code line by line. Easier to debug. Stops at the first error. Slower execution. Example: Python, JavaScript.

🔧 Assembler

Converts assembly language (low-level mnemonics) into machine code. Used for system-level programming.

📊 Compiler vs Interpreter
  • Compiler: compile first, then run
  • Interpreter: translates and runs line by line
  • Compiler: faster execution
  • Interpreter: easier debugging

Topic 7 Operating System

▼
🖥️ Operating System (OS)An Operating System is a system software that acts as an interface between the user and the computer hardware. It manages all hardware resources and provides a platform for running application software.

Functions of an Operating System

  • Process Management: Handles CPU scheduling, creation and termination of processes.
  • Memory Management: Allocates and deallocates RAM to programs as needed.
  • File Management: Creates, deletes, organises, and controls access to files and folders.
  • Device Management: Controls and manages input/output devices via device drivers.
  • Security & Protection: Provides user authentication (login), access control, and data protection.
  • User Interface: Provides either a CLI (Command Line Interface) or GUI (Graphical User Interface).
Type of OSDescriptionExample
Batch OSJobs are collected and processed in batches without user interactionEarly IBM mainframes
Time-Sharing OSMultiple users share CPU time simultaneouslyUNIX, Linux
Real-Time OS (RTOS)Guarantees response within a strict time deadline — used in critical systemsVxWorks, FreeRTOS (robots, medical devices)
Network OSManages network resources and servicesWindows Server, Novell NetWare
Mobile OSDesigned for mobile and touch-based devicesAndroid, iOS
Distributed OSManages a group of networked computers as a single systemGoogle's internal systems
⭐ Popular Operating Systems
Windows (Microsoft)  |  macOS (Apple)  |  Linux (Open Source)  |  Ubuntu  |  Android (Google)  |  iOS (Apple)

Topic 8 Number System

▼
🔢 Number SystemA number system is a way to represent numbers using a set of symbols (digits). Computers internally use only Binary (0s and 1s). We need to understand different number systems and how to convert between them.
Number SystemBaseDigits UsedExample
Decimal100 – 925510
Binary20, 1111111112
Octal80 – 73778
Hexadecimal160 – 9, A – FFF16

Conversions

🔄 Decimal → Binary (255)
255 ÷ 2 = 127 R 1 → 127 ÷ 2 = 63 R 1 → 63 ÷ 2 = 31 R 1 → 31 ÷ 2 = 15 R 1 → 15 ÷ 2 = 7 R 1 → 7 ÷ 2 = 3 R 1 → 3 ÷ 2 = 1 R 1 → 1 ÷ 2 = 0 R 1
Read remainders bottom to top → 111111112
🔄 Binary → Decimal (1010)
1×2³ + 0×2² + 1×2¹ + 0×2⁰ = 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 1010
🔄 Decimal → Octal (255)
255 ÷ 8 = 31 R 7 → 31 ÷ 8 = 3 R 7 → 3 ÷ 8 = 0 R 3
Read remainders bottom to top → 3778
🔄 Decimal → Hexadecimal (255)
255 ÷ 16 = 15 R 15 (F) → 15 ÷ 16 = 0 R 15 (F)
Read remainders bottom to top → FF16
⭐ Shortcut Rules
1 Hex digit = 4 Binary digits  |  1 Octal digit = 3 Binary digits
F = 1111, E = 1110, D = 1101, C = 1100, B = 1011, A = 1010
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

🐍 IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python

🐍
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 2

Getting Started with Python

Python introduction, tokens, data types, variables, operators, input/output and type conversion — complete CBSE IP Class 11 notes.

Topic 1 Introduction to Python

▼
🐍 PythonPython is a high-level, interpreted, general-purpose programming language created by Guido van Rossum in 1991. It is widely used because of its simple, readable syntax that resembles English.

Features of Python

  • Simple and Easy to Learn: English-like syntax makes it beginner-friendly
  • Interpreted: Code is executed line by line — no separate compilation needed
  • Free and Open Source: Python is freely available and its source code is open
  • Platform Independent: Runs on Windows, Linux and macOS without modification
  • Dynamically Typed: No need to declare the data type of a variable
  • Large Standard Library: Rich set of built-in modules for various tasks
  • Object-Oriented: Supports OOP concepts like classes and objects
⭐ Python Versions
Python 2 and Python 3 are the two major versions. Python 3 is the current standard. CBSE curriculum uses Python 3.

Topic 2 Tokens in Python

▼
🔤 TokenA token is the smallest individual unit of a Python program. Every Python program is made up of tokens. There are five types of tokens.
Token TypeDescriptionExamples
KeywordsReserved words with special meaning — cannot be used as identifiersif, else, for, while, def, class, True, False, None, return, import
IdentifiersNames given to variables, functions, classesname, age, calculate, Student
LiteralsFixed constant values written directly in code10, 3.14, "Hello", True
OperatorsSymbols that perform operations on values+, -, *, /, %, //, **, ==, !=
PunctuatorsSymbols that define the structure of code( ) [ ] { } : , . ; @ #

Rules for Identifiers

  • Can contain letters (a–z, A–Z), digits (0–9) and underscore (_)
  • Must NOT begin with a digit — 2name ❌ , name2 ✅
  • Cannot be a Python keyword — for ❌
  • Case-sensitive — Name and name are different identifiers
  • No special characters allowed — my-var ❌ , my_var ✅

Topic 3 Data Types

▼
📊 Data TypeA data type defines the kind of value a variable can hold and the operations that can be performed on it. Python automatically determines the type (dynamic typing).
Data TypeKeywordExampleDescription
Integerintx = 10Whole numbers, positive or negative
Floatfloatpi = 3.14Numbers with a decimal point
Complexcomplexz = 3+4jReal + imaginary part
Stringstrs = "Hello"Sequence of characters
Booleanboolflag = TrueTrue or False only
Listlist[1, 2, 3]Ordered, mutable collection
Tupletuple(1, 2, 3)Ordered, immutable collection
Dictionarydict{"a": 1}Key-value pairs
NoneNoneTypex = NoneRepresents absence of value
📝 type() Function
print(type(10)) # <class 'int'>
print(type(3.14)) # <class 'float'>
print(type("Hello")) # <class 'str'>
print(type(True)) # <class 'bool'>

Topic 4 Variables, Input & Output

▼
📦 VariableA variable is a named location in memory that stores a value. In Python, the data type is automatically assigned based on the value (no explicit declaration needed).
📝 Variable Assignment
name = "Riya" # string
age = 17 # integer
marks = 95.5 # float
a = b = c = 10 # multiple assignment — all get 10
x, y, z = 1, 2, 3 # unpack multiple values at once
📝 input() and print()
name = input("Enter your name: ") # always returns a string
age = int(input("Enter age: ")) # convert to int
print("Hello", name, "Age:", age)
print(f"Hello {name}, Age: {age}") # f-string formatting

Type Conversion (Casting)

FunctionConverts ToExampleResult
int()Integerint("10")10
float()Floatfloat("3.14")3.14
str()Stringstr(100)"100"
bool()Booleanbool(0)False (0=False, non-zero=True)

Topic 5 Operators

▼
TypeOperatorsExample & Result
Arithmetic+   -   *   /   %   //   **10 % 3 = 1  |  10 // 3 = 3  |  2 ** 3 = 8  |  10 / 3 = 3.333
Relational==   !=   >   <   >=   <=5 == 5 → True  |  3 != 4 → True
Logicaland   or   notTrue and False → False  |  not True → False
Assignment=   +=   -=   *=   /=x += 5 means x = x + 5
Identityis   is notx is y → True if same object in memory
Membershipin   not in"a" in "apple" → True
⭐ Operator Precedence (Highest → Lowest)
** → +x,-x → * / // % → + - → Comparisons → not → and → or
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

🔀 IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 3 - Flow of Control

🔀
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 3

Flow of Control

Conditional statements (if / elif / else), for and while loops, loop control statements (break, continue, pass) and nested loops.

Topic 1 Conditional Statements

▼
🔀 Flow of ControlThe order in which statements are executed is called flow of control. By default it is sequential (top to bottom), but it can be altered using conditional statements and loops.
if Statement
if condition:
    statement(s)

Executes the block only if condition is True.

if-else Statement
if condition:
    block1
else:
    block2
if-elif-else
if c1:
    b1
elif c2:
    b2
else:
    b3
Nested if
if c1:
    if c2:
        block
📝 Grade Calculator
marks = int(input("Enter marks: "))
if marks >= 90:
    print("Grade A+")
elif marks >= 75:
    print("Grade A")
elif marks >= 60:
    print("Grade B")
elif marks >= 40:
    print("Grade C")
else:
    print("Fail")
⭐ Indentation Rule
Python uses indentation (4 spaces) to define code blocks. All statements inside an if/else must be equally indented. Wrong indentation causes IndentationError.

Topic 2 for Loop

▼
🔁 for LoopA for loop is used when the number of iterations is known in advance. It iterates over a sequence (list, string, tuple) or a range of numbers.
range() SyntaxValues Generated
range(n)0, 1, 2, … n-1
range(start, stop)start, start+1, … stop-1
range(start, stop, step)start, start+step, … (below stop)
📝 for loop examples
for i in range(5): # 0,1,2,3,4
    print(i)

for i in range(1, 6): # 1,2,3,4,5
    print(i)

for i in range(0, 10, 2): # 0,2,4,6,8
    print(i)

for ch in "Python": # P,y,t,h,o,n
    print(ch)

for item in [10, 20, 30]: # list iteration
    print(item)

Topic 3 while Loop

▼
🔄 while LoopA while loop repeats a block of code as long as its condition is True. Used when the number of repetitions is not known in advance.
📝 while loop
n = 1
while n <= 5:
    print(n)
    n += 1 # update is essential — prevents infinite loop!
⭐ for vs while
Use for when iterations are known (loop 10 times). Use while when iterations depend on a condition (loop until user types "quit").

Topic 4 Loop Control: break, continue, pass

▼
StatementDescriptionUse Case
breakImmediately exits the loopStop when item is found
continueSkips the current iteration, moves to nextSkip even numbers
passDoes nothing — placeholder for empty blockEmpty loop body
📝 break, continue, pass
for i in range(10): # break: stops at 5
    if i == 5: break
    print(i) # prints 0,1,2,3,4

for i in range(10): # continue: skips even
    if i % 2 == 0: continue
    print(i) # prints 1,3,5,7,9

for i in range(5): # pass: valid empty loop
    pass

Topic 5 Nested Loops & Patterns

▼
🔁 Nested LoopA loop inside another loop. For each iteration of the outer loop, the inner loop completes its full cycle.
📝 Multiplication Table
for i in range(1, 4):
    for j in range(1, 4):
        print(i * j, end=" ")
    print()
# Output:
# 1 2 3
# 2 4 6
# 3 6 9
📝 Star Pattern
n = 5
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    print("* " * i)
# * / * * / * * * / * * * * / * * * * *
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

⚙️ IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 4 - Functions

⚙️
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 4

Functions

Defining and calling functions, types of arguments, return values, variable scope and built-in functions.

Topic 1 Introduction to Functions

▼
⚙️ FunctionA function is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task. Functions make programs modular and avoid code repetition.
  • Built-in Functions: Predefined in Python — print(), input(), len(), range(), type(), abs(), max(), min(), sum()
  • User-defined Functions: Created by the programmer using the def keyword
  • Lambda Functions: Anonymous small functions using the lambda keyword
📝 Define and Call a Function
def greet(name): # def = keyword, name = parameter
    print("Hello,", name) # function body

greet("Riya") # call — Output: Hello, Riya
greet("Amit") # reusable — Output: Hello, Amit
⭐ Advantages of Functions
1. Code Reusability — write once, use many times
2. Modularity — large program broken into smaller parts
3. Easy Debugging — fix one function without affecting others
4. Readability — cleaner, easier to understand code

Topic 2 Arguments & Return Statement

▼
Argument TypeDescriptionExample
PositionalPassed in order — position mattersadd(3, 5) → a=3, b=5
KeywordPassed by name — order does not matteradd(b=5, a=3)
DefaultHas a default value used when no argument is passeddef greet(name="Guest")
Variable Length*args accepts any number of positional arguments as a tupledef add(*nums): return sum(nums)
📝 return Statement
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 5)
print(result) # 8

def min_max(lst):
    return min(lst), max(lst) # multiple return values

lo, hi = min_max([3, 1, 7, 2])
print(lo, hi) # 1 7

Topic 3 Scope of Variables

▼
🌍 Global Variable

Defined outside all functions. Accessible anywhere in the program.

x = 10
def show():
    print(x) # OK — prints 10
🏠 Local Variable

Defined inside a function. Only accessible within that function.

def show():
    y = 5
    print(y) # OK
# print(y) — NameError!
📝 global keyword
x = 10
def change():
    global x # tells Python to use the global x
    x = 20
change()
print(x) # 20

Topic 4 Built-in Functions & math Module

▼
FunctionDescriptionExample
len()Returns length of a sequencelen("Hello") → 5
abs()Absolute (positive) valueabs(-7) → 7
round(x, n)Rounds x to n decimal placesround(3.567, 2) → 3.57
max() / min()Largest / smallest valuemax(3,1,7) → 7
sum()Sum of an iterablesum([1,2,3]) → 6
pow(x, y)x raised to power ypow(2, 8) → 256
sorted()Returns a new sorted listsorted([3,1,2]) → [1,2,3]
📝 math Module
import math
print(math.sqrt(16)) # 4.0
print(math.pi) # 3.14159265...
print(math.floor(3.9)) # 3
print(math.ceil(3.1)) # 4
print(math.factorial(5)) # 120
print(math.pow(2, 10)) # 1024.0
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

📝 IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 5 - Strings

📝
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 5

Strings

String creation, indexing, slicing, operators, traversal and all important built-in string methods.

Topic 1 String Basics & Indexing

▼
📝 StringA string is an ordered, immutable sequence of characters enclosed in single, double or triple quotes. Immutable means individual characters cannot be changed after creation.
P
y
t
h
o
n
0
1
2
3
4
5
-6
-5
-4
-3
-2
-1
📝 Indexing and Slicing [start:stop:step]
s = "Python"
print(s[0]) # P — first character (positive index)
print(s[-1]) # n — last character (negative index)
print(s[1:4]) # yth — index 1,2,3 (stop is excluded)
print(s[:3]) # Pyt — from start to index 2
print(s[3:]) # hon — from index 3 to end
print(s[::-1]) # nohtyP — reversed string (step = -1)
print(s[::2]) # Pto — every 2nd character
⭐ Key Rule
In slicing s[start:stop:step], the stop index is always EXCLUDED. Default: start=0, stop=end of string, step=1.

Topic 2 String Operators

▼
OperatorNameExampleOutput
+Concatenation — joins strings"Hello" + " " + "World""Hello World"
*Repetition — repeats string n times"Ha" * 3"HaHaHa"
inMembership — checks if substring is present"Py" in "Python"True
not inMembership — checks if substring is absent"z" not in "Python"True

Topic 3 String Methods

▼
MethodDescriptionExample & Result
upper()Converts to UPPERCASE"hello".upper() → "HELLO"
lower()Converts to lowercase"HELLO".lower() → "hello"
title()Capitalises first letter of each word"hello world".title() → "Hello World"
capitalize()Capitalises only the first character"hello".capitalize() → "Hello"
strip()Removes leading and trailing whitespace" hi ".strip() → "hi"
replace(old, new)Replaces all occurrences of old with new"apple".replace("p","b") → "abble"
split(sep)Splits string into a list at separator"a,b,c".split(",") → ['a','b','c']
join(list)Joins list items into a string"-".join(['a','b','c']) → "a-b-c"
find(sub)Returns index of first occurrence; -1 if not found"hello".find("l") → 2
count(sub)Counts how many times sub appears"banana".count("a") → 3
isalpha()True if all characters are alphabets"abc".isalpha() → True
isdigit()True if all characters are digits"123".isdigit() → True
isalnum()True if all chars are letters or digits"abc123".isalnum() → True
startswith(s)True if string starts with s"Python".startswith("Py") → True
endswith(s)True if string ends with s"Python".endswith("on") → True
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

📋 IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 6 - Lists

📋
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 6

Lists

List creation, indexing, slicing, mutability, list operations and all important list methods — complete CBSE notes.

Topic 1 List Basics

▼
📋 ListA list is an ordered, mutable (changeable) collection of items enclosed in square brackets [ ]. Lists can store mixed data types — integers, strings, floats, booleans and even other lists.
📝 Creating and Accessing Lists
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
mixed = [1, "Hello", 3.14, True]
nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
empty = []

print(nums[0]) # 1 — first element
print(nums[-1]) # 5 — last element
print(nums[1:4]) # [2, 3, 4]
nums[0] = 10 # MUTABLE — can change elements!
print(nums) # [10, 2, 3, 4, 5]
⭐ List vs String
List is mutable — elements can be changed after creation. String is immutable — characters cannot be changed after creation.

Topic 2 List Methods

▼
MethodDescriptionExample
append(x)Adds x at the endlst.append(10)
insert(i, x)Inserts x at index ilst.insert(2, 99)
extend(lst2)Appends all elements of lst2lst.extend([4, 5])
remove(x)Removes first occurrence of xlst.remove(3)
pop(i)Removes and returns element at index i (default: last)lst.pop()
sort()Sorts list in place (ascending by default)lst.sort(reverse=True)
reverse()Reverses the list in placelst.reverse()
index(x)Returns index of first occurrence of xlst.index(5)
count(x)Returns how many times x appearslst.count(3)
copy()Returns a shallow copy of the listlst2 = lst.copy()
clear()Removes all elementslst.clear()
📝 List Operations
a = [1, 2, 3]; b = [4, 5, 6]
print(a + b) # [1,2,3,4,5,6] — concatenation
print(a * 2) # [1,2,3,1,2,3] — repetition
print(3 in a) # True — membership
print(len(a)) # 3 — length
print(sum(a)) # 6 — sum
print(max(a)) # 3
print(min(a)) # 1
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

📦 IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries

📦
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 7

Tuples & Dictionaries

Tuples (immutable sequences) and Dictionaries (key-value pairs) — creation, operations, methods and differences.

Topic 1 Tuples

▼
📦 TupleA tuple is an ordered, immutable sequence enclosed in round brackets ( ). Once created, its elements cannot be added, removed or modified.
📝 Tuple Creation and Access
t = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
single = (5,) # single-element tuple — trailing comma is ESSENTIAL
empty = ()

print(t[0]) # 1
print(t[-1]) # 5
print(t[1:4]) # (2, 3, 4)
# t[0] = 10 — TypeError: tuples are immutable!
MethodDescriptionExample
count(x)Number of times x appears(1,2,2,3).count(2) → 2
index(x)Index of first occurrence of x(1,2,3).index(2) → 1
⭐ List vs Tuple
ListTuple
MutableImmutable
[ ] brackets( ) brackets
SlowerFaster
Many methodsOnly count() and index()
For changeable dataFor fixed/constant data

Topic 2 Dictionaries

▼
🔑 DictionaryA dictionary is an unordered collection of key-value pairs enclosed in curly braces { }. Keys must be unique and immutable. Values can be of any type.
📝 Dictionary Operations
student = {"name": "Riya", "age": 17, "marks": 95}

print(student["name"]) # Riya
print(student.get("age")) # 17 (safe — no KeyError)
student["city"] = "Delhi" # add new key-value
student["age"] = 18 # update existing value
del student["marks"] # delete a key
MethodDescription
keys()Returns all keys
values()Returns all values
items()Returns all key-value pairs as tuples
get(k, default)Returns value for key k; default if not found
update(d2)Updates with key-value pairs from d2
pop(k)Removes key k and returns its value
clear()Removes all items
copy()Returns a shallow copy
📝 Iterating a Dictionary
for key in student:
    print(key, ":", student[key])

for k, v in student.items():
    print(k, "->", v)
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

🗃️ IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 8 - Database Concepts

🗃️
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 3 · Chapter 8

Database Concepts

Introduction to databases, DBMS vs RDBMS, important terminology, types of keys and table relationships.

Topic 1 Database & DBMS

▼
🗃️ DatabaseA database is an organised collection of related data stored in a structured format so that it can be easily accessed, managed and updated.
💾 DBMSA Database Management System (DBMS) is software that creates, maintains and controls access to a database. Examples: MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SQLite, MS Access.
File SystemDBMS
Data redundancy (duplication) is commonRedundancy minimised through normalisation
Data inconsistency possibleData consistency enforced
Poor securityRole-based access control
Concurrent access difficultMultiple users can access simultaneously
No crash recoveryBuilt-in backup and recovery

Topic 2 RDBMS & Key Terminology

▼
🔗 RDBMSA Relational DBMS stores data in tables (relations). Tables are linked through keys. Examples: MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL.
TermAlso CalledMeaning
TableRelationStructured collection of data in rows and columns
RowTuple / RecordOne complete entry in a table
ColumnAttribute / FieldOne property or characteristic
Degree—Total number of columns in a table
Cardinality—Total number of rows in a table

Topic 3 Types of Keys

▼
KeyDescriptionExample
Primary KeyUniquely identifies each row. Cannot be NULL. Only one per table.RollNo in Students table
Candidate KeyAny column that could serve as primary keyRollNo or Aadhar No
Alternate KeyCandidate keys not chosen as primary keyAadhar No (if RollNo is primary)
Foreign KeyReferences Primary Key of another table — creates relationshipsDeptID in Employee referencing Department
Composite KeyPrimary key made of two or more columns(StudentID + SubjectCode) in Marks table
⭐ Primary Key Rules
1. Must be UNIQUE   2. Cannot be NULL   3. Only ONE per table   4. Should not change over time
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

🔍 IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL

🔍
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 3 · Chapter 9

Introduction to SQL

DDL, DML, DCL commands; CREATE, INSERT, SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, HAVING and aggregate functions.

Topic 1 SQL Categories & Data Types

▼
🔍 SQLSQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language used to communicate with an RDBMS. It is used to create structures, insert, retrieve, update and delete data.
CategoryFull FormCommands
DDLData Definition LanguageCREATE, DROP, ALTER, TRUNCATE
DMLData Manipulation LanguageINSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT
DCLData Control LanguageGRANT, REVOKE
TCLTransaction Control LanguageCOMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT
SQL Data TypeDescriptionExample
INT / INTEGERWhole numbersRollNo INT
FLOAT / DECIMALDecimal numbersMarks FLOAT
VARCHAR(n)Variable-length string up to n charactersName VARCHAR(50)
CHAR(n)Fixed-length string of exactly n charsGender CHAR(1)
DATEDate in YYYY-MM-DD formatDOB DATE

Topic 2 CREATE, INSERT & SELECT

▼
📝 CREATE TABLE
CREATE TABLE Students (
  RollNo INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Class INT,
  Marks FLOAT,
  City VARCHAR(30)
);
📝 INSERT INTO
INSERT INTO Students VALUES (1, 'Riya', 11, 95.5, 'Delhi');
INSERT INTO Students (RollNo, Name, Marks) VALUES (2, 'Amit', 88);
📝 SELECT Statement
SELECT * FROM Students; -- all rows & columns
SELECT Name, Marks FROM Students; -- specific columns
SELECT * FROM Students WHERE Marks > 80; -- with condition
SELECT * FROM Students WHERE City='Delhi' AND Marks>90;
SELECT DISTINCT City FROM Students; -- unique values only
SELECT * FROM Students ORDER BY Marks DESC; -- sort descending
SELECT * FROM Students LIMIT 5; -- first 5 rows
OperatorMeaningExample
=Equal toWHERE City = 'Delhi'
!= or <>Not equal toWHERE Marks != 0
BETWEENWithin a range (inclusive)WHERE Marks BETWEEN 60 AND 90
LIKEPattern matching (% = any chars)WHERE Name LIKE 'R%'
INMatches any in a listWHERE City IN ('Delhi','Mumbai')
IS NULLChecks for NULL valueWHERE City IS NULL

Topic 3 Aggregate Functions, GROUP BY & DML

▼
FunctionDescriptionExample
COUNT(*)Total number of rowsSELECT COUNT(*) FROM Students;
SUM(col)Sum of all values in a columnSELECT SUM(Marks) FROM Students;
AVG(col)Average value of a columnSELECT AVG(Marks) FROM Students;
MAX(col)Maximum value in a columnSELECT MAX(Marks) FROM Students;
MIN(col)Minimum value in a columnSELECT MIN(Marks) FROM Students;
📝 GROUP BY, HAVING, UPDATE, DELETE
-- Average marks per city
SELECT City, AVG(Marks) FROM Students GROUP BY City;

-- Only cities where average > 80
SELECT City, AVG(Marks) FROM Students
GROUP BY City HAVING AVG(Marks) > 80;

-- UPDATE existing data
UPDATE Students SET Marks = 90 WHERE RollNo = 1;

-- DELETE rows
DELETE FROM Students WHERE Marks < 40;
⭐ WHERE vs HAVING
WHERE — filters individual rows BEFORE grouping (cannot use aggregate functions)
HAVING — filters groups AFTER GROUP BY (can use aggregate functions like AVG, SUM)
📚 Chapters
🗄️ IP Class 11
Ch 1 - Introduction to Computer System Ch 2 - Getting Started with Python Ch 3 - Flow of Control Ch 4 - Functions Ch 5 - Strings Ch 6 - Lists Ch 7 - Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 8 - Database Concepts Ch 9 - Introduction to SQL Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

🚀 IP Class 11 (065) — Ch 10 - Emerging Trends

🚀
CBSE Class XI · IP Code 065 · Unit 4 · Chapter 10

Emerging Trends in Technology

Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, NLP, IoT, Cloud Computing (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), Big Data (3V's), Blockchain, AR, VR and 5G.

Topic 1 Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning

▼
🤖 Artificial Intelligence (AI)AI is the simulation of human intelligence in machines so they can think, learn, reason and solve problems. AI systems improve their performance with experience.
📊 Machine Learning (ML)ML is a subset of AI where machines automatically learn from data and improve over time without being explicitly programmed for every task.
Type of MLDescriptionExample
Supervised LearningTrained on labelled input-output pairs; predicts output for new inputsEmail spam detection, image classification
Unsupervised LearningFinds hidden patterns in unlabelled data without guidanceCustomer segmentation, clustering
Reinforcement LearningLearns by trial and error — rewards for correct actions, penalties for wrong onesChess AI (AlphaGo), self-driving cars
💬 Natural Language Processing (NLP)NLP enables computers to understand, interpret and generate human language (text and speech). Examples: Google Translate, Siri, Alexa, ChatGPT, Google Assistant.
⭐ AI Applications
Virtual Assistants · Recommendation Systems (Netflix, YouTube) · Facial Recognition · Medical Diagnosis · Self-driving Cars · Fraud Detection

Topic 2 IoT & Cloud Computing

▼
🌐 Internet of Things (IoT)IoT is the network of physical devices (sensors, appliances, vehicles) connected to the internet, capable of collecting and exchanging data without human intervention.
  • Smart Home: Smart bulbs, locks, thermostats controlled via phone
  • Wearables: Smartwatches, fitness bands tracking health data
  • Smart Agriculture: Soil moisture sensors, automated irrigation systems
  • Healthcare: Remote patient monitoring, connected medical devices
☁️ Cloud ComputingCloud computing provides on-demand access to computing resources (servers, storage, software) over the internet. You pay only for what you use — no need to own hardware.
ModelFull FormProvidesExample
IaaSInfrastructure as a ServiceVirtual machines, storage, networkingAWS EC2, Google Compute Engine
PaaSPlatform as a ServiceDevelopment platform and runtimeGoogle App Engine, Heroku
SaaSSoftware as a ServiceReady-to-use software over internetGmail, Google Docs, Office 365, Zoom

Topic 3 Big Data, Blockchain & Other Trends

▼
📊 Big DataBig Data refers to extremely large, complex datasets that cannot be processed using traditional data management tools. Characterised by the 3 V's.
3 V's of Big Data
  • Volume — enormous quantity of data
  • Velocity — speed of data generation
  • Variety — different formats: text, images, videos, sensor data
Big Data Tools
  • Hadoop — distributed storage & processing
  • Spark — fast in-memory processing
  • Applications: healthcare, finance, social media analytics
🔗 BlockchainBlockchain is a distributed, decentralised digital ledger that records transactions in linked blocks. Once recorded, data cannot be altered — making it tamper-proof and transparent. Used in Bitcoin, NFTs, supply chain management.

Properties of Blockchain

  • Decentralised: No single controlling authority — copies exist across all nodes
  • Immutable: Once data is in a block, it cannot be changed
  • Transparent: All participants can view the transaction history
  • Secure: Protected using cryptographic hashing
TechnologyDescriptionExample
Augmented Reality (AR)Overlays digital content on the real worldPokémon Go, Snapchat filters, IKEA Place
Virtual Reality (VR)Fully immersive computer-generated 3D environmentOculus VR headsets, VR gaming
5G TechnologyUltra-fast wireless network, very low latencySupports autonomous vehicles, smart cities
3D PrintingCreates physical objects from digital models layer by layerMedical implants, prototypes
RoboticsMachines performing tasks autonomouslyIndustrial robots, surgical robots
⭐ Must Remember for CBSE Exam
AI · ML (Supervised / Unsupervised / Reinforcement) · NLP · IoT · Cloud (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS) · Big Data (3 V's: Volume, Velocity, Variety) · Blockchain (Decentralised, Immutable, Transparent, Secure) · AR · VR · 5G

📊 IP Class 12

🐼 IP Class 12 (065) — Ch 1 – Data Handling using Pandas I

🐼
CBSE Class XII · IP Code 065 · Unit 1 · Chapter 1

Data Handling using Pandas – I

Introduction to Pandas, Series and DataFrame — creation, indexing, attributes and basic operations.

Topic 1 Introduction to Pandas

▼
🐼 PandasPandas is an open-source Python library used for data manipulation and analysis. It provides two primary data structures: Series (1D) and DataFrame (2D). Import with: import pandas as pd

Why Pandas?

  • Handles structured data (tabular, CSV, Excel, SQL)
  • Fast and efficient — built on top of NumPy
  • Provides tools for data cleaning, transformation and analysis
  • Supports missing data handling

Topic 2 Series

▼
📊 SeriesA Series is a one-dimensional labelled array that can hold any data type (int, float, string, etc.). It has an index (labels) and values.
📝 Creating a Series
import pandas as pd

# From a list
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30, 40])
print(s)
# 0 10
# 1 20
# 2 30
# 3 40

# With custom index
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30], index=['a', 'b', 'c'])

# From a dictionary
data = {'Math': 95, 'Science': 88, 'English': 76}
s = pd.Series(data)

# From a scalar (same value repeated)
s = pd.Series(5, index=[0, 1, 2, 3])

Series Attributes

AttributeDescriptionExample
s.valuesReturns the data as a NumPy array[10 20 30]
s.indexReturns the index labelsIndex(['a','b','c'])
s.dtypeData type of valuesint64
s.sizeNumber of elements3
s.shapeTuple of dimensions(3,)
s.nameName of the SeriesNone or assigned name
📝 Accessing Series Elements
s = pd.Series([10,20,30], index=['a','b','c'])
print(s['a']) # 10 — label-based
print(s[0]) # 10 — position-based
print(s['a':'c']) # all — slicing with labels (inclusive)
print(s[0:2]) # first two — slicing with positions (exclusive)
⭐ Key Difference
Label slicing (s['a':'c']) is INCLUSIVE of both endpoints. Position slicing (s[0:2]) is EXCLUSIVE of the stop index — same as Python lists.

Topic 3 DataFrame

▼
📋 DataFrameA DataFrame is a two-dimensional, tabular data structure with labelled rows and columns. It is like a spreadsheet or SQL table. Each column is a Series.
📝 Creating a DataFrame
import pandas as pd

# From a dictionary of lists
data = {'Name': ['Riya', 'Amit', 'Sara'],
'Age': [17, 18, 17],
'Marks':[95, 88, 92]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# From a list of dictionaries
data = [{'Name':'Riya','Marks':95},
{'Name':'Amit','Marks':88}]
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# From a CSV file
df = pd.read_csv('students.csv')

DataFrame Attributes

AttributeDescription
df.shapeReturns (rows, columns) as a tuple
df.sizeTotal number of elements (rows × columns)
df.ndimNumber of dimensions (always 2 for DataFrame)
df.columnsList of column names
df.indexRow index labels
df.dtypesData type of each column
df.valuesReturns data as a 2D NumPy array
📝 Accessing DataFrame Data
df['Name'] # select one column → returns Series
df[['Name','Marks']] # select multiple columns → returns DataFrame
df.loc[0] # select row by label
df.loc[0,'Name'] # select specific cell by label
df.iloc[0] # select row by position
df.iloc[0, 1] # select cell by position (row 0, col 1)
df.head(3) # first 3 rows
df.tail(3) # last 3 rows

📊 IP Class 12 (065) — Ch 2 – Data Handling using Pandas II

📊
CBSE Class XII · IP Code 065 · Unit 1 · Chapter 2

Data Handling using Pandas – II

DataFrame operations — adding/deleting rows and columns, import/export, statistical functions and data analysis.

Topic 1 Adding & Deleting Rows / Columns

▼
📝 Adding a Column
df['Grade'] = ['A', 'B', 'A'] # add new column from list
df['Pass'] = df['Marks'] >= 40 # computed column
df['Total'] = df['Math'] + df['Science'] # sum of two columns
📝 Deleting Columns and Rows
# Delete a column
df.drop('Grade', axis=1, inplace=True)
df.drop(columns=['Grade'], inplace=True)

# Delete a row
df.drop(0, axis=0, inplace=True) # drop row with label 0
df.drop([0, 2], axis=0, inplace=True) # drop multiple rows
📝 Adding a Row
# Using loc
df.loc[3] = ['Pooja', 17, 90]

# Using concat
new_row = pd.DataFrame([{'Name':'Sam','Age':18,'Marks':85}])
df = pd.concat([df, new_row], ignore_index=True)
⭐ axis Parameter
axis=0 → operate on ROWS (default) | axis=1 → operate on COLUMNS
inplace=True → modifies the original DataFrame directly (no need to reassign)

Topic 2 Import / Export Data

▼
OperationFunctionExample
Read CSVpd.read_csv()df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
Write CSVdf.to_csv()df.to_csv('output.csv', index=False)
Read Excelpd.read_excel()df = pd.read_excel('data.xlsx')
Write Exceldf.to_excel()df.to_excel('output.xlsx')
Read JSONpd.read_json()df = pd.read_json('data.json')

Topic 3 Statistical Functions & Missing Values

▼
FunctionDescriptionExample
df.describe()Statistical summary of numeric columnscount, mean, std, min, max, quartiles
df['col'].mean()Arithmetic meandf['Marks'].mean()
df['col'].median()Middle valuedf['Marks'].median()
df['col'].mode()Most frequent valuedf['City'].mode()
df['col'].std()Standard deviationdf['Marks'].std()
df['col'].min()/max()Minimum / Maximumdf['Marks'].max()
df['col'].sum()Sum of all valuesdf['Marks'].sum()
df['col'].count()Count of non-null valuesdf['Marks'].count()
df['col'].value_counts()Frequency of each unique valuedf['City'].value_counts()

Handling Missing Values (NaN)

📝 Missing Value Operations
df.isnull() # True where value is NaN
df.isnull().sum() # count of missing values per column
df.notnull() # True where value is NOT NaN

df.dropna() # remove rows with any NaN
df.dropna(axis=1) # remove columns with any NaN

df.fillna(0) # replace NaN with 0
df.fillna(df.mean()) # replace NaN with column mean
df['Marks'].fillna(df['Marks'].median(), inplace=True)

📈 IP Class 12 (065) — Ch 3 – Plotting Data using Matplotlib

📈
CBSE Class XII · IP Code 065 · Unit 1 · Chapter 3

Plotting Data using Matplotlib

Line chart, bar chart, histogram and pie chart — creation, customisation and when to use each chart type.

Topic 1 Introduction to Matplotlib

▼
📈 MatplotlibMatplotlib is a Python plotting library used to create static, animated and interactive visualisations. The most commonly used module is matplotlib.pyplot.
📝 Basic Setup
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Essential functions
plt.show() # display the plot
plt.title('Title') # add a title
plt.xlabel('X Label')# X-axis label
plt.ylabel('Y Label')# Y-axis label
plt.legend() # show legend
plt.savefig('plot.png') # save plot to file

Topic 2 Line Chart

▼
📉 Line ChartUsed to show trends over time or continuous data. Best for showing how a value changes over a period.
📝 Line Chart
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = [2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023]
y = [50, 60, 55, 70, 80]

plt.plot(x, y, color='blue', marker='o', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='Sales')
plt.title('Sales Trend')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Sales (thousands)')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
ParameterDescriptionValues
colorLine colour'red', 'blue', '#ff6600'
markerData point marker'o', 's', '^', '*', '+'
linestyleStyle of line'-', '--', '-.', ':'
linewidthThickness of line1, 2, 3 (float)
labelLegend labelAny string

Topic 3 Bar Chart

▼
📊 Bar ChartUsed to compare discrete categories or groups. Bars can be vertical (bar) or horizontal (barh).
📝 Bar Chart
subjects = ['Math', 'Science', 'English', 'Hindi']
marks = [85, 92, 78, 88]

plt.bar(subjects, marks, color=['blue','green','orange','red'], width=0.5)
plt.title('Subject-wise Marks')
plt.xlabel('Subject')
plt.ylabel('Marks')
plt.show()

# Horizontal bar chart
plt.barh(subjects, marks, color='steelblue')
plt.show()

Topic 4 Histogram & Pie Chart

▼
📊 HistogramShows the frequency distribution of continuous numerical data. Bars touch each other (no gaps) — unlike bar charts.
📝 Histogram
marks = [45,52,60,61,70,72,75,78,80,85,88,90,92,95]

plt.hist(marks, bins=5, color='skyblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Marks Distribution')
plt.xlabel('Marks')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.show()
🥧 Pie ChartShows proportions or percentages of a whole. Best for showing part-to-whole relationships.
📝 Pie Chart
labels = ['Python', 'Java', 'C++', 'Other']
sizes = [45, 30, 15, 10]
explode = (0.1, 0, 0, 0) # explode first slice

plt.pie(sizes, labels=labels, explode=explode,
autopct='%1.1f%%', shadow=True, startangle=90)
plt.title('Programming Language Popularity')
plt.show()
⭐ When to Use Which Chart
Line Chart → trends over time | Bar Chart → compare categories | Histogram → frequency distribution | Pie Chart → parts of a whole

🔧 IP Class 12 (065) — Ch 4 – Functions in SQL

🔧
CBSE Class XII · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 4

Functions in SQL

String functions, numeric functions, date functions and aggregate functions in MySQL — with syntax and examples.

Topic 1 String Functions

▼
FunctionDescriptionExample & Result
UCASE(s) / UPPER(s)Converts string to uppercaseUCASE('hello') → HELLO
LCASE(s) / LOWER(s)Converts string to lowercaseLCASE('HELLO') → hello
LENGTH(s)Returns number of charactersLENGTH('Python') → 6
MID(s, pos, len)Extracts substring from position pos of length lenMID('Python', 2, 3) → yth
SUBSTR(s, pos, len)Same as MID (alias)SUBSTR('Python', 1, 3) → Pyt
LEFT(s, n)Returns leftmost n charactersLEFT('Python', 3) → Pyt
RIGHT(s, n)Returns rightmost n charactersRIGHT('Python', 3) → hon
INSTR(s, sub)Returns position of first occurrence of sub in sINSTR('Python','th') → 3
LTRIM(s)Removes leading spacesLTRIM(' Hi') → Hi
RTRIM(s)Removes trailing spacesRTRIM('Hi ') → Hi
TRIM(s)Removes both leading and trailing spacesTRIM(' Hi ') → Hi
CONCAT(s1, s2, ...)Joins two or more stringsCONCAT('Hello',' ','World') → Hello World
REVERSE(s)Reverses the stringREVERSE('abc') → cba
⭐ SQL String Index starts at 1
In SQL, string positions start from 1 (not 0 like Python). MID('Python', 1, 3) returns 'Pyt'.

Topic 2 Numeric & Date Functions

▼

Numeric Functions

FunctionDescriptionExample & Result
POWER(x, y)Returns x raised to the power yPOWER(2, 8) → 256
ROUND(x, d)Rounds x to d decimal placesROUND(3.5678, 2) → 3.57
MOD(x, y)Returns x modulo y (remainder)MOD(10, 3) → 1
TRUNCATE(x, d)Truncates x to d decimal places (no rounding)TRUNCATE(3.999, 1) → 3.9
SQRT(x)Returns square root of xSQRT(16) → 4
ABS(x)Returns absolute (positive) valueABS(-7) → 7

Date & Time Functions

FunctionDescriptionExample
NOW()Returns current date and time2025-06-28 10:30:00
CURDATE()Returns current date2025-06-28
CURTIME()Returns current time10:30:00
DATE(datetime)Extracts date partDATE(NOW())
MONTH(date)Returns month number (1–12)MONTH('2025-06-28') → 6
YEAR(date)Returns yearYEAR('2025-06-28') → 2025
DAY(date)Returns day of monthDAY('2025-06-28') → 28
DAYNAME(date)Returns name of weekdayDAYNAME('2025-06-28') → Saturday
MONTHNAME(date)Returns name of monthMONTHNAME('2025-06-28') → June
DATEDIFF(d1, d2)Returns d1 − d2 in daysDATEDIFF('2025-12-31','2025-01-01') → 364

🗂️ IP Class 12 (065) — Ch 5 – GROUP BY & Operations on Relations

🗂️
CBSE Class XII · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 5

GROUP BY & Operations on Relations

GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, aggregate functions, and set operations (UNION, INTERSECT, MINUS) in SQL.

Topic 1 GROUP BY & HAVING

▼
🗂️ GROUP BYGROUP BY groups rows that have the same value in specified column(s). It is used with aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN) to produce summary results.
📝 GROUP BY Examples
-- Count students in each city
SELECT City, COUNT(*) AS Total
FROM Students GROUP BY City;

-- Average marks per class
SELECT Class, AVG(Marks) AS AvgMarks
FROM Students GROUP BY Class;

-- Total salary per department
SELECT DeptID, SUM(Salary) AS TotalSalary
FROM Employee GROUP BY DeptID
ORDER BY TotalSalary DESC;
🔍 HAVINGHAVING filters groups AFTER GROUP BY. It is like WHERE but works on aggregated results. WHERE cannot use aggregate functions — HAVING can.
📝 HAVING Clause
-- Cities where average marks > 80
SELECT City, AVG(Marks) AS AvgMarks
FROM Students
GROUP BY City
HAVING AVG(Marks) > 80;

-- Departments with more than 5 employees
SELECT DeptID, COUNT(*) AS EmpCount
FROM Employee
GROUP BY DeptID
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
⭐ WHERE vs HAVING
WHERE — filters individual rows BEFORE grouping — cannot use aggregate functions
HAVING — filters groups AFTER GROUP BY — can use aggregate functions like COUNT, AVG, SUM

Topic 2 ORDER BY & Query Execution Order

▼
📝 ORDER BY
SELECT * FROM Students ORDER BY Marks DESC; -- highest first
SELECT * FROM Students ORDER BY Name ASC; -- alphabetical
SELECT * FROM Students ORDER BY Class, Marks DESC; -- multiple columns

SQL Query Execution Order (Logical)

1. FROM — which table(s)
↓
2. WHERE — filter rows
↓
3. GROUP BY — group rows
↓
4. HAVING — filter groups
↓
5. SELECT — choose columns
↓
6. ORDER BY — sort results
↓
7. LIMIT — restrict rows

Topic 3 Set Operations

▼
OperatorDescriptionExample
UNIONCombines results of two queries — removes duplicatesSELECT Name FROM A UNION SELECT Name FROM B
UNION ALLCombines results — keeps all duplicatesSELECT Name FROM A UNION ALL SELECT Name FROM B
INTERSECTReturns rows common to both queriesSELECT Name FROM A INTERSECT SELECT Name FROM B
MINUS / EXCEPTReturns rows in first query but NOT in secondSELECT Name FROM A MINUS SELECT Name FROM B
⭐ Rules for Set Operations
1. Both queries must have the same number of columns
2. Corresponding columns must have compatible data types
3. Column names in result come from the FIRST query

📊 IP Class 12

🔗 IP Class 12 (065) — Ch 6 – Joins in SQL

🔗
CBSE Class XII · IP Code 065 · Unit 2 · Chapter 6

Joins in SQL

INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL JOIN and CROSS JOIN — concept, syntax and examples for each type.

Topic 1 Introduction to Joins

▼
🔗 JOINA JOIN is used to combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column (usually a foreign key). Joins allow us to retrieve data spread across multiple tables in a single query.
Join TypeReturns
INNER JOINOnly rows where there is a match in BOTH tables
LEFT JOINAll rows from LEFT table + matching rows from right (NULL for non-matches)
RIGHT JOINAll rows from RIGHT table + matching rows from left (NULL for non-matches)
FULL JOINAll rows from BOTH tables (NULL where no match)
CROSS JOINCartesian product — every row of left combined with every row of right

Topic 2 INNER JOIN & LEFT JOIN

▼
📝 INNER JOIN (Most Common)
-- Returns only rows with matching values in BOTH tables
SELECT S.Name, S.Marks, D.DeptName
FROM Students S
INNER JOIN Department D
ON S.DeptID = D.DeptID;

-- Students with their teacher names
SELECT S.Name, T.TeacherName
FROM Student S
INNER JOIN Teacher T ON S.ClassID = T.ClassID;
📝 LEFT JOIN
-- Returns ALL rows from Students, even if no matching Department
SELECT S.Name, D.DeptName
FROM Students S
LEFT JOIN Department D
ON S.DeptID = D.DeptID;
-- Students with no department get NULL in DeptName column

Topic 3 RIGHT JOIN, FULL JOIN & CROSS JOIN

▼
📝 RIGHT JOIN
-- Returns ALL rows from Department, even if no matching Student
SELECT S.Name, D.DeptName
FROM Students S
RIGHT JOIN Department D
ON S.DeptID = D.DeptID;
-- Departments with no students get NULL in Name column
📝 CROSS JOIN
-- Cartesian product — every student with every subject
-- If Students has 5 rows and Subjects has 4 rows → 20 rows result
SELECT S.Name, Sub.SubjectName
FROM Students S
CROSS JOIN Subjects Sub;
⭐ Key Points for Exam
INNER JOIN = only matching rows
LEFT JOIN = all left + matching right (NULLs for non-matches)
RIGHT JOIN = all right + matching left (NULLs for non-matches)
CROSS JOIN = no condition — total rows = rows in A × rows in B

🌐 IP Class 12 (065) — Ch 7 – Introduction to Computer Networks

🌐
CBSE Class XII · IP Code 065 · Unit 3 · Chapter 7

Introduction to Computer Networks

Network types, topologies, protocols, IP addressing, OSI model and Internet services — complete CBSE notes.

Topic 1 Network Basics & Types

▼
🌐 Computer NetworkA computer network is a group of two or more computers connected together to share resources (files, printers, internet) and communicate with each other.
TypeFull FormCoverageExample
PANPersonal Area NetworkFew meters — personal devicesBluetooth, USB connection
LANLocal Area NetworkSingle building or campusSchool lab, office network
MANMetropolitan Area NetworkA city or metropolitan areaCable TV network, city Wi-Fi
WANWide Area NetworkCountries / worldwideInternet, BSNL network

Network Topologies

TopologyDescriptionAdvantageDisadvantage
BusAll nodes connected to a single cable (backbone)Simple, cheapSingle point of failure; limited length
StarAll nodes connected to a central hub/switchEasy to add/remove nodes; fault isolationHub failure affects all; expensive
RingNodes connected in a closed loopEqual access; no collisionsFailure of one node breaks the ring
TreeHierarchical — combination of star and busScalable; easy managementRoot failure affects all
MeshEvery node connected to every other nodeHighly reliable; no single failure pointVery expensive; complex

Topic 2 Network Devices & Transmission Media

▼
DeviceFunction
HubBroadcasts data to ALL connected devices — no intelligence — Layer 1
SwitchSends data only to the TARGET device using MAC address — Layer 2
RouterConnects different networks and routes data packets using IP address — Layer 3
ModemConverts digital signals to analog (modulation) and vice versa (demodulation) for telephone line transmission
GatewayConnects networks with different protocols — translates between them
RepeaterAmplifies and regenerates weak signals to extend network range
NICNetwork Interface Card — connects a computer to a network

Transmission Media

TypeExamplesFeatures
Twisted Pair CableUTP, STPCheap, most common, limited distance
Coaxial CableTV cableBetter shielding, medium speed
Optical FibreInternet backboneVery fast, immune to EMI, expensive
WirelessWi-Fi, Bluetooth, SatelliteNo physical cable, mobility, interference possible

Topic 3 Protocols, IP & Internet Services

▼
📋 ProtocolA protocol is a set of rules that governs how data is transmitted and received over a network. Both sender and receiver must follow the same protocol.
ProtocolFull FormPurpose
HTTPHyperText Transfer ProtocolWeb browsing — transfers web pages
HTTPSHTTP SecureSecure web browsing with encryption (SSL/TLS)
FTPFile Transfer ProtocolTransferring files between computers
SMTPSimple Mail Transfer ProtocolSending emails
POP3Post Office Protocol v3Receiving/downloading emails
DNSDomain Name SystemTranslates domain names to IP addresses (like a phonebook)
TCP/IPTransmission Control Protocol / Internet ProtocolCore internet protocol — ensures reliable data delivery

IP Address

An IP (Internet Protocol) address is a unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network. IPv4: 32-bit address written as 4 octets (e.g. 192.168.1.1). IPv6: 128-bit address to accommodate more devices.
⭐ Internet Services
WWW (World Wide Web) · Email · Video Conferencing · Cloud Computing · Social Media · VoIP (Voice over IP) · Online Banking · E-commerce
