🌍 IP Class 12 (065) — Ch 8 – Societal Impacts

🌍
CBSE Class XII · IP Code 065 · Unit 4 · Chapter 8

Societal Impacts of IT

Digital footprint, cybercrime, cyber safety, intellectual property rights, plagiarism, open-source licensing and e-waste management.

Topic 1 Digital Footprint & Net Etiquette

▼
👣 Digital FootprintA digital footprint is the trail of data you leave behind whenever you use the internet — browsing history, social media posts, online purchases, emails, location data. It can be active (deliberately shared) or passive (collected without awareness).
  • Active footprint: Data you intentionally share — social media posts, filling forms, sending emails
  • Passive footprint: Data collected without your knowledge — websites tracking your IP, browser cookies, location tracking

Net Etiquette (Netiquette)

  • Be respectful and polite in online communication
  • Do not share others' private information without consent
  • Avoid spreading rumours or fake news (misinformation)
  • Use appropriate language in emails and messages
  • Respect intellectual property — do not use others' work without permission
  • Do not troll, bully or harass others online

Topic 2 Cybercrime & Cyber Safety

▼
🚨 CybercrimeCybercrime refers to criminal activities carried out using computers or the internet. It targets individuals, organisations or governments.
CybercrimeDescription
HackingUnauthorised access to a computer system or network
PhishingFraudulent emails or websites that trick users into revealing personal information (passwords, bank details)
Identity TheftStealing someone's personal information to commit fraud
CyberbullyingUsing digital platforms to harass, threaten or intimidate others
MalwareMalicious software — viruses, worms, ransomware, spyware, trojans
RansomwareMalware that encrypts victim's files and demands a ransom for decryption
Denial of Service (DoS)Overloading a server with traffic to make it unavailable
SpamUnsolicited bulk emails sent to large numbers of people

Cyber Safety Measures

  • Use strong, unique passwords for each account
  • Enable two-factor authentication (2FA)
  • Keep software and antivirus updated
  • Do not click suspicious links or attachments
  • Use HTTPS websites for sensitive transactions
  • Regularly back up important data
  • Be cautious about sharing personal information online

Topic 3 Intellectual Property & Licensing

▼
©️ Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)Intellectual Property Rights protect the creations of the mind — inventions, literary works, music, software. The main types are Copyright, Patent and Trademark.
TypeProtectsDuration
CopyrightLiterary, artistic, musical works, softwareLife of author + 60 years (India)
PatentInventions, processes, machines20 years from filing
TrademarkBrand names, logos, symbols (™, ®)10 years (renewable)
📄 PlagiarismPlagiarism is the act of using someone else's work, ideas or words and presenting them as your own without giving credit. It is considered both unethical and illegal.

Software Licensing Types

TypeDescriptionExample
ProprietaryPaid software — cannot be modified or distributed freelyMS Office, Adobe Photoshop
Free SoftwareFreedom to use, study, modify and distribute (not necessarily free of cost)GNU/Linux
Open SourceSource code is publicly available for anyone to view, modify and distributePython, VLC, Firefox, LibreOffice
FreewareFree to use but source code is not availableSkype, Adobe Reader
SharewareFree trial for a limited period; full version requires paymentWinRAR, WinZip

Topic 4 E-Waste & Health Concerns

▼
♻️ E-WasteElectronic waste (e-waste) refers to discarded electronic devices — old computers, phones, batteries, printers. E-waste contains toxic materials like lead, mercury, cadmium that are harmful to health and the environment.

E-Waste Management

  • Reduce: Buy only what you need; extend device lifetime
  • Reuse: Donate working devices to schools or NGOs
  • Recycle: Send e-waste to authorised recycling centres (not regular dustbins)
  • Buy products with eco-friendly certifications (Energy Star, EPEAT)

Health Issues from Excessive Screen Time

IssueCausePrevention
Eye Strain / Computer Vision SyndromeProlonged screen use20-20-20 rule (every 20 min, look 20 feet away for 20 sec)
Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI)Repetitive keyboard/mouse useTake regular breaks; use ergonomic keyboard
Back/Neck PainPoor posture while sittingErgonomic chair; monitor at eye level
TechnoaddictionExcessive and uncontrolled device useSet screen time limits; digital detox
⭐ Must Remember for CBSE
Digital Footprint (Active vs Passive) · Cybercrime types (Phishing, Hacking, Ransomware) · Cyber Safety tips · Copyright / Patent / Trademark · Plagiarism · Software types (Proprietary, Open Source, Freeware, Shareware) · E-Waste management (3 R's) · 20-20-20 rule

🔢 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 1 – Number Systems

🔢
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 1

Number Systems

Rational and irrational numbers, real numbers on the number line, laws of exponents, and rationalisation.

Topic 1 Rational & Irrational Numbers

▼
🔢 Rational NumberA number that can be expressed in the form p/q, where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0. Examples: 3, -5, 1/2, 0.75, 0.333...(repeating)
🔢 Irrational NumberA number that CANNOT be expressed in the form p/q. Its decimal expansion is non-terminating and non-repeating. Examples: √2, √3, π, 0.10110111...

Number System Hierarchy

Real Numbers (R)
↙ ↘
Rational (Q)
Irrational
Rational → Integers → Whole Numbers → Natural Numbers
⭐ Key Fact
Between any two rational numbers, there are infinitely many rational numbers AND infinitely many irrational numbers.

Topic 2 Real Numbers on the Number Line

▼
📏 Real NumberThe collection of all rational and irrational numbers together is called Real Numbers. Every real number can be represented by a unique point on the number line.

Locating √2 on the Number Line (Pythagoras Method)

📝 Steps
1. Draw a number line. Mark point O at 0 and A at 1 (so OA = 1 unit)
2. At A, draw AB perpendicular to OA, with AB = 1 unit
3. Join OB. By Pythagoras theorem: OB = √(1² + 1²) = √2
4. With O as centre and OB as radius, draw an arc cutting the number line at P
5. OP = √2 — this locates √2 on the number line

Decimal Expansion of Real Numbers

TypeDecimal ExpansionExample
RationalTerminating1/4 = 0.25
RationalNon-terminating, repeating1/3 = 0.333...
IrrationalNon-terminating, non-repeating√2 = 1.41421356...

Topic 3 Operations on Real Numbers

▼
⭐ Important Identities
√a × √b = √(ab)
√a / √b = √(a/b)
(√a + √b)(√a − √b) = a − b
(a + √b)(a − √b) = a² − b
(√a + √b)² = a + 2√(ab) + b

Rationalisation of Denominator

✖️ RationalisationThe process of converting an irrational denominator into a rational one by multiplying the numerator and denominator by a suitable irrational number (rationalising factor).
📝 Example: Rationalise 1/√2
1/√2 × √2/√2 = √2/2
📝 Example: Rationalise 1/(√3 + √2)
Multiply by (√3 − √2)/(√3 − √2):
= (√3 − √2) / [(√3)² − (√2)²] = (√3 − √2) / (3 − 2) = √3 − √2

Topic 4 Laws of Exponents for Real Numbers

▼
LawFormula
Product Lawam × an = am+n
Quotient Lawam ÷ an = am−n
Power Law(am)n = amn
Product of Powersam × bm = (ab)m
Negative Exponenta−n = 1/an
Zero Exponenta0 = 1 (a ≠ 0)
Fractional Exponenta1/n = n√a
📝 Example
Simplify: 21/2 × 21/3 = 2(1/2 + 1/3) = 25/6
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

➗ Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 2 – Polynomials

➗
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 2

Polynomials

Polynomials in one variable, zeroes, Remainder Theorem, Factor Theorem and algebraic identities.

Topic 1 Polynomials Basics

▼
➗ PolynomialAn algebraic expression of the form anxn + an-1xn-1 + ... + a1x + a0, where the exponents are non-negative integers and coefficients are real numbers.

Types of Polynomials Based on Number of Terms

TypeNumber of TermsExample
Monomial15x, 3x²
Binomial2x + 2, 3x² − 5
Trinomial3x² + 2x + 1

Types of Polynomials Based on Degree

TypeDegreeExample
Constant Polynomial05
Linear Polynomial12x + 3
Quadratic Polynomial2x² + 2x + 1
Cubic Polynomial3x³ + 2x² + x + 1
⭐ Degree
The highest power of the variable in a polynomial is called its degree.

Topic 2 Zeroes of a Polynomial

▼
0️⃣ Zero of a PolynomialA value of x for which p(x) = 0 is called a zero (or root) of the polynomial p(x).
📝 Example
For p(x) = x − 2, when x = 2: p(2) = 2 − 2 = 0. So x = 2 is a zero of p(x).
⭐ Key Facts
A linear polynomial has exactly 1 zero. A quadratic polynomial has at most 2 zeroes. A polynomial of degree n has at most n zeroes.

Topic 3 Remainder Theorem & Factor Theorem

▼
📐 Remainder TheoremIf a polynomial p(x) is divided by (x − a), the remainder is p(a).
📝 Example
Find the remainder when p(x) = x³ − 3x² + 2x − 1 is divided by (x − 1).
Remainder = p(1) = 1 − 3 + 2 − 1 = −1
🔑 Factor Theorem(x − a) is a factor of p(x) if and only if p(a) = 0.
📝 Example
Check if (x − 1) is a factor of p(x) = x³ − 1.
p(1) = 1 − 1 = 0 → Since p(1) = 0, (x − 1) IS a factor.

Topic 4 Algebraic Identities

▼
⭐ Standard Identities — Must Memorise
(x + y)² = x² + 2xy + y²
(x − y)² = x² − 2xy + y²
x² − y² = (x + y)(x − y)
(x + a)(x + b) = x² + (a+b)x + ab
(x + y + z)² = x² + y² + z² + 2xy + 2yz + 2zx
(x + y)³ = x³ + y³ + 3xy(x + y)
(x − y)³ = x³ − y³ − 3xy(x − y)
x³ + y³ = (x + y)(x² − xy + y²)
x³ − y³ = (x − y)(x² + xy + y²)
x³ + y³ + z³ − 3xyz = (x+y+z)(x²+y²+z²−xy−yz−zx)
📝 Example
Expand (2x + 3y)² using identity (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
= (2x)² + 2(2x)(3y) + (3y)² = 4x² + 12xy + 9y²
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

📍 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry

📍
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 3

Coordinate Geometry

Cartesian plane, coordinates of a point, quadrants and plotting points.

Topic 1 The Cartesian Plane

▼
📍 Cartesian PlaneA plane formed by two perpendicular number lines — the horizontal x-axis and vertical y-axis. Their point of intersection is called the Origin (0,0).

Key Terms

  • X-axis: The horizontal line
  • Y-axis: The vertical line
  • Origin: Point where x-axis and y-axis intersect — coordinates (0,0)
  • Abscissa: The x-coordinate of a point
  • Ordinate: The y-coordinate of a point
  • Coordinates: Written as (x, y) — abscissa first, then ordinate

Topic 2 Quadrants

▼
🧭 QuadrantsThe two axes divide the Cartesian plane into four regions called quadrants.
QuadrantSign of xSign of yExample Point
I (First)++(3, 4)
II (Second)−+(−3, 4)
III (Third)−−(−3, −4)
IV (Fourth)+−(3, −4)
⭐ Special Cases
If x = 0, the point lies on the Y-axis: (0, y)
If y = 0, the point lies on the X-axis: (x, 0)
The Origin is (0, 0)
📝 Example
Plot the point (−2, 3): Move 2 units left of origin on x-axis, then 3 units up — this point lies in Quadrant II.
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

📏 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables

📏
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 4

Linear Equations in Two Variables

Linear equations, solutions, graphical representation, and equations of lines parallel to axes.

Topic 1 Linear Equation in Two Variables

▼
📏 Linear EquationAn equation of the form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b, c are real numbers and a ≠ 0, b ≠ 0, is called a linear equation in two variables.
✅ SolutionA pair of values (x, y) that satisfies the equation is called a solution. A linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions.
📝 Example
For 2x + y = 5: if x = 1, then y = 3, so (1, 3) is a solution. If x = 0, then y = 5, so (0, 5) is also a solution.

Topic 2 Graph of a Linear Equation

▼
📊 GraphEvery solution of a linear equation in two variables is a point on a straight line. The graph of ax + by + c = 0 is always a straight line.
📝 Steps to Draw the Graph
1. Find at least 2 solutions of the equation
2. Plot the points on the Cartesian plane
3. Join the points with a straight line
4. Extend the line in both directions

Lines Parallel to the Axes

EquationRepresents
x = aA line parallel to the Y-axis, at distance 'a' from it
y = bA line parallel to the X-axis, at distance 'b' from it
x = 0The Y-axis itself
y = 0The X-axis itself
⭐ Key Fact
Any point on the X-axis has y-coordinate = 0. Any point on the Y-axis has x-coordinate = 0.
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

📐 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry

📐
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 5

Introduction to Euclid's Geometry

Euclid's definitions, axioms, postulates, and the five postulates of Euclidean geometry.

Topic 1 Euclid's Definitions

▼
📐 Euclid's GeometryEuclid, a Greek mathematician, organised geometric knowledge into a system based on definitions, axioms (postulates) and theorems, presented in his book "Elements".

Important Definitions

  • Point: That which has no part (no length, breadth, or height)
  • Line: Breadthless length
  • Straight Line: A line which lies evenly with the points on itself
  • Surface: That which has length and breadth only
  • Plane Surface: A surface which lies evenly with the straight lines on itself

Topic 2 Axioms & Postulates

▼
⚖️ AxiomA universally accepted truth (used in all branches of mathematics), not requiring proof.
📌 PostulateAssumptions specific to geometry, not requiring proof.

Euclid's Five Postulates

PostulateStatement
1A straight line may be drawn from any one point to any other point
2A terminated line (segment) can be produced indefinitely
3A circle can be drawn with any centre and any radius
4All right angles are equal to one another
5If a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on the same side less than two right angles, the two lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on that side (the Parallel Postulate)

Euclid's Common Notions (Axioms)

  • Things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another
  • If equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal
  • If equals are subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal
  • Things which coincide with one another are equal to one another
  • The whole is greater than the part
  • Things which are double of the same things are equal to one another
⭐ Equivalent of Fifth Postulate
"Two distinct intersecting lines cannot be parallel to the same line" — this is also known as Playfair's Axiom.
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

📐 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 6 – Lines and Angles

📐
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 6

Lines and Angles

Types of angles, pairs of angles, parallel lines and a transversal, and angle sum properties of a triangle.

Topic 1 Basic Terms & Types of Angles

▼
Angle TypeMeasure
Acute Angle0° < angle < 90°
Right Angle= 90°
Obtuse Angle90° < angle < 180°
Straight Angle= 180°
Reflex Angle180° < angle < 360°
Complete Angle= 360°

Pairs of Angles

PairDefinition
Complementary AnglesSum = 90°
Supplementary AnglesSum = 180°
Adjacent AnglesCommon vertex, common arm, no overlap
Linear PairAdjacent angles whose non-common arms form a straight line (sum = 180°)
Vertically Opposite AnglesFormed when two lines intersect; always equal

Topic 2 Parallel Lines and a Transversal

▼
📏 TransversalA line that intersects two or more lines at distinct points.

Angles Formed When a Transversal Cuts Two Parallel Lines

Angle PairRelation (if lines are parallel)
Corresponding AnglesEqual
Alternate Interior AnglesEqual
Alternate Exterior AnglesEqual
Co-interior (Allied) AnglesSupplementary (sum = 180°)
⭐ Converse is Also True
If a transversal cuts two lines such that corresponding angles are equal (or alternate angles are equal, or co-interior angles are supplementary), then the two lines are parallel.

Topic 3 Angle Sum Property of a Triangle

▼
⭐ Angle Sum Property
The sum of all three interior angles of a triangle is always 180°.
⭐ Exterior Angle Property
An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two interior opposite (remote) angles.
📝 Example
In a triangle, two angles are 50° and 60°. Find the third angle.
Third angle = 180° − 50° − 60° = 70°
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

🔺 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 7 – Triangles

🔺
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 7

Triangles

Congruence of triangles, criteria for congruence (SAS, ASA, SSS, RHS), properties of isosceles triangles and inequalities in triangles.

Topic 1 Congruence of Triangles

▼
🔺 Congruent FiguresTwo figures are congruent if they have the exact same shape and size. Congruent triangles are denoted by the symbol ≅.

Criteria for Congruence of Triangles

CriterionFull FormCondition
SASSide-Angle-SideTwo sides and the included angle of one triangle equal to corresponding parts of another
ASAAngle-Side-AngleTwo angles and the included side equal
AASAngle-Angle-SideTwo angles and a non-included side equal
SSSSide-Side-SideAll three sides equal
RHSRight angle-Hypotenuse-SideFor right triangles — hypotenuse and one side equal
⭐ Note
AAA (Angle-Angle-Angle) is NOT a valid congruence criterion — it only proves similarity, not congruence.

Topic 2 Properties of Triangles

▼

Isosceles Triangle Properties

  • Angles opposite to equal sides are equal
  • Sides opposite to equal angles are equal (converse)
  • The altitude from the apex bisects the base and the apex angle

Inequalities in a Triangle

⭐ Triangle Inequality Theorems
1. If two sides of a triangle are unequal, the angle opposite the longer side is larger
2. In any triangle, the side opposite the larger angle is longer
3. The sum of any two sides of a triangle is always greater than the third side
📝 Example
Can a triangle have sides 3 cm, 4 cm, 8 cm?
3 + 4 = 7, which is NOT greater than 8 → No, such a triangle cannot exist.
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

🔲 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals

🔲
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 8

Quadrilaterals

Angle sum property, types of quadrilaterals, properties of a parallelogram, and the Mid-point Theorem.

Topic 1 Angle Sum Property & Types

▼
⭐ Angle Sum Property
The sum of all four interior angles of a quadrilateral is always 360°.
QuadrilateralProperties
ParallelogramBoth pairs of opposite sides parallel and equal
RectangleParallelogram with all angles = 90°
RhombusParallelogram with all sides equal
SquareRectangle + Rhombus — all sides equal, all angles 90°
TrapeziumOne pair of opposite sides parallel
KiteTwo pairs of adjacent sides equal

Topic 2 Properties of a Parallelogram

▼
⭐ Key Properties (Theorems)
1. A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles
2. In a parallelogram, opposite sides are equal
3. In a parallelogram, opposite angles are equal
4. The diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other
5. If each pair of opposite sides of a quadrilateral is equal, it is a parallelogram (converse)
6. If diagonals of a quadrilateral bisect each other, it is a parallelogram (converse)

Topic 3 The Mid-Point Theorem

▼
⭐ Mid-Point Theorem
The line segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and equal to half of it.
⭐ Converse of Mid-Point Theorem
The line drawn through the midpoint of one side of a triangle, parallel to another side, bisects the third side.
📝 Example
In triangle ABC, D and E are midpoints of AB and AC. If BC = 10 cm, then DE = 5 cm (and DE is parallel to BC).
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

⭕ Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 9 – Circles

⭕
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 9

Circles

Circle terminology, angle subtended by a chord, perpendicular from centre to a chord, equal chords, and cyclic quadrilaterals.

Topic 1 Circle Terminology

▼
TermDefinition
CircleThe collection of all points equidistant from a fixed point (centre)
RadiusDistance from centre to any point on the circle
ChordA line segment joining two points on a circle
DiameterA chord passing through the centre — longest chord, = 2 × radius
ArcA piece of a circle between two points
SectorRegion enclosed by two radii and an arc
SegmentRegion enclosed by a chord and an arc
CircumferenceThe perimeter (total length) of a circle

Topic 2 Theorems on Chords

▼
⭐ Key Theorems
1. Equal chords of a circle subtend equal angles at the centre
2. The perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord bisects the chord
3. The line drawn through the centre, bisecting a chord, is perpendicular to it (converse)
4. Equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the centre
5. Chords equidistant from the centre are equal (converse)
6. There is one and only one circle passing through three given non-collinear points

Topic 3 Angle Subtended by an Arc & Cyclic Quadrilaterals

▼
⭐ Angle Theorems
1. The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle
2. Angles in the same segment of a circle are equal
3. The angle in a semicircle is a right angle (90°)
⭕ Cyclic QuadrilateralA quadrilateral whose all four vertices lie on a circle.
⭐ Cyclic Quadrilateral Theorem
The sum of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180° (they are supplementary). Conversely, if a pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral is supplementary, the quadrilateral is cyclic.
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

📊 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 10 – Heron's Formula

📊
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 10

Heron's Formula

Area of a triangle using Heron's formula, and its application to find areas of quadrilaterals.

Topic 1 Heron's Formula

▼
📐 Heron's FormulaUsed to find the area of a triangle when all three side lengths are known (without needing the height).
⭐ Formula
Semi-perimeter: s = (a + b + c) / 2
Area = √[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)]
where a, b, c are the three sides of the triangle
📝 Example
Find the area of a triangle with sides 3 cm, 4 cm, 5 cm.
s = (3+4+5)/2 = 6
Area = √[6(6−3)(6−4)(6−5)] = √[6×3×2×1] = √36 = 6 cm²

Topic 2 Application to Quadrilaterals

▼
🔲 Finding Area of QuadrilateralsTo find the area of a quadrilateral using Heron's formula, draw a diagonal to divide it into two triangles, find the area of each triangle separately, then add them.
📝 Example
For quadrilateral ABCD, draw diagonal AC.
Area of ABCD = Area of △ABC + Area of △ACD
(Calculate each triangle's area using Heron's formula, then sum them)
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

📦 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes

📦
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 11

Surface Areas and Volumes

Surface area and volume formulas for cuboid, cube, cylinder, cone and sphere.

Topic 1 Cuboid & Cube

▼
ShapeTotal Surface Area (TSA)Curved/Lateral Surface Area (LSA)Volume
Cuboid (l, b, h)2(lb + bh + hl)2h(l + b)l × b × h
Cube (side a)6a²4a²a³
⭐ Diagonal
Diagonal of a cuboid = √(l² + b² + h²)  |  Diagonal of a cube = a√3

Topic 2 Cylinder, Cone & Sphere

▼
ShapeCurved Surface AreaTotal Surface AreaVolume
Cylinder (r, h)2πrh2πr(r + h)πr²h
Cone (r, h, l)πrlπr(r + l)(1/3)πr²h
Sphere (r)4πr²4πr²(4/3)πr³
Hemisphere (r)2πr²3πr²(2/3)πr³
⭐ Slant Height of a Cone
l = √(r² + h²) — where r is radius, h is height, l is slant height
📝 Example
Find the volume of a sphere with radius 3 cm. (Use π = 22/7)
Volume = (4/3) × (22/7) × 3³ = (4/3) × (22/7) × 27 = 113.14 cm³ (approx)
📚 Chapters
📐 Maths Class 9
Ch 1 – Number Systems Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 4 – Linear Equations in Two Variables Ch 5 – Introduction to Euclid's Geometry Ch 6 – Lines and Angles Ch 7 – Triangles Ch 8 – Quadrilaterals Ch 9 – Circles Ch 10 – Heron's Formula Ch 11 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 12 – Statistics

📈 Mathematics Class 9 (NCERT) — Ch 12 – Statistics

📈
CBSE Class IX · Mathematics · Chapter 12

Statistics

Collection and presentation of data, graphical representation (bar graph, histogram, frequency polygon), and measures of central tendency.

Topic 1 Collection & Presentation of Data

▼
📊 StatisticsThe branch of mathematics dealing with collection, organisation, analysis and interpretation of data.

Types of Data

  • Primary Data: Data collected by the investigator personally for the first time
  • Secondary Data: Data collected by someone else and used by the investigator

Frequency Distribution Table

Data is organised into a table showing each value (or class interval) and the number of times it occurs (frequency).
Marks (Class Interval)Tally MarksFrequency
0–10||||4
10–20||||| |6
20–30|||3

Topic 2 Graphical Representation of Data

▼
Graph TypeUsed For
Bar GraphComparing discrete categories — bars of uniform width, equal spacing
HistogramContinuous class intervals — bars touch each other (no gaps)
Frequency PolygonDrawn by joining midpoints of the tops of histogram bars
⭐ Key Difference
Histogram has NO gaps between bars (continuous data). Bar graph CAN have gaps (discrete categories).

Topic 3 Measures of Central Tendency

▼
MeasureFormulaDescription
Mean (x̄)Sum of all observations ÷ Number of observationsThe average value
MedianMiddle value when data is arranged in orderIf n is odd: middle term. If n is even: average of two middle terms
ModeValue that occurs most frequentlyCan have one mode, multiple modes, or no mode
📝 Example — Mean
Data: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10
Mean = (2+4+6+8+10)/5 = 30/5 = 6
📝 Example — Median
Data (sorted): 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 (n=5, odd)
Median = middle term = 7

Data (sorted): 2, 4, 6, 8 (n=4, even)
Median = (4+6)/2 = 5
📝 Example — Mode
Data: 2, 3, 3, 4, 3, 5, 6
Mode = 3 (appears most frequently — 3 times)
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🔢 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 1 – Real Numbers

🔢
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 1

Real Numbers

Euclid's Division Lemma, Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, HCF/LCM, and proving irrationality of numbers.

Topic 1 Euclid's Division Lemma

▼
📐 Euclid's Division LemmaFor any two positive integers a and b, there exist unique integers q and r such that a = bq + r, where 0 ≤ r < b.
📝 Example — Find HCF of 135 and 225 using Euclid's Algorithm
225 = 135 × 1 + 90
135 = 90 × 1 + 45
90 = 45 × 2 + 0
Since remainder = 0, HCF(135, 225) = 45
⭐ Euclid's Division Algorithm
A repeated application of Euclid's Division Lemma to find the HCF of two positive integers. Continue dividing until the remainder becomes 0; the last non-zero divisor is the HCF.

Topic 2 Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic

▼
🔢 Fundamental Theorem of ArithmeticEvery composite number can be expressed (factorised) as a product of primes, and this factorisation is unique, apart from the order in which the prime factors occur.
📝 Example
140 = 2 × 2 × 5 × 7 = 2² × 5 × 7

Finding HCF and LCM using Prime Factorisation

⭐ Formulas
HCF = Product of the SMALLEST power of each common prime factor
LCM = Product of the GREATEST power of each prime factor involved
HCF(a, b) × LCM(a, b) = a × b
📝 Example — Find HCF and LCM of 6 and 20
6 = 2 × 3  |  20 = 2² × 5
HCF = 2 (smallest power of common factor 2)
LCM = 2² × 3 × 5 = 60
Check: HCF × LCM = 2 × 60 = 120 = 6 × 20 ✓

Topic 3 Irrationality of Numbers

▼
⭐ Theorem
Let p be a prime number. If p divides a², then p divides a (where a is a positive integer). This is used to prove numbers like √2, √3, √5 are irrational.
📝 Proof that √2 is Irrational (by contradiction)
Assume √2 is rational: √2 = p/q (where p, q are coprime integers, q ≠ 0)
Squaring: 2 = p²/q² → p² = 2q²
So 2 divides p² → 2 divides p. Let p = 2m
Then 4m² = 2q² → q² = 2m² → 2 divides q² → 2 divides q
So both p and q are divisible by 2, contradicting that they are coprime.
Hence, √2 is irrational.
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

➗ Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 2 – Polynomials

➗
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 2

Polynomials

Geometrical meaning of zeroes, relationship between zeroes and coefficients, and division algorithm for polynomials.

Topic 1 Geometrical Meaning of Zeroes

▼
📊 Zeroes of a PolynomialThe zeroes of a polynomial p(x) are the x-coordinates of the points where the graph y = p(x) intersects the x-axis.
Polynomial TypeGraph ShapeMax Number of Zeroes
Linear (degree 1)Straight line1
Quadratic (degree 2)Parabola2
Cubic (degree 3)S-shaped curve3
⭐ Key Fact
A polynomial of degree n can have at most n zeroes.

Topic 2 Relationship Between Zeroes & Coefficients

▼
⭐ For a Quadratic Polynomial ax² + bx + c
If α, β are zeroes:
Sum of zeroes: α + β = −b/a
Product of zeroes: αβ = c/a
📝 Example
Find the zeroes of x² − 3x − 4 and verify the relationship.
x² − 3x − 4 = (x−4)(x+1) → zeroes are 4 and −1
Sum = 4 + (−1) = 3 = −(−3)/1 = −b/a ✓
Product = 4 × (−1) = −4 = (−4)/1 = c/a ✓
⭐ For a Cubic Polynomial ax³ + bx² + cx + d
If α, β, γ are zeroes:
α + β + γ = −b/a
αβ + βγ + γα = c/a
αβγ = −d/a

Topic 3 Division Algorithm for Polynomials

▼
⭐ Division Algorithm
If p(x) and g(x) are polynomials with g(x) ≠ 0, then we can find polynomials q(x) and r(x) such that:
p(x) = g(x) × q(x) + r(x)
where r(x) = 0 OR degree of r(x) < degree of g(x)
(Dividend = Divisor × Quotient + Remainder)
📝 Note
This is used to find other zeroes of a polynomial when one zero is already known — divide the polynomial by the factor corresponding to the known zero.
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

⚖️ Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations

⚖️
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 3

Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

Graphical and algebraic methods (substitution, elimination, cross-multiplication) to solve pairs of linear equations.

Topic 1 Graphical Method

▼
📊 Pair of Linear EquationsTwo linear equations in the same two variables, e.g. a₁x + b₁y + c₁ = 0 and a₂x + b₂y + c₂ = 0.
ConditionGraphNature of Solution
a₁/a₂ ≠ b₁/b₂Intersecting linesUnique solution (consistent)
a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂Parallel linesNo solution (inconsistent)
a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ = c₁/c₂Coincident linesInfinitely many solutions (dependent & consistent)

Topic 2 Algebraic Methods

▼

Substitution Method

📝 Example: Solve x + y = 5 and 2x − y = 4
From eq 1: y = 5 − x
Substitute in eq 2: 2x − (5−x) = 4 → 3x = 9 → x = 3
So y = 5 − 3 = 2 → Solution: (3, 2)

Elimination Method

📝 Example: Solve x + y = 5 and 2x − y = 4
Adding both equations: 3x = 9 → x = 3
Substituting back: 3 + y = 5 → y = 2 → Solution: (3, 2)

Cross-Multiplication Method

⭐ Formula
For a₁x + b₁y + c₁ = 0 and a₂x + b₂y + c₂ = 0:
x / (b₁c₂ − b₂c₁) = y / (c₁a₂ − c₂a₁) = 1 / (a₁b₂ − a₂b₁)
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

📈 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations

📈
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 4

Quadratic Equations

Standard form, solving by factorisation, completing the square, the quadratic formula, and the nature of roots.

Topic 1 Quadratic Equation Basics

▼
📐 Quadratic EquationAn equation of the form ax² + bx + c = 0, where a ≠ 0, is a quadratic equation in variable x.

Solving by Factorisation

📝 Example: Solve x² − 5x + 6 = 0
x² − 5x + 6 = (x−2)(x−3) = 0
So x = 2 or x = 3

Topic 2 Quadratic Formula

▼
⭐ Quadratic Formula
For ax² + bx + c = 0:
x = [−b ± √(b² − 4ac)] / 2a
📝 Example: Solve 2x² − 7x + 3 = 0
a=2, b=−7, c=3
Discriminant = (−7)² − 4(2)(3) = 49 − 24 = 25
x = [7 ± √25]/4 = [7 ± 5]/4
x = 3 or x = 1/2

Topic 3 Nature of Roots

▼
🔍 DiscriminantD = b² − 4ac determines the nature of roots of a quadratic equation.
Discriminant (D)Nature of Roots
D > 0Two distinct real roots
D = 0Two equal real roots (repeated root)
D < 0No real roots
📝 Example
Find nature of roots of 2x² − 4x + 3 = 0
D = 16 − 24 = −8 < 0 → No real roots
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🔁 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions

🔁
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 5

Arithmetic Progressions

nth term of an AP and the sum of the first n terms — formulas and applications.

Topic 1 AP Basics

▼
🔁 Arithmetic Progression (AP)A list of numbers where each term (after the first) is obtained by adding a fixed number (common difference, d) to the preceding term.
📝 Example
2, 5, 8, 11, 14, ... is an AP with first term a = 2 and common difference d = 3
⭐ Common Difference
d = a₂ − a₁ = a₃ − a₂ = ... (same for all consecutive terms)

Topic 2 nth Term & Sum of n Terms

▼
⭐ nth Term Formula
an = a + (n − 1)d
where a = first term, d = common difference, n = term number
⭐ Sum of First n Terms
Sn = n/2 [2a + (n−1)d]
OR if last term (l) is known: Sn = n/2 (a + l)
📝 Example: Find the 10th term of AP 2, 5, 8, ...
a = 2, d = 3, n = 10
a10 = 2 + (10−1)(3) = 2 + 27 = 29
📝 Example: Find the sum of first 10 terms of AP 2, 5, 8, ...
S10 = 10/2 [2(2) + (10−1)(3)] = 5[4 + 27] = 5 × 31 = 155
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🔺 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 6 – Triangles

🔺
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 6

Triangles

Similarity of triangles, criteria for similarity (AA, SAS, SSS), the Basic Proportionality Theorem, and the Pythagoras Theorem.

Topic 1 Similar Triangles

▼
🔺 Similar TrianglesTwo triangles are similar if their corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are in proportion. Denoted by ~.

Criteria for Similarity

CriterionCondition
AA (or AAA)Two pairs of corresponding angles equal
SASOne pair of corresponding angles equal AND sides including them proportional
SSSAll three pairs of corresponding sides proportional

Topic 2 Basic Proportionality Theorem (Thales)

▼
⭐ Basic Proportionality Theorem (BPT)
If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle to intersect the other two sides at distinct points, the other two sides are divided in the same ratio.
⭐ Converse of BPT
If a line divides any two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, the line is parallel to the third side.
📝 Application
In △ABC, if DE ∥ BC where D is on AB and E is on AC, then: AD/DB = AE/EC

Topic 3 Pythagoras Theorem

▼
⭐ Pythagoras Theorem
In a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides:
(Hypotenuse)² = (Base)² + (Height)²
⭐ Converse of Pythagoras Theorem
If the square of one side of a triangle equals the sum of squares of the other two sides, the angle opposite the first side is a right angle.
📝 Example
A right triangle has legs 3 cm and 4 cm. Find the hypotenuse.
Hypotenuse² = 3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25 → Hypotenuse = 5 cm
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

📍 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry

📍
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 7

Coordinate Geometry

Distance formula, section formula, and area of a triangle using coordinates.

Topic 1 Distance Formula

▼
⭐ Distance Formula
Distance between points A(x₁, y₁) and B(x₂, y₂):
AB = √[(x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²]
📝 Example: Find distance between (2,3) and (5,7)
d = √[(5−2)² + (7−3)²] = √[9+16] = √25 = 5
⭐ Distance from Origin
Distance of point (x, y) from origin = √(x² + y²)

Topic 2 Section Formula

▼
⭐ Section Formula
The point P(x, y) that divides the line segment joining A(x₁,y₁) and B(x₂,y₂) in the ratio m:n is:
x = (mx₂ + nx₁)/(m+n), y = (my₂ + ny₁)/(m+n)
⭐ Mid-point Formula (special case, ratio 1:1)
Midpoint = [(x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2]
📝 Example: Find the midpoint of A(2,3) and B(6,7)
Midpoint = [(2+6)/2, (3+7)/2] = (4, 5)

Topic 3 Area of a Triangle

▼
⭐ Area Formula
For vertices A(x₁,y₁), B(x₂,y₂), C(x₃,y₃):
Area = (1/2) |x₁(y₂−y₃) + x₂(y₃−y₁) + x₃(y₁−y₂)|
⭐ Collinearity Check
If the area of the triangle formed by three points is 0, the points are collinear (lie on the same line).
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10

📐 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry

📐
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 8

Introduction to Trigonometry

Trigonometric ratios, values of standard angles, and trigonometric identities.

Topic 1 Trigonometric Ratios

▼
📐 Trigonometric RatiosIn a right-angled triangle, for angle θ:
RatioFormulaReciprocal
sin θOpposite / Hypotenusecosec θ = 1/sin θ
cos θAdjacent / Hypotenusesec θ = 1/cos θ
tan θOpposite / Adjacent = sin θ/cos θcot θ = 1/tan θ
⭐ Memory Trick — SOH CAH TOA
Sin = Opposite/Hypotenuse  |  Cos = Adjacent/Hypotenuse  |  Tan = Opposite/Adjacent

Topic 2 Values of Standard Angles

▼
Angle0°30°45°60°90°
sin01/21/√2√3/21
cos1√3/21/√21/20
tan01/√31√3undefined
cosecundefined2√22/√31
sec12/√3√22undefined
cotundefined√311/√30
⭐ Memory Trick for sin 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°
sin values = √0/2, √1/2, √2/2, √3/2, √4/2 = 0, 1/2, 1/√2, √3/2, 1
cos values are sin values in reverse order

Topic 3 Trigonometric Identities

▼
⭐ Fundamental Identities — Must Memorise
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 → sin²θ = 1 − cos²θ, cos²θ = 1 − sin²θ
1 + tan²θ = sec²θ → tan²θ = sec²θ − 1
1 + cot²θ = cosec²θ → cot²θ = cosec²θ − 1
📝 Example: Prove sin²θ + cos²θ = 1
In right △ with hypotenuse h, opposite p, adjacent b:
sin²θ + cos²θ = (p/h)² + (b/h)² = (p² + b²)/h² = h²/h² = 1 (by Pythagoras)
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🏔️ Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry

🏔️
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 9

Applications of Trigonometry

Heights and distances — angle of elevation, angle of depression, and real-life word problems.

Topic 1 Angle of Elevation & Depression

▼
⬆️ Angle of ElevationThe angle formed between the horizontal line and the line of sight when an observer looks UPWARD at an object.
⬇️ Angle of DepressionThe angle formed between the horizontal line and the line of sight when an observer looks DOWNWARD at an object.
⭐ Key Formula
tan(angle) = Perpendicular / Base = Height / Horizontal Distance

Topic 2 Word Problems

▼
📝 Example 1: Angle of Elevation
A tower is 30m high. From a point on the ground, the angle of elevation of the top is 30°. Find the distance of the point from the base of the tower.
tan 30° = 30/d → 1/√3 = 30/d → d = 30√3 m
📝 Example 2: Angle of Depression
From the top of a 10m high building, the angle of depression of a car is 45°. Find the distance of the car from the building.
tan 45° = 10/d → 1 = 10/d → d = 10 m
⭐ Tips for Solving
1. Draw a neat diagram first
2. Mark all given angles and distances
3. Identify which trig ratio to use
4. The angle of elevation from A to B = angle of depression from B to A
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10

⭕ Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 10 – Circles

⭕
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 10

Circles

Tangent to a circle — properties, number of tangents from an external point, and related theorems.

Topic 1 Tangent to a Circle

▼
⭕ TangentA line that touches a circle at exactly one point (called the point of tangency/contact).
📏 SecantA line that intersects a circle at two points.
⭐ Theorem 1
The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius drawn to the point of contact.
⭐ Theorem 2
The lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.

Topic 2 Number of Tangents & Key Results

▼
Position of PointNumber of Tangents
Inside the circle0 tangents
On the circle1 tangent
Outside the circle2 tangents
⭐ Important Results
1. If PA and PB are tangents from external point P to a circle:
   PA = PB (equal tangent lengths)
   OP bisects angle APB
   OP bisects angle AOB
2. Angle between tangent and radius at point of contact = 90°
📝 Example
From external point P, two tangents PA and PB are drawn to a circle with centre O. If PA = 5 cm, then PB = 5 cm (equal tangent lengths).
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🔵 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles

🔵
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 11

Areas Related to Circles

Perimeter and area of a circle, sector, segment, and combinations of plane figures.

Topic 1 Circle, Sector & Segment Formulas

▼
FigurePerimeter / Arc LengthArea
Circle2πrπr²
Semicircleπr + 2rπr²/2
Sector (angle θ°)Arc length = (θ/360) × 2πr(θ/360) × πr²
Minor Segment—Area of sector − Area of triangle
Major Segment—Area of circle − Area of minor segment
⭐ Key Formulas
Arc length = (θ/360°) × 2πr
Area of sector = (θ/360°) × πr²
Area of minor segment = Area of sector − Area of triangle OAB
Area of triangle OAB = (1/2) × r² × sin θ

Topic 2 Areas of Combinations of Figures

▼
📝 Example 1: Ring (Annulus)
Area of ring = π(R² − r²) where R = outer radius, r = inner radius
📝 Example 2: Square with inscribed circle
Area of shaded region = Area of square − Area of circle
If side = 2r: Shaded area = (2r)² − πr² = 4r² − πr² = r²(4 − π)
⭐ Standard Value of π
Use π = 22/7 unless specified. Use π = 3.14 when given explicitly.
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

📦 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes

📦
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 12

Surface Areas and Volumes

Surface areas and volumes of combinations of solids — cone on cylinder, sphere on cylinder, frustum of a cone, and conversions.

Topic 1 Formulas Recap & Combinations

▼
SolidCSATSAVolume
Cube (a)4a²6a²a³
Cuboid (l,b,h)2h(l+b)2(lb+bh+hl)lbh
Cylinder (r,h)2πrh2πr(r+h)πr²h
Cone (r,h,l)πrlπr(r+l)πr²h/3
Sphere (r)4πr²4πr²4πr³/3
Hemisphere (r)2πr²3πr²2πr³/3
⭐ Slant Height of Cone
l = √(r² + h²)
📝 Combination of Solids
For a solid made by placing a cone on top of a cylinder:
TSA = CSA of cylinder + CSA of cone + Area of base of cylinder
= 2πrh + πrl + πr²
Volume = πr²h (cylinder) + (1/3)πr²h (cone)

Topic 2 Frustum of a Cone

▼
🔺 FrustumWhen a cone is cut by a plane parallel to its base, the portion between the cutting plane and the base is called a frustum.
⭐ Frustum Formulas (R = large radius, r = small radius, h = height)
Slant height: l = √[h² + (R−r)²]
CSA = π(R + r)l
TSA = π[(R + r)l + R² + r²]
Volume = (πh/3)(R² + r² + Rr)
📝 Conversion Problems
When a solid is melted and recast into another shape:
Volume of original solid = Volume of new solid(s)
Example: 1 sphere melted → n small spheres
(4/3)πR³ = n × (4/3)πr³ → R³ = nr³
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

📊 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 13 – Statistics

📊
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 13

Statistics

Mean, median and mode of grouped data, cumulative frequency and the ogive.

Topic 1 Mean of Grouped Data

▼

Three Methods to Find Mean

MethodFormulaWhen to Use
Direct MethodMean = Σfᵢxᵢ / ΣfᵢWhen values are small
Assumed MeanMean = a + (Σfᵢdᵢ / Σfᵢ), where dᵢ = xᵢ − aWhen values are large
Step DeviationMean = a + (Σfᵢuᵢ / Σfᵢ) × h, where uᵢ = (xᵢ − a)/hWhen class sizes are equal
📝 Example — Direct Method
Class: 0-10, 10-20, 20-30 | Frequency: 5, 10, 5
xᵢ (midpoints): 5, 15, 25 | fᵢxᵢ: 25, 150, 125
Mean = (25+150+125)/(5+10+5) = 300/20 = 15

Topic 2 Mode of Grouped Data

▼
📊 Modal ClassThe class interval with the highest frequency is called the modal class.
⭐ Mode Formula
Mode = l + [(f₁ − f₀) / (2f₁ − f₀ − f₂)] × h
where: l = lower limit of modal class
f₁ = frequency of modal class
f₀ = frequency of class preceding modal class
f₂ = frequency of class succeeding modal class
h = class size (width)

Topic 3 Median of Grouped Data & Ogive

▼
⭐ Median Formula
Median = l + [(n/2 − cf) / f] × h
where: l = lower limit of median class
n = total frequency (Σf)
cf = cumulative frequency of class preceding median class
f = frequency of median class
h = class size
📈 Ogive (Cumulative Frequency Curve)A curve obtained by plotting cumulative frequencies against the upper class boundaries. Useful for finding median graphically — the x-coordinate of the point where the ogive intersects the line y = n/2 is the median.
⭐ Empirical Relationship
Mode = 3 × Median − 2 × Mean (approximate relationship)
📚 Chapters
📏 Maths Class 10
Ch 1 – Real Numbers Ch 2 – Polynomials Ch 3 – Pair of Linear Equations Ch 4 – Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Arithmetic Progressions Ch 6 – Triangles Ch 7 – Coordinate Geometry Ch 8 – Introduction to Trigonometry Ch 9 – Applications of Trigonometry Ch 10 – Circles Ch 11 – Areas Related to Circles Ch 12 – Surface Areas and Volumes Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🎲 Mathematics Class 10 — Ch 14 – Probability

🎲
CBSE Class X · Mathematics · Chapter 14

Probability

Classical (theoretical) probability, impossible and sure events, complementary events, and problems on cards, coins and dice.

Topic 1 Probability Basics

▼
🎲 ProbabilityProbability is the measure of the likelihood that an event will occur. It ranges from 0 to 1.
⭐ Classical Probability Formula
P(E) = Number of outcomes favourable to E / Total number of equally likely outcomes

0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1 always
P(impossible event) = 0
P(sure/certain event) = 1
P(E) + P(Ē) = 1, so P(Ē) = 1 − P(E) (complementary events)

Topic 2 Standard Probability Problems

▼

Playing Cards (Total = 52 cards)

TypeCountExamples
Total cards52—
Suits4 (13 each)Hearts ♥, Diamonds ♦, Clubs ♣, Spades ♠
Red cards26Hearts + Diamonds
Black cards26Clubs + Spades
Face cards12Jack, Queen, King (3 per suit × 4 suits)
Ace cards4One per suit

Coins & Dice

ExperimentTotal OutcomesSample Space
1 coin toss2{H, T}
2 coins tossed4{HH, HT, TH, TT}
3 coins tossed8{HHH, HHT, HTH, ...}
1 die thrown6{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}
2 dice thrown36{(1,1),(1,2),...,(6,6)}
📝 Example
A card is drawn from a well-shuffled deck of 52 cards. Find P(King).
P(King) = 4/52 = 1/13
📝 Example
Two coins are tossed simultaneously. Find P(at least one head).
Favourable outcomes = {HH, HT, TH} = 3
P(at least one head) = 3/4

🔣 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 1 – Sets

🔣
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 1

Sets

Sets, types of sets, Venn diagrams, operations on sets and their properties.

Topic 1 Sets & Types

▼
🔣 SetA well-defined collection of distinct objects. Elements of a set are written inside curly braces { }. Membership is denoted by ∈ (belongs to) and ∉ (does not belong to).
TypeDescriptionExample
Empty Set (∅)A set with no elements{ } or ∅
Finite SetSet with countable number of elements{1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
Infinite SetSet with uncountable/unlimited elementsSet of all natural numbers
Singleton SetSet with exactly one element{5}
Equal SetsSets with exactly the same elements{1,2,3} = {3,2,1}
Subset (⊆)Every element of A is also in B: A ⊆ B{1,2} ⊆ {1,2,3}
Power Set P(A)Set of all subsets of A. If |A|=n, |P(A)|=2ⁿA={1,2}: P(A)={∅,{1},{2},{1,2}}
Universal Set (U)Set containing all elements under considerationU = set of all real numbers

Topic 2 Operations on Sets

▼
OperationSymbolMeaningExample
UnionA ∪ BElements in A OR B (or both){1,2}∪{2,3}={1,2,3}
IntersectionA ∩ BElements in BOTH A and B{1,2}∩{2,3}={2}
DifferenceA – BElements in A but NOT in B{1,2,3}–{2,3}={1}
ComplementA'Elements in U but not in AIf U={1..5}, A={1,2}: A'={3,4,5}
⭐ Important Properties
Commutative: A∪B = B∪A, A∩B = B∩A
Associative: (A∪B)∪C = A∪(B∪C)
Distributive: A∩(B∪C) = (A∩B)∪(A∩C)
De Morgan's Laws: (A∪B)' = A'∩B'  |  (A∩B)' = A'∪B'
⭐ Cardinality Formula
n(A∪B) = n(A) + n(B) – n(A∩B)
n(A∪B∪C) = n(A)+n(B)+n(C) – n(A∩B) – n(B∩C) – n(A∩C) + n(A∩B∩C)
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🔗 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 2 – Relations and Functions

🔗
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 2

Relations and Functions

Cartesian product, relations, functions, types of functions, domain and range.

Topic 1 Relations

▼
✖️ Cartesian ProductA × B = {(a, b) : a ∈ A, b ∈ B}. If n(A) = p and n(B) = q, then n(A×B) = pq.
🔗 RelationA relation R from set A to set B is a subset of A × B. i.e., R ⊆ A × B.
⭐ Domain, Range & Codomain
Domain of R = set of all first elements (x values)
Range of R = set of all second elements (y values) that are actually used
Codomain = the set B (all possible y values)

Topic 2 Functions & Their Types

▼
⚙️ FunctionA relation f from A to B is a function if every element of A has exactly one image in B. Written as f: A → B.
TypeDescription
One-One (Injective)Different inputs have different outputs: f(x₁)=f(x₂) ⇒ x₁=x₂
Onto (Surjective)Every element of codomain B has at least one pre-image in A (Range = Codomain)
BijectiveBoth one-one AND onto
Many-OneTwo or more inputs have the same output
IntoRange ⊂ Codomain (at least one element of B has no pre-image)

Standard Real Functions

FunctionDomainRange
Identity f(x) = xℝℝ
Constant f(x) = cℝ{c}
Modulus f(x) = |x|ℝ[0, ∞)
Signumℝ{-1, 0, 1}
Greatest Integer f(x) = [x]ℝℤ (integers)
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

📐 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions

📐
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 3

Trigonometric Functions

Radian measure, trig functions of any angle, signs in quadrants, graphs, identities and compound angle formulas.

Topic 1 Angle Measurement & Signs in Quadrants

▼
⭐ Radian ↔ Degree Conversion
π radians = 180°  |  1 radian = 180°/π ≈ 57.27°  |  1° = π/180 radians

Signs of Trig Functions in Quadrants (ASTC Rule)

QuadrantPositive Functions
I (0° to 90°)All (sin, cos, tan)
II (90° to 180°)Sin only
III (180° to 270°)Tan only
IV (270° to 360°)Cos only
⭐ Memory: Add Sugar To Coffee (ASTC)
All → Sin → Tan → Cos (quadrants I → II → III → IV)

Topic 2 Trigonometric Identities

▼
⭐ Compound Angle Formulas
sin(A+B) = sinA cosB + cosA sinB
sin(A−B) = sinA cosB − cosA sinB
cos(A+B) = cosA cosB − sinA sinB
cos(A−B) = cosA cosB + sinA sinB
tan(A+B) = (tanA + tanB)/(1 − tanA tanB)
tan(A−B) = (tanA − tanB)/(1 + tanA tanB)
⭐ Double Angle Formulas
sin 2A = 2 sinA cosA
cos 2A = cos²A − sin²A = 1 − 2sin²A = 2cos²A − 1
tan 2A = 2tanA / (1 − tan²A)
⭐ Sum to Product Formulas
sinC + sinD = 2 sin((C+D)/2) cos((C−D)/2)
sinC − sinD = 2 cos((C+D)/2) sin((C−D)/2)
cosC + cosD = 2 cos((C+D)/2) cos((C−D)/2)
cosC − cosD = −2 sin((C+D)/2) sin((C−D)/2)
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🔢 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations

🔢
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 4

Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations

Complex numbers, algebra, modulus, argument, polar form and quadratic equations with complex roots.

Topic 1 Complex Numbers

▼
🔢 Complex NumberA number of the form z = a + ib, where a, b ∈ ℝ and i = √(−1). Here a = Real part (Re z), b = Imaginary part (Im z).
⭐ Powers of i
i¹ = i  |  i² = −1  |  i³ = −i  |  i⁴ = 1  |  Pattern repeats every 4 powers

Algebra of Complex Numbers

OperationFormula
Addition(a+ib)+(c+id) = (a+c) + i(b+d)
Subtraction(a+ib)−(c+id) = (a−c) + i(b−d)
Multiplication(a+ib)(c+id) = (ac−bd) + i(ad+bc)
Division(a+ib)/(c+id): multiply by conjugate (c−id)/(c−id)
⭐ Modulus, Conjugate & Argument
Conjugate of z = a+ib is z̄ = a−ib
Modulus |z| = √(a² + b²)
Argument (angle) θ = tan⁻¹(b/a)
Polar form: z = r(cosθ + i sinθ) where r = |z|
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

⚖️ Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities

⚖️
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 5

Linear Inequalities

Algebraic and graphical solutions of linear inequalities in one and two variables.

Topic 1 Inequalities in One Variable

▼
⚖️ Linear InequalityAn inequality involving a linear expression. Types: ax + b > 0, ax + b < 0, ax + b ≥ 0, ax + b ≤ 0.
⭐ Rules for Solving Inequalities
1. Adding/subtracting same number on both sides: inequality sign unchanged
2. Multiplying/dividing by positive number: inequality sign unchanged
3. Multiplying/dividing by NEGATIVE number: inequality sign REVERSES
4. Solution represented on number line or in interval notation
📝 Example
Solve: 3x − 5 < 10
3x < 15 → x < 5
Solution: x ∈ (−∞, 5)

Interval Notation

NotationMeaning
(a, b)Open interval: a < x < b (endpoints excluded)
[a, b]Closed interval: a ≤ x ≤ b (endpoints included)
(a, b]Half-open: a < x ≤ b
(−∞, b)x < b (no left boundary)

Topic 2 Inequalities in Two Variables

▼
📊 Graphical SolutionThe graph of ax + by > c is a half-plane. The boundary line ax + by = c divides the plane into two half-planes. Substitute a point (usually origin) to determine which half-plane satisfies the inequality.
📝 Steps
1. Draw the boundary line ax + by = c (solid line if ≥ or ≤, dashed if > or <)
2. Test origin (0,0) in the inequality
3. If satisfied, shade the origin side; otherwise shade the other side
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🔀 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations

🔀
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 6

Permutations and Combinations

Fundamental principle of counting, factorials, permutations ⁿPᵣ and combinations ⁿCᵣ with formulas and applications.

Topic 1 Permutations

▼
⭐ Fundamental Principle of Counting
If event A can occur in m ways and event B can occur in n ways, then A followed by B can occur in m × n ways.
🔀 PermutationAn arrangement of objects where ORDER matters. ⁿPᵣ = number of ways to arrange r objects from n distinct objects.
⭐ Permutation Formulas
ⁿPᵣ = n! / (n−r)!  |  ⁿPₙ = n! (all n objects)
n! = n × (n−1) × (n−2) × ... × 2 × 1  |  0! = 1
📝 Example
In how many ways can 3 letters be arranged from A, B, C, D, E?
⁵P₃ = 5!/(5−3)! = 5!/2! = 120/2 = 60 ways

Topic 2 Combinations

▼
🃏 CombinationA selection of objects where ORDER does NOT matter. ⁿCᵣ = number of ways to choose r objects from n distinct objects.
⭐ Combination Formulas
ⁿCᵣ = n! / [r!(n−r)!]
ⁿCᵣ = ⁿCₙ₋ᵣ  |  ⁿC₀ = ⁿCₙ = 1  |  ⁿC₁ = n
ⁿCᵣ + ⁿCᵣ₋₁ = ⁿ⁺¹Cᵣ (Pascal's identity)
📝 Example
In how many ways can a committee of 3 be chosen from 8 people?
⁸C₃ = 8!/(3! × 5!) = (8×7×6)/(3×2×1) = 56 ways
⭐ Permutation vs Combination
Order matters → Permutation (ⁿPᵣ)  |  Order does NOT matter → Combination (ⁿCᵣ)
ⁿPᵣ = r! × ⁿCᵣ
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

📊 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem

📊
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 7

Binomial Theorem

Binomial theorem for positive integral index, general term, middle term and Pascal's triangle.

Topic 1 Binomial Theorem

▼
⭐ Binomial Theorem
(a + b)ⁿ = Σ ⁿCᵣ × aⁿ⁻ʳ × bʳ (r = 0 to n)
= ⁿC₀aⁿ + ⁿC₁aⁿ⁻¹b + ⁿC₂aⁿ⁻²b² + ... + ⁿCₙbⁿ
⭐ General Term (Tᵣ₊₁)
Tᵣ₊₁ = ⁿCᵣ × aⁿ⁻ʳ × bʳ
⭐ Middle Term
If n is even: middle term = T(n/2 + 1)
If n is odd: two middle terms = T((n+1)/2) and T((n+3)/2)
📝 Example: Find general term of (x + 2)⁸
Tᵣ₊₁ = ⁸Cᵣ × x⁸⁻ʳ × 2ʳ
⭐ Key Results
Number of terms in (a+b)ⁿ = n+1
Sum of all coefficients: put a=b=1 → 2ⁿ
Sum of odd-position coefficients = Sum of even-position coefficients = 2ⁿ⁻¹
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🔁 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 8 – Sequences and Series

🔁
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 8

Sequences and Series

AP, GP, their nth terms and sums, arithmetic mean, geometric mean and special series.

Topic 1 Arithmetic Progression (AP)

▼
⭐ AP Formulas
nth term: aₙ = a + (n−1)d
Sum of n terms: Sₙ = n/2[2a + (n−1)d] = n/2(a + l)
Arithmetic Mean (AM) between a and b: AM = (a+b)/2

Topic 2 Geometric Progression (GP)

▼
🔁 GPA sequence where each term is obtained by multiplying the previous term by a fixed ratio r (common ratio).
⭐ GP Formulas
nth term: aₙ = arⁿ⁻¹
Sum of n terms: Sₙ = a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1) if r≠1; Sₙ = na if r=1
Sum of infinite GP (|r|<1): S∞ = a/(1−r)
Geometric Mean (GM) between a and b: GM = √(ab)
📝 Example: Sum of infinite GP 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + ...
a=1, r=1/2 → S∞ = 1/(1−1/2) = 1/(1/2) = 2

Topic 3 Special Series

▼
⭐ Standard Summation Formulas
Σn = n(n+1)/2
Σn² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6
Σn³ = [n(n+1)/2]²
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11

📏 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 9 – Straight Lines

📏
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 9

Straight Lines

Slope, forms of equations of a line, angle between lines, distance from a point to a line.

Topic 1 Slope & Forms of Lines

▼
⭐ Slope of a Line
m = tanθ = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁)
Parallel lines: m₁ = m₂  |  Perpendicular lines: m₁ × m₂ = −1
FormEquation
Slope-intercepty = mx + c
Point-slopey − y₁ = m(x − x₁)
Two-point(y−y₁)/(y₂−y₁) = (x−x₁)/(x₂−x₁)
Intercept formx/a + y/b = 1
Normal formx cosω + y sinω = p
General formax + by + c = 0

Topic 2 Distance Formulas

▼
⭐ Distance from Point to Line
Distance from point (x₁, y₁) to line ax + by + c = 0:
d = |ax₁ + by₁ + c| / √(a² + b²)
⭐ Distance between Parallel Lines
Between ax + by + c₁ = 0 and ax + by + c₂ = 0:
d = |c₁ − c₂| / √(a² + b²)
⭐ Angle between Two Lines
tan θ = |(m₁ − m₂)/(1 + m₁m₂)|

∑ Maths Class 11

⭕ Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 10 – Conic Sections

⭕
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 10

Conic Sections

Circle, parabola, ellipse and hyperbola — standard equations and key properties.

Topic 1 Circle & Parabola

▼
ConicStandard EquationKey Features
Circle(x−h)² + (y−k)² = r²Centre (h,k), radius r. General: x²+y²+2gx+2fy+c=0, centre (−g,−f), r=√(g²+f²−c)
Parabola (opens right)y² = 4axFocus (a,0), Directrix x=−a, Vertex (0,0), Axis: x-axis
Parabola (opens left)y² = −4axFocus (−a,0), Directrix x=a
Parabola (opens up)x² = 4ayFocus (0,a), Directrix y=−a

Topic 2 Ellipse & Hyperbola

▼
ConicEquationKey Features
Ellipse (a>b)x²/a² + y²/b² = 1c²=a²−b², e=c/a<1, Foci (±c,0), Vertices (±a,0), Latus rectum = 2b²/a
Hyperbolax²/a² − y²/b² = 1c²=a²+b², e=c/a>1, Foci (±c,0), Asymptotes: y=±(b/a)x

∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

📍 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry

📍
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 11

Introduction to Three Dimensional Geometry

Coordinate axes in 3D, octants, distance formula and section formula in three dimensions.

Topic 1 3D Coordinate System

▼
📍 3D CoordinatesA point in space is represented as P(x, y, z). Three mutually perpendicular axes (X, Y, Z) divide space into 8 regions called octants.
Axis/PlaneCoordinates
X-axisy = 0, z = 0
Y-axisx = 0, z = 0
Z-axisx = 0, y = 0
XY-planez = 0
YZ-planex = 0
XZ-planey = 0
⭐ 3D Formulas
Distance PQ = √[(x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)² + (z₂−z₁)²]
Section formula (m:n): ((mx₂+nx₁)/(m+n), (my₂+ny₁)/(m+n), (mz₂+nz₁)/(m+n))
Midpoint: ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2, (z₁+z₂)/2)
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

∞ Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives

∞
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 12

Limits and Derivatives

Concept of limits, standard limit formulas, derivatives from first principles and differentiation rules.

Topic 1 Limits

▼
∞ Limitlim(x→a) f(x) = L means f(x) approaches L as x approaches a (but x ≠ a).
⭐ Standard Limits
lim(x→0) sinx/x = 1
lim(x→0) tanx/x = 1
lim(x→0) (1−cosx)/x = 0
lim(x→a) (xⁿ−aⁿ)/(x−a) = naⁿ⁻¹
lim(x→0) (eˣ−1)/x = 1
lim(x→0) log(1+x)/x = 1
⭐ Limit Algebra Rules
lim[f(x) ± g(x)] = lim f(x) ± lim g(x)
lim[f(x) · g(x)] = lim f(x) · lim g(x)
lim[f(x)/g(x)] = lim f(x) / lim g(x), provided lim g(x) ≠ 0

Topic 2 Derivatives

▼
d/dx DerivativeThe derivative of f(x) at x is: f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) − f(x)] / h
⭐ Standard Derivatives
d/dx(xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹  |  d/dx(c) = 0
d/dx(sin x) = cos x  |  d/dx(cos x) = −sin x
d/dx(tan x) = sec²x  |  d/dx(cot x) = −cosec²x
d/dx(sec x) = sec x tan x  |  d/dx(cosec x) = −cosec x cot x
⭐ Rules of Differentiation
Sum/Difference: d/dx[f±g] = f'±g'
Product Rule: d/dx[f·g] = f'g + fg'
Quotient Rule: d/dx[f/g] = (f'g − fg')/g²
Scalar multiple: d/dx[cf] = c·f'
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

📈 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 13 – Statistics

📊
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 13

Statistics

Measures of dispersion — range, mean deviation, variance and standard deviation.

Topic 1 Measures of Dispersion

▼
MeasureFormulaDescription
RangeMaximum − MinimumSimplest measure, affected by extreme values
Mean Deviation (MD)MD = Σ|xᵢ − x̄| / n (ungrouped) or Σfᵢ|xᵢ−x̄|/Σfᵢ (grouped)Average of absolute deviations from mean/median
Variance (σ²)σ² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/n (ungrouped)Average of squared deviations from mean
Standard Deviation (σ)σ = √VarianceSquare root of variance — same unit as data
⭐ Shortcut for Variance
σ² = (Σxᵢ²/n) − x̄² = Mean of squares − Square of mean
⭐ Coefficient of Variation (CV)
CV = (σ/x̄) × 100 — used to compare variability of two datasets. Lower CV = more consistent.
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 11
Ch 1 – Sets Ch 2 – Relations and Functions Ch 3 – Trigonometric Functions Ch 4 – Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations Ch 5 – Linear Inequalities Ch 6 – Permutations and Combinations Ch 7 – Binomial Theorem Ch 8 – Sequences and Series Ch 9 – Straight Lines Ch 10 – Conic Sections Ch 11 – Introduction to 3D Geometry Ch 12 – Limits and Derivatives Ch 13 – Statistics Ch 14 – Probability

🎲 Mathematics Class 11 — Ch 14 – Probability

🎲
CBSE Class XI · Mathematics · Chapter 14

Probability

Random experiments, sample space, events, axiomatic probability and its properties.

Topic 1 Basic Concepts

▼
TermDefinition
Random ExperimentAn experiment whose outcome cannot be predicted with certainty
Sample Space (S)Set of all possible outcomes of an experiment
EventAny subset of the sample space
Sure EventThe entire sample space S — always occurs
Impossible EventEmpty set ∅ — never occurs
Complementary EventA' = all outcomes in S not in A

Topic 2 Axiomatic Probability & Properties

▼
⭐ Axioms of Probability
1. P(E) ≥ 0 for every event E
2. P(S) = 1
3. If A and B are mutually exclusive: P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B)
⭐ Important Theorems
P(∅) = 0  |  P(A') = 1 − P(A)
P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A∩B) (Addition theorem)
If A⊆B then P(A) ≤ P(B)
0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1 always
📝 Example
If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.5, P(A∩B) = 0.2, find P(A∪B).
P(A∪B) = 0.4 + 0.5 − 0.2 = 0.7
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part A
Unit 1: Communication Skills-III Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-III Unit 3: ICT Skills-III Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-III Unit 5: Fundamentals of Java

🖥️ IT Class 11 (402) — Unit 1: Communication Skills-III

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part A · Unit 1

🗣️ Communication Skills-III

Advanced communication — reading comprehension, writing skills, verbal and non-verbal communication.

Topic 1 Reading & Comprehension Skills

▼
📖 Reading ComprehensionThe ability to read a passage and understand its meaning, draw inferences and answer questions based on it. A key skill for professional communication.
⭐ Types of Reading
Skimming: Reading quickly to get the general idea/main topic of a text
Scanning: Reading quickly to find specific information (date, name, number)
Intensive Reading: Reading carefully to understand every detail — for study
Extensive Reading: Reading for pleasure/general knowledge at own pace
⭐ Tips to Improve Comprehension
Read the questions first, then read the passage
Identify the main idea of each paragraph
Look for keywords that match questions
Infer meaning of unknown words from context
Avoid bringing outside knowledge — answer only from the passage

Topic 2 Writing Skills

▼
⭐ Formal Letter Writing
Structure: Sender's address → Date → Receiver's address → Salutation → Subject → Body → Closing → Signature
Salutation: Dear Sir/Madam (if name unknown) or Dear Mr./Ms. [Name]
Closing: Yours faithfully (if name unknown) / Yours sincerely (if name known)
Language: Formal, polite, clear and concise
⭐ Email Writing
To: Recipient's email address
Subject: Brief, specific and informative subject line
Salutation: Dear [Name] / Hello [Name]
Body: Short paragraphs — purpose, details, request/action needed
Closing: Regards / Warm regards / Best wishes
Signature: Full name, designation, contact details
TypePurposeTone
Formal LetterOfficial communication — complaints, applications, enquiriesFormal, polite
EmailQuick professional communicationSemi-formal to formal
ReportPresent findings/data to managementFormal, objective
NoticeInform people about an event/meetingFormal, brief

Topic 3 Verbal & Non-Verbal Communication

▼
🗣️ Verbal CommunicationCommunication using words — spoken (face-to-face, phone, presentation) or written (letter, email, report).
🤝 Non-Verbal CommunicationCommunication without words — body language, facial expressions, gestures, eye contact, posture, tone of voice, appearance.
⭐ Components of Non-Verbal Communication
Body Language (Kinesics): Posture, gestures, facial expressions
Eye Contact: Shows confidence, interest and sincerity
Proxemics: Use of personal space — intimate (0-45cm), personal (45cm-1.2m), social (1.2-3.6m)
Paralanguage: Tone, pitch, speed, volume of voice
Appearance: Dress, grooming — creates first impression
⭐ Barriers to Communication
Physical: Noise, distance, poor internet connection
Language: Jargon, technical words, different mother tongue
Psychological: Stress, prejudice, lack of attention
Cultural: Different customs and gestures mean different things
Overcome by: Using simple language, active listening, feedback, appropriate channel

∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

🔄 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions

🔄
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 2

Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Domain, range, principal values, properties and important results of inverse trig functions.

Topic 1 Domain & Range of Inverse Trig Functions

▼
FunctionDomainRange (Principal Value)
sin⁻¹x[−1, 1][−π/2, π/2]
cos⁻¹x[−1, 1][0, π]
tan⁻¹xℝ(−π/2, π/2)
cot⁻¹xℝ(0, π)
sec⁻¹xℝ − (−1, 1)[0, π] − {π/2}
cosec⁻¹xℝ − (−1, 1)[−π/2, π/2] − {0}

Topic 2 Properties of Inverse Trig Functions

▼
⭐ Key Properties — Must Memorise
sin⁻¹(sin x) = x for x ∈ [−π/2, π/2]
cos⁻¹(cos x) = x for x ∈ [0, π]
sin⁻¹(−x) = −sin⁻¹x  |  cos⁻¹(−x) = π − cos⁻¹x
tan⁻¹(−x) = −tan⁻¹x  |  cot⁻¹(−x) = π − cot⁻¹x
sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x = π/2 for x ∈ [−1, 1]
tan⁻¹x + cot⁻¹x = π/2 for x ∈ ℝ
sec⁻¹x + cosec⁻¹x = π/2 for |x| ≥ 1
⭐ Addition Formulas
tan⁻¹x + tan⁻¹y = tan⁻¹[(x+y)/(1−xy)] if xy < 1
tan⁻¹x − tan⁻¹y = tan⁻¹[(x−y)/(1+xy)] if xy > −1
2tan⁻¹x = tan⁻¹[2x/(1−x²)] = sin⁻¹[2x/(1+x²)] = cos⁻¹[(1−x²)/(1+x²)]

∑ Maths Class 12
