🔢 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 3 – Matrices

🔢
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 3

Matrices

Types of matrices, matrix operations, transpose, symmetric, skew-symmetric matrices and elementary operations.

Topic 1 Types of Matrices

▼
TypeDescription
Row MatrixOnly 1 row — order 1×n
Column MatrixOnly 1 column — order m×1
Square MatrixNumber of rows = Number of columns (m = n)
Diagonal MatrixSquare matrix where all non-diagonal elements are 0
Scalar MatrixDiagonal matrix with all diagonal elements equal
Identity Matrix (I)Diagonal matrix with all diagonal elements = 1
Zero Matrix (O)All elements are 0
Symmetric MatrixA = Aᵀ (transpose equals original)
Skew-SymmetricAᵀ = −A (all diagonal elements must be 0)
⭐ Key Results
Every square matrix can be expressed as: A = ½(A + Aᵀ) + ½(A − Aᵀ)
where ½(A + Aᵀ) is symmetric and ½(A − Aᵀ) is skew-symmetric.

Topic 2 Matrix Operations

▼
⭐ Matrix Multiplication
For AB to exist: columns of A = rows of B
If A is m×n and B is n×p, then AB is m×p
Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative: AB ≠ BA (generally)
Matrix multiplication is associative: (AB)C = A(BC)
Distributive: A(B+C) = AB + AC
⭐ Transpose Properties
(Aᵀ)ᵀ = A  |  (A+B)ᵀ = Aᵀ + Bᵀ  |  (kA)ᵀ = kAᵀ  |  (AB)ᵀ = BᵀAᵀ
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

📊 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 4 – Determinants

📊
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 4

Determinants

Determinant of 2×2 and 3×3 matrices, properties, cofactors, adjoint, inverse of a matrix, and solving equations using Cramer's rule.

Topic 1 Determinant Formulas

▼
⭐ Determinant of 2×2 Matrix
|A| = |a b; c d| = ad − bc
⭐ Determinant of 3×3 Matrix (Expansion along Row 1)
|A| = a₁₁(a₂₂a₃₃ − a₂₃a₃₂) − a₁₂(a₂₁a₃₃ − a₂₃a₃₁) + a₁₃(a₂₁a₃₂ − a₂₂a₃₁)

Key Properties of Determinants

  • |Aᵀ| = |A|
  • If any two rows (or columns) are identical, |A| = 0
  • If any row/column is all zeros, |A| = 0
  • |kA| = kⁿ|A| where n is the order of matrix
  • |AB| = |A||B|
  • Multiplying any row/column by k multiplies the determinant by k

Topic 2 Inverse of a Matrix & Applications

▼
⭐ Adjoint & Inverse Formulas
Adjoint (adj A) = Transpose of cofactor matrix
A⁻¹ = adj(A) / |A|   (exists only if |A| ≠ 0)
A · adj(A) = adj(A) · A = |A| · I
|A⁻¹| = 1/|A|
⭐ Solving AX = B using Matrix Method
If |A| ≠ 0 (non-singular): X = A⁻¹B
If |A| = 0 (singular):
  — If (adj A)B ≠ 0: No solution
  — If (adj A)B = 0: Infinitely many solutions
📝 Area of Triangle using Determinant
Area = ½|x₁(y₂−y₃) + x₂(y₃−y₁) + x₃(y₁−y₂)|
= ½ |det of 3×3 matrix with rows (x₁,y₁,1), (x₂,y₂,1), (x₃,y₃,1)|
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

∞ Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability

∞
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 5

Continuity and Differentiability

Continuity, differentiability, chain rule, derivatives of implicit/parametric functions, logarithmic differentiation and higher order derivatives.

Topic 1 Continuity & Differentiability

▼
∞ Continuityf(x) is continuous at x = a if: lim(x→a⁻)f(x) = lim(x→a⁺)f(x) = f(a)
d/dx Differentiabilityf(x) is differentiable at x = a if LHD = RHD = finite value. Differentiability ⟹ Continuity (but NOT vice versa).
⭐ Standard Derivatives
d/dx(xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹  |  d/dx(eˣ) = eˣ  |  d/dx(aˣ) = aˣ log a
d/dx(log x) = 1/x  |  d/dx(log_a x) = 1/(x log a)
d/dx(sin x) = cos x  |  d/dx(cos x) = −sin x
d/dx(sin⁻¹x) = 1/√(1−x²)  |  d/dx(cos⁻¹x) = −1/√(1−x²)
d/dx(tan⁻¹x) = 1/(1+x²)  |  d/dx(cot⁻¹x) = −1/(1+x²)
d/dx(sec⁻¹x) = 1/(x√(x²−1))  |  d/dx(cosec⁻¹x) = −1/(x√(x²−1))

Topic 2 Chain Rule & Special Methods

▼
⭐ Chain Rule
If y = f(g(x)), then dy/dx = f'(g(x)) · g'(x)
⭐ Logarithmic Differentiation
Used when y = [f(x)]^g(x). Take log on both sides first, then differentiate.
log y = g(x) · log f(x) → (1/y)(dy/dx) = g'(x)·log f(x) + g(x)·f'(x)/f(x)
⭐ Parametric Differentiation
If x = f(t) and y = g(t), then dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt)
⭐ Rolle's Theorem
If f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b) and f(a)=f(b), then ∃ c ∈ (a,b) such that f'(c) = 0.
⭐ Mean Value Theorem (Lagrange)
If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then ∃ c ∈ (a,b) such that f'(c) = [f(b)−f(a)]/(b−a)
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

📈 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives

📈
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 6

Application of Derivatives

Rate of change, increasing/decreasing functions, tangent/normal, maxima and minima.

Topic 1 Rate of Change & Increasing/Decreasing

▼
⭐ Rate of Change
dy/dx gives the rate of change of y with respect to x. If y = f(t) and x = g(t), then dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt)
⭐ Increasing/Decreasing Functions
f is strictly increasing on (a,b) if f'(x) > 0 for all x ∈ (a,b)
f is strictly decreasing on (a,b) if f'(x) < 0 for all x ∈ (a,b)
f is constant on (a,b) if f'(x) = 0 for all x ∈ (a,b)

Topic 2 Tangent, Normal & Maxima/Minima

▼
⭐ Tangent & Normal
Slope of tangent at (x₁,y₁) = dy/dx|(x₁,y₁)
Equation of tangent: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁)
Slope of normal = −1/m (perpendicular to tangent)
Equation of normal: y − y₁ = (−1/m)(x − x₁)
⭐ Maxima & Minima — First Derivative Test
Find f'(x) = 0 → critical points
If f'(x) changes + to − at c → Local Maximum
If f'(x) changes − to + at c → Local Minimum
If f'(x) doesn't change sign → Point of Inflection
⭐ Second Derivative Test
If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) < 0 → Local Maximum at c
If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0 → Local Minimum at c
If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) = 0 → Test fails, use first derivative test
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

∫ Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 7 – Integrals

∫
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 7

Integrals

Indefinite and definite integrals — standard formulas, methods of integration, and properties of definite integrals.

Topic 1 Standard Integral Formulas

▼
∫ f(x) dxResult
∫ xⁿ dxxⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C   (n ≠ −1)
∫ 1/x dxlog|x| + C
∫ eˣ dxeˣ + C
∫ aˣ dxaˣ/log a + C
∫ sin x dx−cos x + C
∫ cos x dxsin x + C
∫ sec²x dxtan x + C
∫ cosec²x dx−cot x + C
∫ sec x tan x dxsec x + C
∫ 1/√(1−x²) dxsin⁻¹x + C
∫ 1/(1+x²) dxtan⁻¹x + C
∫ 1/√(x²−a²) dxlog|x + √(x²−a²)| + C

Topic 2 Methods of Integration

▼
⭐ Integration by Substitution
Put u = g(x), then du = g'(x)dx. Replace and integrate with respect to u.
⭐ Integration by Parts (ILATE rule)
∫u·v dx = u·∫v dx − ∫(u'·∫v dx) dx
ILATE order for choosing u: Inverse trig → Logarithm → Algebraic → Trigonometric → Exponential
⭐ Properties of Definite Integrals
∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = −∫ᵦₐ f(x)dx
∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = ∫ₐᶜ f(x)dx + ∫ᶜᵇ f(x)dx
∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = ∫ₐᵇ f(a+b−x)dx
∫₀ᵃ f(x)dx = ∫₀ᵃ f(a−x)dx
If f(x) is even: ∫₋ₐᵃ f(x)dx = 2∫₀ᵃ f(x)dx
If f(x) is odd: ∫₋ₐᵃ f(x)dx = 0

∑ Maths Class 12

📐 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 8 – Application of Integrals

📐
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 8

Application of Integrals

Area under curves, area between two curves using definite integrals.

Topic 1 Area Under a Curve

▼
⭐ Area Formulas
Area between curve y=f(x), x-axis and lines x=a, x=b:
A = ∫ₐᵇ |f(x)| dx

Area between curve x=g(y), y-axis and lines y=c, y=d:
A = ∫ᶜᵈ |g(y)| dy

Area between two curves y=f(x) and y=g(x) from x=a to x=b:
A = ∫ₐᵇ |f(x) − g(x)| dx (where f(x) ≥ g(x))
📝 Standard Areas
Area of circle x²+y²=r²: πr²
Area of ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1: πab
Area under parabola y²=4ax from x=0 to x=h: (4/3)a·h^(3/2)... use integration
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

📉 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 9 – Differential Equations

dy/dx
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 9

Differential Equations

Order, degree, formation, variable separable, homogeneous and linear differential equations.

Topic 1 Basic Concepts

▼
dy/dx OrderThe ORDER is the highest derivative present. The DEGREE is the power of the highest order derivative (after clearing fractions/radicals).
📝 Example
d²y/dx² + (dy/dx)³ + y = 0 → Order = 2, Degree = 1
⭐ Methods of Solving
1. Variable Separable: dy/dx = f(x)·g(y) → dy/g(y) = f(x)dx → Integrate both sides

2. Homogeneous: dy/dx = F(y/x) → Put y = vx, so dy/dx = v + x(dv/dx) → Separate variables

3. Linear (dy/dx + Py = Q):
Integrating Factor (IF) = e^(∫P dx)
Solution: y × IF = ∫Q × IF dx + C
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

➡️ Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 10 – Vector Algebra

➡️
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 10

Vector Algebra

Vectors, types, operations, dot product, cross product and their geometrical interpretations.

Topic 1 Vector Basics & Operations

▼
➡️ VectorA quantity having both magnitude and direction. Denoted by →a or bold a. Position vector of point P(x,y,z) = xî + yĵ + zk̂
⭐ Magnitude & Unit Vector
|→a| = √(x² + y² + z²)
Unit vector: â = →a / |→a|
Direction cosines: l = x/|→a|, m = y/|→a|, n = z/|→a| and l²+m²+n² = 1

Topic 2 Dot Product & Cross Product

▼
⭐ Scalar (Dot) Product
→a · →b = |→a||→b|cosθ = a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + a₃b₃
→a · →b = 0 ⟹ vectors are perpendicular (if neither is zero)
→a · →a = |→a|²
Projection of →a on →b = (→a · →b)/|→b|
⭐ Vector (Cross) Product
→a × →b = |→a||→b|sinθ n̂
|→a × →b| = area of parallelogram with sides →a and →b
Area of triangle = ½|→a × →b|
→a × →b = 0 ⟹ vectors are parallel (if neither is zero)
→a × →b = −(→b × →a) — anti-commutative
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

📍 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry

📍
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 11

Three Dimensional Geometry

Direction cosines, equation of a line, angle between lines, equation of a plane and distance formulas in 3D.

Topic 1 Lines in 3D

▼
⭐ Equation of a Line
Vector form: →r = →a + λ→b
Cartesian form: (x−x₁)/l = (y−y₁)/m = (z−z₁)/n
where (x₁,y₁,z₁) is a point and (l,m,n) are direction ratios
⭐ Angle between Two Lines
cos θ = |l₁l₂ + m₁m₂ + n₁n₂| / (√(l₁²+m₁²+n₁²) × √(l₂²+m₂²+n₂²))
Lines are perpendicular if l₁l₂ + m₁m₂ + n₁n₂ = 0
Lines are parallel if l₁/l₂ = m₁/m₂ = n₁/n₂
⭐ Shortest Distance between Two Skew Lines
d = |(→a₂ − →a₁) · (→b₁ × →b₂)| / |→b₁ × →b₂|

Topic 2 Planes in 3D

▼
⭐ Equation of a Plane
General: ax + by + cz + d = 0 (where a,b,c are direction ratios of normal)
Normal form: lx + my + nz = p (where l,m,n are direction cosines)
Intercept form: x/a + y/b + z/c = 1
Vector form: →r · n̂ = d
⭐ Distance Formulas
Distance from point (x₁,y₁,z₁) to plane ax+by+cz+d=0:
Distance = |ax₁+by₁+cz₁+d| / √(a²+b²+c²)

Angle between plane and line: sin θ = |al+bm+cn| / (√(a²+b²+c²) · √(l²+m²+n²))
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

📊 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 12 – Linear Programming

📊
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 12

Linear Programming

Linear programming problems — formulation, graphical method, corner point method and types of solutions.

Topic 1 LPP Basics & Graphical Method

▼
📊 Linear Programming Problem (LPP)Optimising (maximising or minimising) a linear objective function Z = ax + by subject to linear constraints (inequalities).

Key Terms

TermMeaning
Objective FunctionThe function Z = ax + by to be maximised or minimised
ConstraintsThe linear inequalities that restrict the values of x and y
Non-negativity Restrictionsx ≥ 0, y ≥ 0 (usually)
Feasible RegionThe common region satisfying all constraints
Corner Points (Vertices)The vertices of the feasible region polygon
⭐ Corner Point Method
1. Graph all constraints and find the feasible region
2. Find all corner points (vertices) of the feasible region
3. Calculate Z = ax + by at each corner point
4. The maximum or minimum value of Z is the answer

Types of Solutions:
Bounded region → Both max and min exist
Unbounded region → Max may not exist (for maximisation) or min may not exist
No feasible region → No solution
📚 Chapters
∑ Maths Class 12
Ch 1 – Relations and Functions Ch 2 – Inverse Trigonometric Functions Ch 3 – Matrices Ch 4 – Determinants Ch 5 – Continuity and Differentiability Ch 6 – Application of Derivatives Ch 7 – Integrals Ch 8 – Application of Integrals Ch 9 – Differential Equations Ch 10 – Vector Algebra Ch 11 – Three Dimensional Geometry Ch 12 – Linear Programming Ch 13 – Probability

🎲 Mathematics Class 12 — Ch 13 – Probability

🎲
CBSE Class XII · Mathematics · Chapter 13

Probability

Conditional probability, multiplication theorem, independent events, Bayes' theorem and random variables.

Topic 1 Conditional Probability & Multiplication Theorem

▼
⭐ Conditional Probability
P(A|B) = P(A∩B) / P(B), provided P(B) ≠ 0
"Probability of A given B has already occurred"
⭐ Multiplication Theorem
P(A∩B) = P(A) · P(B|A) = P(B) · P(A|B)
If A and B are INDEPENDENT: P(A∩B) = P(A) · P(B)
A and B are independent if P(A|B) = P(A) i.e. occurrence of B doesn't affect A

Topic 2 Total Probability & Bayes' Theorem

▼
⭐ Theorem of Total Probability
If E₁, E₂, ..., Eₙ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events, and A is any event:
P(A) = P(E₁)P(A|E₁) + P(E₂)P(A|E₂) + ... + P(Eₙ)P(A|Eₙ)
⭐ Bayes' Theorem
P(Eᵢ|A) = P(Eᵢ) · P(A|Eᵢ) / Σ[P(Eⱼ) · P(A|Eⱼ)]
"Given that A has occurred, what is the probability that it was caused by Eᵢ?"

Topic 3 Random Variables & Probability Distribution

▼
🎲 Random VariableA variable X whose values are determined by outcomes of a random experiment. Probability distribution: list of all values of X with their probabilities.
⭐ Mean & Variance of a Random Variable
Mean (Expected Value): μ = E(X) = Σxᵢ · P(xᵢ)
Variance: Var(X) = E(X²) − [E(X)]² = Σxᵢ² · P(xᵢ) − μ²
Standard Deviation = √Var(X)
⭐ Binomial Distribution
P(X=r) = ⁿCᵣ · pʳ · qⁿ⁻ʳ where q = 1−p
Mean = np  |  Variance = npq  |  SD = √(npq)
n = number of trials, p = probability of success, r = number of successes
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 9 Part B
Unit 1: Introduction to IT–ITeS Industry Unit 2: Data Entry & Keyboarding Skills Unit 3: Digital Documentation (LibreOffice Writer) Unit 4: Electronic Spreadsheet (LibreOffice Calc) Unit 5: Digital Presentation (LibreOffice Impress)

💻 IT Class 9 (402) — Unit 1: Introduction to IT–ITeS Industry

💻
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part B · Unit 1

Introduction to IT–ITeS Industry

Overview of IT and ITeS sector, career opportunities, work environment and professional skills.

Topic 1 IT and ITeS Industry Overview

▼
💻 IT (Information Technology)IT refers to the use of computers, networks, software and other electronic devices to store, process and transmit information. Examples: software development, hardware manufacturing, networking.
📞 ITeS (IT Enabled Services)ITeS refers to services that are delivered using IT infrastructure. Also called BPO (Business Process Outsourcing). Examples: call centres, data entry, medical transcription, online education.
IT SectorITeS Sector
Software developmentCall centre / BPO
Hardware manufacturingData entry services
Networking & cybersecurityMedical transcription
Web developmentOnline tutoring / e-learning
Embedded systemsFinance & accounting outsourcing
⭐ Why India is a Hub for IT–ITeS
Large English-speaking workforce  |  Cost-effective services
Skilled technical graduates  |  Favourable government policies
Strong telecom infrastructure  |  Time zone advantage (serves USA, Europe)

Topic 2 Career Opportunities in IT–ITeS

▼
Job RoleKey Responsibility
Data Entry OperatorEnter and manage data in computers accurately
BPO ExecutiveHandle customer queries via phone/email/chat
Technical SupportHelp customers resolve technical problems
Web DesignerDesign and build websites
Network AdministratorManage and maintain computer networks
Software DeveloperWrite and maintain software programs
Cyber Security AnalystProtect systems from cyber attacks

Topic 3 Work Environment & Professional Skills

▼
⭐ Types of Work Environment
Office-based: Fixed location, fixed hours, team environment
Remote/Work from Home: Work from any location using internet
Hybrid: Mix of office and remote work
On-site: Work at client's location
⭐ Essential Professional Skills for IT–ITeS
Technical Skills: Computer proficiency, typing speed, software knowledge
Communication: Clear spoken and written English
Time Management: Meeting deadlines, prioritising tasks
Teamwork: Collaborating with colleagues
Problem Solving: Logical thinking to resolve issues
Adaptability: Learning new tools and technologies quickly
⭐ NASSCOMNational Association of Software and Service Companies — the apex body for the IT–ITeS industry in India. It promotes the growth and development of the IT sector.
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 9 Part B
Unit 1: Introduction to IT–ITeS Industry Unit 2: Data Entry & Keyboarding Skills Unit 3: Digital Documentation (LibreOffice Writer) Unit 4: Electronic Spreadsheet (LibreOffice Calc) Unit 5: Digital Presentation (LibreOffice Impress)

⌨️ IT Class 9 (402) — Unit 2: Data Entry & Keyboarding Skills

⌨️
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part B · Unit 2

Data Entry & Keyboarding Skills

Keyboard layout, touch typing, data entry techniques, accuracy and speed improvement.

Topic 1 Keyboard Layout & Touch Typing

▼
⌨️ QWERTY KeyboardThe standard keyboard layout used worldwide. Named after the first 6 letters in the top row. Has 104 keys including function keys, numeric keypad and special keys.
⭐ Home Row Keys
Home row (middle row) keys: A S D F G H J K L ;
Left hand fingers rest on: A(pinky) S(ring) D(middle) F(index) — thumb on Space
Right hand fingers rest on: J(index) K(middle) L(ring) ;(pinky) — thumb on Space
F and J keys have a raised bump to find home position without looking
⭐ Touch TypingTyping without looking at the keyboard. Uses muscle memory. Each finger is responsible for specific keys. Increases speed and reduces errors.
FingerLeft Hand KeysRight Hand Keys
IndexF, G, R, T, V, B, 4, 5J, H, U, Y, M, N, 6, 7
MiddleD, E, C, 3K, I, ,, 8
RingS, W, X, 2L, O, ., 9
PinkyA, Q, Z, 1, Tab, Caps; P / 0, Enter, Shift
ThumbSpace Bar

Topic 2 Data Entry & Accuracy

▼
📋 Data EntryThe process of entering information (text, numbers) into a computer system using input devices like keyboard, scanner or OCR. Common in BPO, banking, hospitals and government offices.
⭐ Measuring Typing Performance
WPM (Words Per Minute): Number of words typed in one minute (1 word = 5 keystrokes)
KPH (Keystrokes Per Hour): Total keystrokes typed in one hour
Accuracy %: (Correct keystrokes / Total keystrokes) × 100
Industry standard: 30–40 WPM with 95%+ accuracy for data entry jobs
⭐ Tips to Improve Speed & Accuracy
Maintain correct posture — sit upright, feet flat on floor
Keep wrists slightly raised above keyboard
Eyes on source document, not on keyboard
Practice daily with typing tutor software
Start slow, focus on accuracy first — speed comes naturally
Take regular breaks to avoid RSI (Repetitive Strain Injury)
KeyFunction
BackspaceDelete character to the left of cursor
DeleteDelete character to the right of cursor
Enter/ReturnMove to new line / confirm command
TabMove cursor forward by fixed spaces
Caps LockToggle uppercase letters on/off
ShiftType uppercase or special characters
Ctrl+C / Ctrl+VCopy / Paste
Ctrl+ZUndo last action
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 9 Part B
Unit 1: Introduction to IT–ITeS Industry Unit 2: Data Entry & Keyboarding Skills Unit 3: Digital Documentation (LibreOffice Writer) Unit 4: Electronic Spreadsheet (LibreOffice Calc) Unit 5: Digital Presentation (LibreOffice Impress)

📝 IT Class 9 (402) — Unit 3: Digital Documentation (LibreOffice Writer)

📝
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part B · Unit 3

Digital Documentation — LibreOffice Writer

Creating, formatting and managing documents using LibreOffice Writer — the free alternative to MS Word.

Topic 1 Introduction to LibreOffice Writer

▼
📝 LibreOffice WriterA free, open-source word processing application. Part of LibreOffice suite. Used to create letters, reports, resumes, notices and other documents. Saves files in ODF (.odt) format but also supports .docx.
⭐ LibreOffice Writer — Interface Components
Title Bar: Shows document name and application name
Menu Bar: File, Edit, View, Insert, Format, Tools, Table, Window, Help
Standard Toolbar: New, Open, Save, Print, Undo, Redo buttons
Formatting Toolbar: Font, size, bold, italic, underline, alignment
Ruler: Shows margins, tab stops and indentation
Status Bar: Page number, word count, language at the bottom
Scroll Bars: Vertical and horizontal for navigating document
OperationShortcut Key
New DocumentCtrl + N
Open DocumentCtrl + O
SaveCtrl + S
Save AsCtrl + Shift + S
PrintCtrl + P
UndoCtrl + Z
RedoCtrl + Y
Select AllCtrl + A
Find & ReplaceCtrl + H

Topic 2 Text Formatting & Page Setup

▼
⭐ Character Formatting
Font: Style of text — Times New Roman, Arial, Calibri etc.
Font Size: Size measured in points (pt). Default is 12pt
Bold (Ctrl+B): Makes text thicker/darker
Italic (Ctrl+I): Makes text slanted
Underline (Ctrl+U): Adds a line below text
Font Colour: Change text colour
Highlight: Background colour behind text
⭐ Paragraph Formatting
Alignment: Left (Ctrl+L), Centre (Ctrl+E), Right (Ctrl+R), Justify (Ctrl+J)
Line Spacing: Single, 1.5, Double — Format → Paragraph
Indentation: Increase/decrease indent buttons or ruler
Bullets & Numbering: Format → Bullets and Numbering
⭐ Page Setup (Format → Page Style)
Paper Size: A4 (21cm × 29.7cm) is standard
Orientation: Portrait (vertical) or Landscape (horizontal)
Margins: Top, Bottom, Left, Right — typically 2.5 cm each
Header & Footer: Insert → Header and Footer

Topic 3 Tables, Images & Other Features

▼
⭐ Inserting a Table
Table → Insert Table → Set rows and columns
To add row: Tab at end of last cell or right-click → Insert Rows
To merge cells: Select cells → Table → Merge Cells
To split cells: Table → Split Cells
⭐ Inserting Images & Objects
Insert → Image → From File (browse and select image)
Right-click image → Position and Size to resize precisely
Wrapping: how text flows around image — None, Page Wrap, Optimal Page Wrap
FeatureMenu Path
Spell CheckTools → Spelling (F7)
Word CountTools → Word Count
Mail MergeTools → Mail Merge Wizard
Page NumbersInsert → Header/Footer → Edit → Insert Field → Page Number
Track ChangesEdit → Track Changes → Record Changes
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 9 Part B
Unit 1: Introduction to IT–ITeS Industry Unit 2: Data Entry & Keyboarding Skills Unit 3: Digital Documentation (LibreOffice Writer) Unit 4: Electronic Spreadsheet (LibreOffice Calc) Unit 5: Digital Presentation (LibreOffice Impress)

📊 IT Class 9 (402) — Unit 4: Electronic Spreadsheet (LibreOffice Calc)

📊
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part B · Unit 4

Electronic Spreadsheet — LibreOffice Calc

Spreadsheet basics, cell operations, formulas, functions and charts using LibreOffice Calc.

Topic 1 Introduction to LibreOffice Calc

▼
📊 LibreOffice CalcA free open-source spreadsheet application. Used for calculations, data analysis and charts. Part of LibreOffice suite. Saves in ODS (.ods) format, also supports .xlsx.
⭐ Spreadsheet Basic Terms
Workbook: The entire file/document (contains multiple sheets)
Worksheet/Sheet: A single spreadsheet tab (Sheet1, Sheet2...)
Row: Horizontal arrangement — numbered 1, 2, 3... (max 1,048,576)
Column: Vertical arrangement — lettered A, B, C... (max 1024)
Cell: Intersection of a row and column — named by column+row e.g. A1, B3
Cell Range: Group of cells — e.g. A1:D5 (A1 to D5)
Active Cell: Currently selected cell (shown by thick border)
Name Box: Shows address of active cell (top-left)
Formula Bar: Shows content/formula of active cell

Topic 2 Formulas & Functions

▼
⭐ FormulaA formula always starts with = sign. It performs calculations using cell references, numbers and operators. Example: =A1+B1, =C3*D3, =(A1+B1)/2
⭐ Arithmetic Operators in Calc
+ (Addition)  |  − (Subtraction)  |  * (Multiplication)  |  / (Division)  |  ^ (Power/Exponent)
FunctionSyntaxUse
SUM=SUM(A1:A10)Add all values in range
AVERAGE=AVERAGE(A1:A10)Calculate mean/average
MAX=MAX(A1:A10)Find largest value
MIN=MIN(A1:A10)Find smallest value
COUNT=COUNT(A1:A10)Count cells with numbers
COUNTA=COUNTA(A1:A10)Count non-empty cells
IF=IF(condition, true, false)Conditional formula
ROUND=ROUND(A1, 2)Round to given decimal places
⭐ Cell Referencing
Relative Reference (A1): Changes when formula is copied — default type
Absolute Reference ($A$1): Does NOT change when formula is copied — use $ sign
Mixed Reference ($A1 or A$1): Only row or only column is fixed

Topic 3 Formatting & Charts

▼
⭐ Cell Formatting
Right-click → Format Cells (Ctrl+1)
Number tab: Number, Currency, Date, Time, Percentage, Text formats
Alignment tab: Horizontal, vertical, wrap text, merge cells
Font tab: Font family, size, style, colour
Border tab: Add borders around cells
Background tab: Fill colour for cells
⭐ Creating a Chart
1. Select the data range
2. Insert → Chart
3. Choose chart type: Bar, Column, Line, Pie, Area etc.
4. Set data range, labels and title
5. Click Finish — chart is embedded in sheet
Chart TypeBest Used For
Column / BarComparing values across categories
LineShowing trends over time
PieShowing parts of a whole (percentage)
AreaShowing cumulative totals over time
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 9 Part B
Unit 1: Introduction to IT–ITeS Industry Unit 2: Data Entry & Keyboarding Skills Unit 3: Digital Documentation (LibreOffice Writer) Unit 4: Electronic Spreadsheet (LibreOffice Calc) Unit 5: Digital Presentation (LibreOffice Impress)

🖥️ IT Class 9 (402) — Unit 5: Digital Presentation (LibreOffice Impress)

🖥️
CBSE Class IX · IT Code 402 · Part B · Unit 5

Digital Presentation — LibreOffice Impress

Creating and delivering effective presentations using LibreOffice Impress — slides, transitions, animations and delivery tips.

Topic 1 Introduction to LibreOffice Impress

▼
🖥️ LibreOffice ImpressA free open-source presentation software. Part of LibreOffice suite. Used to create slideshows for teaching, business meetings, seminars. Saves as ODP (.odp) format, also supports .pptx.
⭐ Impress — Interface Components
Slide Panel (left): Thumbnail view of all slides
Editing Area (centre): Main area to edit current slide
Task Panel (right): Layouts, slide design, animations panel
View Bar: Normal, Outline, Slide Sorter, Slide Show views
Status Bar: Slide number, layout info at bottom
TermMeaning
SlideA single page/screen in a presentation
Slide LayoutPredefined arrangement of placeholders on a slide
PlaceholderDotted box for entering text, images or other content
Slide MasterTemplate slide that controls design of all slides
Slide ShowFull-screen display of the presentation

Topic 2 Creating & Formatting Slides

▼
⭐ Adding & Managing Slides
Insert new slide: Right-click in Slide Panel → Insert Slide, or Ctrl+M
Delete slide: Right-click → Delete Slide
Duplicate slide: Right-click → Duplicate Slide
Move slide: Drag and drop in Slide Panel
Hide slide: Right-click → Hide Slide (won't show in presentation)
⭐ Inserting Objects
Text Box: Insert → Text Box → draw on slide
Image: Insert → Image → From File
Table: Insert → Table
Chart: Insert → Chart
Video/Audio: Insert → Audio or Video
Shapes: View → Toolbars → Drawing
⭐ Slide Design & Background
Slide → Slide Properties → Background tab
Can set: Colour, Gradient, Hatching or Bitmap as background
Slide Master: View → Master → Slide Master — change design for all slides at once

Topic 3 Transitions, Animations & Slide Show

▼
⭐ Slide TransitionThe visual effect that appears when moving from one slide to the next. Set via: Slide → Slide Transition. Options: Fade, Dissolve, Wipe, Fly In etc. Can set speed (Slow/Medium/Fast) and sound.
⭐ AnimationMovement effect applied to individual objects (text, image) ON a slide. Set via: View → Animation panel. Animation can be set to appear On Click, With Previous, or After Previous.
FeatureApplies ToMenu Path
TransitionWhole slide (entry effect)Slide → Slide Transition
AnimationIndividual objects on slideView → Animation
⭐ Running a Slide Show
Start from beginning: F5 or Slide Show → Start from First Slide
Start from current slide: Shift+F5
Next slide: Click / Right arrow / Page Down / Space
Previous slide: Left arrow / Page Up / Backspace
End slide show: Escape key
Slide Show Settings: Slide Show → Slide Show Settings — set loop, timing
⭐ Good Presentation Tips
Use 6×6 rule: max 6 lines per slide, max 6 words per line
Use large readable fonts (minimum 24pt for body, 36pt+ for headings)
Use contrasting colours — dark background + light text or vice versa
Include relevant images and avoid clutter
Use consistent theme throughout
Practice delivery — maintain eye contact, speak clearly
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 10 Part B
Ch 1 – Introduction to Styles Ch 2 – Working with Images Ch 3 – Advanced Features of Writer Ch 4 – Scenarios & Goal Seek Ch 5 – Using Macros in Spreadsheet Ch 8 – Introduction to DBMS Ch 9 – Starting with LibreOffice Base

🎨 IT Class 10 (402) — Ch 1 – Introduction to Styles

CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part B · Chapter 1

🎨 Introduction to Styles

Using paragraph and character styles in LibreOffice Writer for consistent document formatting.

Topic 1 What are Styles?

▼
🎨 StyleA style is a set of formatting options saved under a name. Instead of applying font, size, colour, spacing individually each time, you apply the style once and all formatting is applied together.
⭐ Types of Styles in Writer
Paragraph Styles: Apply to entire paragraphs — Heading 1, Heading 2, Body Text, Default Style
Character Styles: Apply to selected characters within a paragraph — Bold, Emphasis, Strong Emphasis
Page Styles: Control page layout — margins, header/footer, orientation
Frame Styles: Formatting for text frames and image frames
List Styles: Formatting for bulleted and numbered lists
⭐ Applying Styles
Method 1: Click in paragraph → Select style from Style drop-down in Formatting Toolbar
Method 2: Format → Styles → open Styles and Formatting panel (F11)
Method 3: Format → Styles → Apply a Style

Topic 2 Creating & Modifying Styles

▼
⭐ Creating a New Style
1. Format → Styles → Manage Styles (F11)
2. Click New Style from Selection button (bottom of panel)
3. Give a name → OK
OR: Right-click existing style → New
⭐ Modifying an Existing Style
1. Right-click on style name in Styles panel → Edit Style
2. Change font, size, colour, spacing etc.
3. Click OK — all text using that style updates automatically
This is the power of styles — change once, update everywhere!
Style NameDefault Use
Default Paragraph StyleBody text of the document
Heading 1Main chapter/section headings
Heading 2Sub-section headings
Heading 3Sub-sub-section headings
TitleDocument title at top of page
SubtitleDocument subtitle below title
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 10 Part B
Ch 1 – Introduction to Styles Ch 2 – Working with Images Ch 3 – Advanced Features of Writer Ch 4 – Scenarios & Goal Seek Ch 5 – Using Macros in Spreadsheet Ch 8 – Introduction to DBMS Ch 9 – Starting with LibreOffice Base

🖼️ IT Class 10 (402) — Ch 2 – Working with Images

CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part B · Chapter 2

🖼️ Working with Images

Inserting, editing and formatting images, frames and drawing objects in LibreOffice Writer.

Topic 1 Inserting & Resizing Images

▼
⭐ Inserting an Image
Insert → Image → From File → Browse and select image → Open
Image appears at cursor position with green handles around it
⭐ Resizing an Image
Using mouse: Drag corner handles (hold Shift to maintain aspect ratio)
Precise size: Right-click → Position and Size (F4) → set exact Width and Height
Keep proportions: Check "Keep Ratio" option in Position and Size dialog
⭐ Text Wrapping Options
Right-click image → Image → Type tab
None: Image on its own line — text does not wrap
Page Wrap / Wrap Right / Wrap Left: Text flows around image
Optimal Page Wrap: Text wraps on side with more space
Through: Text flows over image

Topic 2 Image Properties & Drawing Tools

▼
⭐ Image Properties (Format → Image)
Type tab: Size, position, anchor type
Wrap tab: Text wrapping settings
Crop tab: Crop image from any side
Borders tab: Add border around image
Image tab: Brightness, Contrast, Colour mode (Greyscale, Black/White)
⭐ Anchoring Types
Anchor to Page: Image stays at fixed position on page
Anchor to Paragraph: Image moves with the paragraph it is anchored to
As Character: Image treated as a character in the text line
⭐ Drawing Toolbar
View → Toolbars → Drawing
Draw: Lines, Rectangles, Circles, Text boxes, Arrows, Callouts
Group: Select all objects → Format → Group → Group (Ctrl+Shift+G)
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 10 Part B

📝 IT Class 10 (402) — Ch 3 – Advanced Features of Writer

CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part B · Chapter 3

📝 Advanced Features of Writer

Mail merge, table of contents, templates and track changes in LibreOffice Writer.

Topic 1 Mail Merge

▼
📧 Mail MergeA feature to create personalised letters/envelopes/labels by combining a main document with a data source (spreadsheet) containing recipient details.
⭐ Steps for Mail Merge
1. Create main document (letter template) in Writer
2. Tools → Mail Merge Wizard
3. Select document type (Letter/Email/Label)
4. Select data source (spreadsheet with names, addresses)
5. Insert address block and salutation
6. Insert merge fields at required positions
7. Preview individual letters
8. Save merged document or print directly
⭐ Table of Contents (TOC)
Insert → Table of Contents and Index → Table of Contents
Auto-generated from Heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2 etc.)
To update: Right-click TOC → Update Index

Topic 2 Templates & Track Changes

▼
⭐ Creating a Template
1. Design document with required formatting
2. File → Templates → Save as Template
3. Give name and select category → OK
To use: File → Templates → Manage Templates → Open
⭐ Track Changes
Edit → Track Changes → Record Changes (Ctrl+Shift+C)
All edits shown with coloured marks and author name
Accept/Reject: Edit → Track Changes → Accept All / Reject All
⭐ Comments
Insert → Comment (Ctrl+Alt+C) — adds sticky-note comment in margin
Used for review and feedback without changing actual document
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 10 Part B

📊 IT Class 10 (402) — Ch 4 – Scenarios & Goal Seek

CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part B · Chapter 4

📊 Scenarios & Goal Seek

What-if analysis using Scenarios and Goal Seek in LibreOffice Calc.

Topic 1 Scenarios

▼
📊 ScenarioA saved set of values that can be substituted into a spreadsheet for what-if analysis. Create multiple scenarios with different values and switch between them to compare results.
⭐ Creating a Scenario
1. Select cells whose values will change
2. Tools → Scenarios
3. Enter scenario name (e.g. "Best Case", "Worst Case")
4. Enter different values for selected cells
5. Click Add → OK
Switch: Use Scenario drop-down that appears above the cells

Topic 2 Goal Seek

▼
⭐ Goal SeekWorks backwards — you specify the desired RESULT in a formula cell, and Goal Seek finds the INPUT value needed to achieve it.
⭐ Using Goal Seek
Tools → Goal Seek
Formula Cell: Cell containing formula (result cell)
Target Value: The result you want
Variable Cell: Cell whose value Goal Seek will change
Click OK → Calc finds the variable value
FeatureScenarioGoal Seek
PurposeCompare multiple input setsFind input for desired output
DirectionInput → OutputOutput → Input (reverse)
VariablesMultiple cellsONE variable cell only
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 10 Part B

⚙️ IT Class 10 (402) — Ch 5 – Using Macros in Spreadsheet

CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part B · Chapter 5

⚙️ Using Macros in Spreadsheet

Recording and running macros in LibreOffice Calc to automate repetitive tasks.

Topic 1 Introduction to Macros

▼
⚙️ MacroA recorded sequence of commands that can be replayed to automate repetitive tasks. Written in LibreOffice Basic language.
⭐ Recording a Macro
1. Tools → Macros → Record Macro
2. Perform the actions to record (typing, formatting, clicking)
3. Click Stop Recording
4. Save macro with a name in a module
⭐ Running a Macro
Tools → Macros → Run Macro → select → Run
OR assign to a button on sheet
OR assign keyboard shortcut: Tools → Customize → Keyboard

Topic 2 Editing Macros

▼
⭐ Basic IDE
Tools → Macros → Edit Macros → opens LibreOffice Basic IDE
Sub: Keyword to start a macro
End Sub: End of macro
Dim: Declare a variable
Press F5 to run and test
📝 Simple Macro
Sub HelloWorld()
    Dim oSheet As Object
    oSheet = ThisComponent.Sheets.getByIndex(0)
    oSheet.getCellByPosition(0,0).setString("Hello!")
End Sub
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 10 Part B
