🗄️ IT Class 10 (402) — Ch 8 – Introduction to DBMS

CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part B · Chapter 8

🗄️ Introduction to DBMS

Database concepts, DBMS advantages, data models and basic terminology.

Topic 1 Database Concepts

▼
🗄️ DatabaseOrganised collection of logically related data stored for easy access, management and update. Example: school database storing student records.
⭐ Problems with File System (solved by DBMS)
Data Redundancy — same data stored multiple times
Data Inconsistency — different copies may differ
Difficult sharing — hard to access across departments
No centralised security or access control
TermMeaning
Table / RelationTwo-dimensional structure with rows and columns
Field / AttributeColumn — represents one characteristic
Record / TupleRow — one complete entry
Primary KeyUnique identifier — no NULL, no duplicates
RDBMSRelational DBMS — data stored in related tables

Topic 2 Data Models

▼
Data ModelDescription
HierarchicalTree structure — parent-child. Oldest model.
NetworkChild can have multiple parents. Complex.
RelationalData in tables. Most popular — MySQL, Oracle.
Object-OrientedData as objects. Used with OOP languages.
📚 Chapters
🖥️ IT Class 10 Part B
Ch 1 – Introduction to Styles Ch 2 – Working with Images Ch 3 – Advanced Features of Writer Ch 4 – Scenarios & Goal Seek Ch 5 – Using Macros in Spreadsheet Ch 8 – Introduction to DBMS Ch 9 – Starting with LibreOffice Base

🗃️ IT Class 10 (402) — Ch 9 – Starting with LibreOffice Base

CBSE Class X · IT Code 402 · Part B · Chapter 9

🗃️ Starting with LibreOffice Base

Creating databases, tables, forms and queries using LibreOffice Base.

Topic 1 LibreOffice Base & Tables

▼
🗃️ LibreOffice BaseFree open-source DBMS. Part of LibreOffice suite. Equivalent to MS Access. Creates and manages databases, tables, forms, queries and reports. Saves as .odb format.
⭐ Creating a Table in Design View
1. Open Base → Create new database → Save
2. Tables → Create Table in Design View
3. Enter Field Names and Data Types
4. Right-click first column → Set as Primary Key
5. Close → Give table name → Save
Data TypeUse
IntegerWhole numbers — RollNo, Age
VARCHAR / TextText up to 255 chars — Name, City
DecimalNumbers with decimals — Marks, Salary
DateDate values — DOB, JoiningDate
BooleanTrue or False values

Topic 2 Forms & Queries

▼
⭐ Creating a Form (Wizard)
Forms → Use Wizard to Create Form
Select table → Add fields → Choose layout (Columnar/Tabular)
Set style → Give name → Finish
Form opens for easy data entry
⭐ Creating a Query (Design View)
Queries → Create Query in Design View
Select table → drag fields to query grid
Add conditions in Criterion row (e.g. >75)
Run query (F5) → shows filtered results
ObjectPurpose
TableStore raw data
FormUser-friendly data entry
QueryFilter and retrieve specific data
ReportFormatted printable summary
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part A
Unit 1: Communication Skills-III Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-III Unit 3: ICT Skills-III Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-III Unit 5: Green Skills-III

🧠 IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-III

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part A · Unit 2

🧠 Self-Management Skills-III

Stress management, motivation, goal setting and continuous learning for professional growth.

Topic 1 Stress Management

▼
😓 StressA state of mental or emotional strain caused by demanding circumstances. Eustress (positive) motivates; distress (negative) is harmful to health and performance.
⭐ Causes of Stress at Workplace
Work overload and tight deadlines
Lack of job security or career uncertainty
Poor relationships with colleagues or managers
Work-life imbalance — no time for family/hobbies
Unclear roles and responsibilities
⭐ Stress Management Techniques
Time Management: Prioritise tasks using To-Do lists; avoid procrastination
Exercise: Regular physical activity releases endorphins that reduce stress
Relaxation: Deep breathing, meditation, yoga
Positive Thinking: Focus on solutions, not problems
Talk it out: Share with trusted friends, family or counsellor
Sleep: 7–8 hours of quality sleep daily

Topic 2 Motivation & SMART Goals

▼
💪 MotivationThe internal drive to take action towards a goal. Intrinsic motivation = internal satisfaction; Extrinsic motivation = external rewards (money, praise, recognition).
⭐ Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (Bottom to Top)
1. Physiological — food, water, shelter, sleep
2. Safety — security, employment, health
3. Social — friends, family, belonging
4. Esteem — respect, recognition, achievement
5. Self-Actualisation — reaching full potential
⭐ SMART Goal Setting
Specific — clearly defined goal
Measurable — track progress with numbers
Achievable — realistic given resources
Relevant — aligned with overall objectives
Time-bound — has a clear deadline
⭐ Continuous Learning
IT industry changes rapidly — stay updated with new tools and technologies
Online courses: Coursera, NPTEL, Udemy, Khan Academy
Certifications: Google, Microsoft, Cisco, AWS
Practice: personal projects, GitHub, hackathons
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part A
Unit 1: Communication Skills-III Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-III Unit 3: ICT Skills-III Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-III Unit 5: Green Skills-III

💻 IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 3: ICT Skills-III

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part A · Unit 3

💻 ICT Skills-III

Advanced internet usage, digital safety, cloud computing and emerging technologies.

Topic 1 Advanced Internet & Collaboration Tools

▼
⭐ Effective Web Searching
Use specific keywords — avoid vague terms
Use quotes for exact phrases: "LibreOffice Writer shortcuts"
Use site: to search within a site: site:ncert.nic.in
Use filetype: for specific files: class11 notes filetype:pdf
Use – to exclude words: jaguar –car
Use Boolean operators: AND, OR, NOT
⭐ Digital Collaboration Tools
Google Workspace: Docs, Sheets, Slides — real-time collaborative editing
Microsoft Teams / Zoom: Video conferencing and meetings
Slack / Discord: Team messaging and channels
Trello / Asana: Project management and task tracking
GitHub: Version control and collaborative coding

Topic 2 Cloud Computing & Digital Safety

▼
☁️ Cloud ComputingDelivery of computing services over the internet — servers, storage, databases, software. Pay only for what you use. Examples: Google Drive, Dropbox, AWS, Azure.
ModelFull FormExample
IaaSInfrastructure as a ServiceAWS EC2, Google Compute
PaaSPlatform as a ServiceHeroku, Google App Engine
SaaSSoftware as a ServiceGmail, Google Docs, Office 365
⭐ Digital Safety Practices
Strong passwords — mix of letters, numbers, symbols (min 8 chars)
Enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) on accounts
Avoid public Wi-Fi for banking or sensitive tasks
Update software/OS regularly — patches fix security holes
Never share OTP, passwords or card details online
Verify sender before clicking links in emails (phishing)

Topic 3 Emerging Technologies

▼
TechnologyDescription
AIMachines simulate human intelligence — ChatGPT, Siri, Alexa
MLMachines learn from data without explicit programming
IoTPhysical devices connected to internet — smart home, wearables
BlockchainDecentralised digital ledger — used in cryptocurrency
AR / VRAR overlays digital on real world; VR creates virtual environments
Big DataVery large datasets analysed to find patterns and insights
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part A
Unit 1: Communication Skills-III Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-III Unit 3: ICT Skills-III Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-III Unit 5: Green Skills-III

💼 IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-III

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part A · Unit 4

💼 Entrepreneurial Skills-III

Business planning, financial literacy, marketing and India startup ecosystem.

Topic 1 Business Plan & Startup Ecosystem

▼
💼 EntrepreneurA person who starts a new business venture, takes risks and works to make it successful by identifying a problem and creating a product/service to solve it.
⭐ Business Plan Components
Executive Summary: Overview of the entire plan
Business Description: What the business does, mission, vision
Market Analysis: Target customers, competitors, market size
Products/Services: What you offer and its unique value
Marketing Strategy: How you will reach customers
Financial Plan: Revenue, expenses, profit projections
⭐ Indian Startup Ecosystem
Startup India: Govt initiative — tax benefits, funding support
Mudra Loan: Loan up to ₹10 lakh for small businesses
Incubator: Supports early-stage startups — space, mentorship
Angel Investor: Individual investing personal money for equity
Venture Capital: Firms investing in high-growth startups
MSME: Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises — backbone of economy

Topic 2 Financial Literacy & Marketing

▼
⭐ Basic Financial Terms
Revenue: Total money earned from sales
Expense: Money spent to run business
Profit: Revenue − Expenses
Break-Even: Revenue = Expenses (no profit, no loss)
Cash Flow: Movement of money in and out
ROI: (Profit / Investment) × 100
⭐ 4 Ps of Marketing
Product: What you sell — features, quality, branding
Price: What you charge — competitive/cost/value-based
Place: Where you sell — online, retail, distributor
Promotion: How you advertise — social media, TV, pamphlets
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part A
Unit 1: Communication Skills-III Unit 2: Self-Management Skills-III Unit 3: ICT Skills-III Unit 4: Entrepreneurial Skills-III Unit 5: Green Skills-III

🌿 IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 5: Green Skills-III

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part A · Unit 5

🌿 Green Skills-III

Sustainability, e-waste, green computing and eco-friendly practices in IT.

Topic 1 Sustainability & E-Waste

▼
🌿 SustainabilityMeeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs. Balances economic growth, social development and environmental protection.
⭐ Environmental Issues in IT
E-Waste: Discarded electronics — toxic materials (lead, mercury) harm soil and water
Energy Consumption: Data centres consume massive electricity, generate CO₂
Carbon Footprint: Total greenhouse gases emitted by activities
Resource Mining: Rare earth minerals for chips destroy ecosystems
⭐ 3Rs of Environment
Reduce: Use less — buy only what you need, reduce printing
Reuse: Use items multiple times before discarding
Recycle: Convert waste into reusable material — e-waste recycling centres

Topic 2 Green Computing & Green Technology

▼
💚 Green ComputingEnvironmentally responsible use of computers. Also called Green IT. Aims to reduce energy consumption and e-waste.
⭐ Green Computing Practices
Enable power saving mode — screen sleep after 5 minutes
Shutdown computers when not in use
Use energy-efficient hardware (Energy Star certified)
Print on both sides of paper (duplex printing)
Use cloud computing — reduces physical servers
Virtualisation — multiple VMs on one physical server
Proper e-waste disposal at authorised recycling centres
Green TechnologyDescription
Solar EnergyConvert sunlight to electricity using panels
Wind EnergyWind turbines generate renewable electricity
Electric VehiclesZero tailpipe emissions — reduces air pollution
LED LightingUses 80% less energy than traditional bulbs
Smart GridsIntelligent electricity distribution — reduces wastage
⭐ Government Initiatives
Solar Mission: India aims 500 GW renewable energy by 2030
Digital India: Paperless governance reduces paper waste
E-Waste Rules 2016: Manufacturers responsible for safe disposal
Smart Cities Mission: Eco-friendly urban development
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part B
Unit 1: Computer Organization Unit 2: Networking and Internet Unit 3: Office Automation Tools Unit 4: RDBMS (MySQL) Unit 5: Fundamentals of Java

💻 IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 1: Computer Organization

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part B · Unit 1

💻 Computer Organization

Computer components, memory types, operating system and troubleshooting basics.

Topic 1 Computer System & Components

▼
💻 ComputerAn electronic machine that accepts data (input), processes it as per instructions (program) and produces output. It can store data and execute instructions at very high speed.
⭐ Characteristics of Computer
Speed: Processes millions of instructions per second (MIPS/GIPS)
Accuracy: Results are always accurate — errors are due to wrong human input
Diligence: Performs same task repeatedly without fatigue
Versatility: Can perform different tasks — calculations, gaming, communication
Memory: Can store large amounts of data permanently or temporarily
ComponentFunction
Input UnitAccepts data from user (keyboard, mouse, scanner, microphone)
CPU — ALUArithmetic Logic Unit — performs calculations and logical operations
CPU — CUControl Unit — controls and coordinates all computer operations
Memory UnitStores data — Primary (RAM/ROM) and Secondary (HDD, SSD, USB)
Output UnitDisplays/produces results (monitor, printer, speaker, projector)

Topic 2 Memory Types

▼
MemoryNatureDetails
RAMVolatile (temporary)Primary memory; stores running programs; lost on power off. Types: DRAM, SRAM
ROMNon-volatileRead only; stores BIOS/boot instructions. Types: PROM, EPROM, EEPROM
CacheVery fast, smallBetween CPU and RAM; reduces access time; most expensive per byte
HDD/SSDPermanentSecondary storage; stores OS, programs, files permanently
⭐ Units of Memory
1 Bit = 0 or 1  |  1 Nibble = 4 bits  |  1 Byte = 8 bits
1 KB = 1024 Bytes  |  1 MB = 1024 KB  |  1 GB = 1024 MB  |  1 TB = 1024 GB

Topic 3 Operating System & Troubleshooting

▼
⭐ Operating SystemSystem software that acts as interface between user and hardware. Examples: Windows, Linux, macOS, Android. Functions: Process management, Memory management, File management, Device management, Security.
OS TypeDescription
Single-userOne user at a time — personal computers
Multi-userMultiple users simultaneously — servers, mainframes
Multi-taskingMultiple programs run simultaneously — Windows, Linux
Real-Time OSResponds within strict time — medical devices, missiles
⭐ Common Troubleshooting
Blank screen: Check power cable, connections, sleep mode
Slow system: Run Disk Cleanup, remove temp files, Defragmentation
No internet: Check network icon, run troubleshooter, ipconfig command
Printer not printing: Check cable, paper, ink, driver
No sound: Check volume, cables, driver
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part B
Unit 1: Computer Organization Unit 2: Networking and Internet Unit 3: Office Automation Tools Unit 4: RDBMS (MySQL) Unit 5: Fundamentals of Java

🌐 IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 2: Networking and Internet

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part B · Unit 2

🌐 Networking and Internet

Network types, topology, devices, protocols and cyber security.

Topic 1 Network Types & Devices

▼
NetworkRangeExample
PAN~10 metresBluetooth between phone and laptop
LANUp to 1 kmSchool/office network
MANUp to 50 kmCity-wide cable TV network
WAN1000+ kmInternet, bank ATM network
DeviceFunction
RepeaterAmplifies/regenerates weak signals for long distances
HubConnects devices; broadcasts data to all ports (not intelligent)
SwitchConnects LAN devices; sends data only to destination port (intelligent)
RouterConnects different networks; determines best path for data packets
GatewayConnects networks with different protocols; converts data format
BridgeConnects two LANs; filters traffic between them

Topic 2 Network Topology

▼
TopologyDescriptionAdvantage / Disadvantage
BusAll nodes on one backbone cableEasy install; fault detection hard
RingNodes in circular formEasy fault detection; one failure breaks all
StarAll nodes to central hub/switchEasy to add nodes; hub failure = total failure
MeshEvery node to every other nodeVery reliable; expensive and complex
TreeHybrid of Star + BusScalable; backbone failure disrupts all
⭐ MAC vs IP Address
MAC Address: 48-bit (12 hex digits), assigned by manufacturer, permanent, identifies hardware
IP Address: 32-bit (4 decimal groups 0–255), assigned by ISP, can change, identifies network location
DNS: Domain Name System — converts domain name (www.google.com) to IP address

Topic 3 Internet Services & Cyber Security

▼
ServiceProtocolUse
E-mailSMTP, POP3, IMAPSend/receive messages with attachments
WWWHTTP / HTTPSAccess web pages via browser
File TransferFTPUpload/download files between systems
Remote LoginTELNET / SSHAccess remote computer from local system
⭐ Cyber Threats
Virus: Attaches to files; corrupts data; spreads via infected files
Worm: Self-replicates through network; no host file needed
Trojan Horse: Looks legitimate but creates backdoor for attackers
Phishing: Fake emails/websites to steal personal info
Ransomware: Encrypts files and demands money to decrypt
DoS Attack: Floods server making it unavailable to genuine users
⭐ Security Measures
Use strong passwords and change regularly
Install updated antivirus and firewall
Avoid clicking suspicious links or unknown emails
Use HTTPS websites for sensitive transactions
Enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part B
Unit 1: Computer Organization Unit 2: Networking and Internet Unit 3: Office Automation Tools Unit 4: RDBMS (MySQL) Unit 5: Fundamentals of Java

📄 IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 3: Office Automation Tools

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part B · Unit 3

📄 Office Automation Tools

LibreOffice Writer, Calc and Impress — advanced features for professional use.

Topic 1 LibreOffice Writer — Advanced

▼
⭐ Styles
A style is a named set of formatting. Apply once — all formatting applied together.
Types: Paragraph styles, Character styles, Page styles, Frame styles, List styles
Apply: Format → Styles → Manage Styles (F11)
Modify: Right-click style → Edit Style → change → OK (all text using that style updates!)
⭐ Mail Merge
Creates personalised letters by combining a template with a data source (spreadsheet)
Tools → Mail Merge Wizard → Select document type → Select data source → Insert merge fields → Preview → Print/Save
⭐ Table of Contents
Insert → Table of Contents and Index → Table of Contents
Auto-generated from Heading styles. To update: Right-click TOC → Update Index
ShortcutFunction
Ctrl+B / I / UBold / Italic / Underline
Ctrl+HFind and Replace
F7Spell Check
Ctrl+Shift+CTrack Changes on/off
Ctrl+PPrint

Topic 2 LibreOffice Calc — Advanced

▼
⭐ Key Functions
=SUM(A1:A10) — Add  |  =AVERAGE() — Mean  |  =MAX() / =MIN()
=COUNT() — Count numbers  |  =COUNTA() — Count non-empty cells
=IF(condition, true_val, false_val) — Conditional
=ROUND(A1, 2) — Round to 2 decimals
⭐ Cell Referencing
Relative (A1): Changes when formula is copied
Absolute ($A$1): Does NOT change when copied — use $ sign
Mixed ($A1 or A$1): Only row or only column is fixed
⭐ Scenarios & Goal Seek
Scenario: Save multiple sets of values — Tools → Scenarios — for what-if analysis
Goal Seek: Find input for desired output — Tools → Goal Seek — works backwards

Topic 3 LibreOffice Impress

▼
⭐ Key Features
Slide Transition: Effect between slides — Slide → Slide Transition
Animation: Effect on objects within a slide — View → Animation
Slide Master: View → Master → Slide Master — uniform design for all slides
Slide Show: F5 to start; Esc to end; Space/Arrow to navigate
6×6 Rule: Max 6 lines per slide, max 6 words per line
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part B
Unit 1: Computer Organization Unit 2: Networking and Internet Unit 3: Office Automation Tools Unit 4: RDBMS (MySQL) Unit 5: Fundamentals of Java

🗄️ IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 4: RDBMS (MySQL)

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part B · Unit 4

🗄️ RDBMS (MySQL)

Database concepts, keys, SQL commands — DDL and DML, constraints and queries.

Topic 1 Database & RDBMS Concepts

▼
🗄️ RDBMSRelational Database Management System — stores data in tables (relations) with rows and columns. Developed by E.F. Codd (1970). MySQL is a popular RDBMS.
TermMeaning
Table / RelationTwo-dimensional structure to store data
TupleA row / single record in a table
AttributeA column in a table
Primary KeyUniquely identifies each row — no NULL, no duplicate
Foreign KeyPrimary key of another table — creates relationship
Candidate KeyAll columns that could be primary key
Alternate KeyCandidate keys not selected as primary key
ConstraintEffect
PRIMARY KEYNo NULL, no duplicate — uniquely identifies row
NOT NULLColumn cannot be left empty
UNIQUENo duplicate values allowed
DEFAULTSets default value if none given
FOREIGN KEYLinks to primary key of another table

Topic 2 SQL — DDL Commands

▼
📝 CREATE DATABASE & TABLE
CREATE DATABASE School;
USE School;

CREATE TABLE Student (
  RollNo INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Name   VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
  Class  INT,
  Marks  FLOAT DEFAULT 0
);
📝 ALTER & DROP
ALTER TABLE Student ADD Email VARCHAR(50);  -- Add column
ALTER TABLE Student DROP Class;             -- Remove column
ALTER TABLE Student MODIFY Name VARCHAR(50); -- Change column
DROP TABLE Student;                          -- Delete table
DROP DATABASE School;                        -- Delete database

Topic 3 SQL — DML Commands

▼
📝 INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT
INSERT INTO Student VALUES (1, 'Rahul', 10, 85.5);
INSERT INTO Student (RollNo, Name) VALUES (2, 'Priya');

UPDATE Student SET Marks = 90 WHERE RollNo = 1;

DELETE FROM Student WHERE RollNo = 2;

SELECT * FROM Student;
SELECT Name, Marks FROM Student WHERE Marks > 75;
SELECT * FROM Student WHERE Name LIKE 'R%';
SELECT * FROM Student WHERE Marks BETWEEN 60 AND 90;
SELECT * FROM Student ORDER BY Marks DESC;
SELECT * FROM Student WHERE Class IN (9, 10, 11);
ClauseUse
WHEREFilter rows by condition
ORDER BY col ASC/DESCSort result set
LIKE 'R%'Pattern match — % = any chars, _ = one char
BETWEEN x AND yRange (inclusive)
IN (x, y, z)Match any value in list
DISTINCTRemove duplicate rows
📚 Units
🖥️ IT Class 11 Part B
Unit 1: Computer Organization Unit 2: Networking and Internet Unit 3: Office Automation Tools Unit 4: RDBMS (MySQL) Unit 5: Fundamentals of Java

☕ IT Class 11 (802) — Unit 5: Fundamentals of Java

CBSE Class XI · IT Code 802 · Part B · Unit 5

☕ Fundamentals of Java

Java basics, NetBeans IDE, OOP concepts, GUI components and control structures.

Topic 1 Java & NetBeans IDE

▼
☕ JavaHigh-level, object-oriented, platform independent programming language. Write Once Run Anywhere (WORA). Used for desktop, mobile, web and game development.
GUI ComponentClassUse
Window/FrameJFrameMain container for other components
ButtonjButtonTriggers action on click
Text FieldjTextFieldSingle-line input/output
LabeljLabelDisplay non-editable text or image
Text AreajTextAreaMulti-line input/output
Password FieldjPasswordFieldMasked input for passwords
Radio ButtonjRadioButtonSelect ONE from multiple options
CheckBoxjCheckBoxSelect ONE OR MORE options
Combo BoxjComboBoxDropdown list — select one item

Topic 2 OOP Concepts & Data Types

▼
⭐ OOP Concepts
Class: Template/blueprint defining data (attributes) and behaviour (methods)
Object: Instance of a class holding actual data
Encapsulation: Binding data and methods together in a class
Inheritance: A class inherits properties of another class
Polymorphism: Same method behaves differently in different contexts
⭐ Access Modifiers
private: Accessible only within the class — for data members
public: Accessible from anywhere — for methods
protected: Accessible within class and subclasses
Data TypeSizeExample
int4 bytesint age = 17;
float4 bytesfloat marks = 85.5f;
double8 bytesdouble pi = 3.14159;
char2 byteschar grade = 'A';
boolean1 bitboolean pass = true;
StringvariesString name = "Rahul";

Topic 3 Control Structures & Key Methods

▼
📝 if-else Example
int n = Integer.parseInt(jTextField1.getText());
if (n % 2 == 0) {
    jLabel1.setText("Even Number");
} else {
    jLabel1.setText("Odd Number");
}
📝 switch-case Example
int day = Integer.parseInt(jTextField1.getText());
switch (day) {
    case 1: jLabel1.setText("Monday"); break;
    case 2: jLabel1.setText("Tuesday"); break;
    default: jLabel1.setText("Other day");
}
⭐ Key Methods
getText(): Get text from jTextField — returns String
setText("value"): Set/display text in jTextField or jLabel
Integer.parseInt(str): Convert String to int
Double.parseDouble(str): Convert String to double
String.valueOf(n): Convert number to String
isSelected(): Check if jRadioButton or jCheckBox is ticked
JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(null,"msg"): Show popup message
📚 Chapters
💻 CS Class 11
Ch 1 – Computer System Ch 2 – Encoding & Number System Ch 3 – Problem Solving Ch 4 – Getting Started with Python Ch 5 – Flow of Control Ch 6 – Functions Ch 7 – Strings Ch 8 – Lists Ch 9 – Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 10 – Society, Law & Ethics Ch 11 – Emerging Trends

🔢 CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 2 – Encoding Schemes & Number System

🔢
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 2

Encoding Schemes & Number System

Complete notes on ASCII, ISCII, UNICODE encoding schemes and Decimal, Binary, Octal, Hexadecimal number systems with all conversions — integer and fractional parts — with step-by-step examples and diagrams.

📚 5 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 🔄 All Conversions 💡 Examples & Diagrams

Topic 1 Encoding Schemes — ASCII, ISCII & UNICODE

▼
📖 Encoding The process of converting data from one form to another is called Encoding. In Computer Science, encoding means converting data (numbers, alphabets, symbols, spaces, graphics etc.) into binary codes.
⭐ Why Encoding? — Purposes
  • Platform Independency — Same encoded data works on any platform.
  • Internationalization — Supports multiple languages worldwide.
  • Security — Helps in secure data transmission.
  • Effective Communication — Standardised way to exchange data between systems.
⭐ ASCII vs ISCII vs UNICODE — Comparison Table
FeatureASCIIISCIIUNICODE
Full FormAmerican Standard Code for Information InterchangeIndian Standard Code for Information InterchangeUniversal Character Encoding Standard
IntroducedFormally 1960; promoted by ANSI1991 by Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)Developed to represent all world languages
Bits Used7-bit8-bit8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit
Total Characters2⁷ = 128 characters2⁸ = 256 characters~1,000,000+ characters
Languages SupportedEnglish only10 Indian languages (Devanagari, Punjabi, Bengali, Gujarati, Oriya, Telugu, Assamese, Kannada, Malayalam, Tamil + Roman)All written languages of the world
Encodings——UTF-8 (most common), UTF-16, UTF-32
RelationBase encodingExtension of ASCIISuperset of ASCII
📊 ASCII Table — Important Characters & Decimal Values
CharacterDecimalCharacterDecimalCharacterDecimal
Space32@64`96
!33A65a97
"34B66b98
#35C67c99
$36D68d100
%37E69e101
&38F70f102
'39G71g103
(40H72h104
)41I73i105
048Z90z122

💡 Key Trick: A=65, a=97 (difference of 32). 0=48. Z=90, z=122.

Topic 2 Number System

▼
📖 Number System A mathematical way to represent numbers using literals (digits, characters or symbols). Also known as Positional Number System because the value of each literal depends on its position within the number.
⭐ Four Number Systems Used in Computers
Number SystemBase / RadixDigits UsedUsed By / For
Decimal100, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9Humans — everyday calculations
Binary20, 1 (called Bits)Computers & digital devices; Machine Language
Octal80, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7Compact representation of binary; Mainframes
Hexadecimal160–9, A, B, C, D, E, FMemory addresses, web colors, MAC addresses
📊 Hexadecimal Digit Values (A–F)
Hex DigitABCDEF
Decimal Value101112131415
Positional Value Representation of Decimal Number 1253.76
⭐ Applications of Number Systems
Number SystemApplications
BinaryMachine Language; ASCII & Unicode; Internet Protocols; Image Processing; HD Video & Audio; Boolean Algebra
OctalRegisters & flip-flops on Motherboards; Mainframes like UNIVAC, PDP
HexadecimalMemory Addresses; Web Page Colors (#FF5733); MAC Addresses; Error Messages

Topic 3 Number System Conversions — Decimal ↔ Binary / Octal / Hex

▼
📌 Method: Decimal → Any Base (Division Method)
  1. Divide the number by the base (2 / 8 / 16).
  2. Note the remainder.
  3. Keep dividing quotient by base until it becomes 0; note each remainder.
  4. Write all remainders in reverse order (bottom to top) — that is the answer.
🔄 Decimal → Binary (÷ 2)
Example 1: (45)₁₀ → Binary
45 ÷ 2 → remainders read bottom to top = (101101)₂
Example 2: (234)₁₀ → Binary
Answer: (11101010)₂
🔄 Decimal → Octal (÷ 8)
Example 1: (186)₁₀ → Octal
Answer: (272)₈
Example 2: (1683)₁₀ → Octal
Answer: (3223)₈
🔄 Decimal → Hexadecimal (÷ 16)
Example 1: (763)₁₀ → Hex
Answer: (2FB)₁₆
Example 2: (2940)₁₀ → Hex
Answer: (B7C)₁₆
📌 Method: Any Base → Decimal (Positional Value Method)
  1. Write down the number.
  2. Starting from right to left, assign position numbers (0, 1, 2, 3…).
  3. Positional value = digit × baseposition.
  4. Add all positional values to get the decimal equivalent.
🔄 Binary → Decimal
Example 1: (10011)₂ → Decimal
Example 2: (111100)₂ → Decimal
1×2⁵ + 1×2⁴ + 1×2³ + 1×2² + 0×2¹ + 0×2⁰ = 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 0 = (60)₁₀
🔄 Octal → Decimal
Example 1: (336)₈ → Decimal
Example 2: (1325)₈ → Decimal
1×8³ + 3×8² + 2×8¹ + 5×8⁰ = 512 + 192 + 16 + 5 = (725)₁₀
🔄 Hexadecimal → Decimal
Example 1: (7AC)₁₆ → Decimal
Example 2: (1BF6)₁₆ → Decimal
1×16³ + 11×16² + 15×16¹ + 6×16⁰ = 4096 + 2816 + 240 + 6 = (7158)₁₀

Topic 4 Binary ↔ Octal & Binary ↔ Hexadecimal Conversions

▼
📌 Binary → Octal (Group of 3 bits)
  1. Write binary number.
  2. Group bits from right to left into sets of 3.
  3. Add 0s to the left if last group has fewer than 3 bits.
  4. Write Octal digit for each group of 3 bits.
📌 Octal → Binary (Each digit = 3 bits)
  1. Write octal number.
  2. Replace each octal digit with its 3-bit binary equivalent.
  3. Write all 3-bit groups together.
🔄 Binary → Octal
Example 1: (100110101)₂ → Octal
Answer: (465)₈
Example 2: (1011)₂ → Octal
1   0   1   1
001   011
 1     3
= (13)₈
🔄 Octal → Binary
Example 1: (745)₈ → Binary
7=111, 4=100, 5=101 → Answer: (111100101)₂
📌 Binary → Hexadecimal (Group of 4 bits)
  1. Write binary number.
  2. Group bits from right to left into sets of 4.
  3. Add 0s to the left if last group has fewer than 4 bits.
  4. Write Hex digit for each group of 4 bits.
📌 Hex → Binary (Each digit = 4 bits)
  1. Write hexadecimal number.
  2. Replace each hex digit with its 4-bit binary equivalent.
  3. Write all 4-bit groups together.
🔄 Binary → Hexadecimal
Example 1: (1100110101)₂ → Hex
Answer: (335)₁₆
Example 2: (11101001101)₂ → Hex
0111   0100   1101
  7      4       D
= (74D)₁₆
🔄 Hexadecimal → Binary
Example 1: (CA12)₁₆ → Binary
C=1100, A=1010, 1=0001, 2=0010 → Answer: (1100101000010010)₂
📊 Quick Reference: Decimal / Binary / Octal / Hex Equivalents (0–15)
DecimalBinary (4-bit)OctalHexadecimal
0000000
1000111
2001022
3001133
4010044
5010155
6011066
7011177
81000108
91001119
10101012A
11101113B
12110014C
13110115D
14111016E
15111117F

Topic 5 Fractional Part Conversions

▼
📌 Method: Decimal (Fractional) → Any Base (Multiplication Method)
  1. Multiply the fractional part by the base (2 / 8 / 16) repeatedly.
  2. Note the integer part of each result.
  3. Continue until fractional part becomes 0 (or up to 10 steps if it repeats).
  4. Read integer parts from top to bottom — that is the fractional answer.
🔄 Decimal (Fraction) → Binary
Example 1: (0.625)₁₀ → Binary
Answer: (0.101)₂
Example 2: (0.36)₁₀ → Binary
Answer (approx.): (0.0101110…)₂
🔄 Decimal (Fraction) → Octal
Example 1: (0.175)₁₀ → Octal
Answer: (0.13)₈
Example 2: (0.345)₁₀ → Octal
Answer (approx.): (0.26066…)₈
🔄 Decimal (Fraction) → Hexadecimal
Example 1: (0.175)₁₀ → Hex
Answer: (0.2CCC…)₁₆
Example 2: (0.220)₁₀ → Hex
Answer: (0.3851…)₁₆
🔄 Binary (with Fraction) → Decimal

For fractional bits, use negative powers of 2: 2⁻¹=0.5, 2⁻²=0.25, 2⁻³=0.125 …

Example 1: (10011.11)₂ → Decimal
Example 2: (101.01)₂ → Decimal
1×2² + 0×2¹ + 1×2⁰ . 0×2⁻¹ + 1×2⁻² = 4 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0.25 = (5.25)₁₀
🔄 Binary (with Fraction) → Octal

Group integer part from right to left (3 bits); group fractional part from left to right (3 bits). Pad with 0s as needed on both sides.

Example 1: (10101.01101)₂ → Octal
Example 2: (1011.10)₂ → Octal
011 011 . 100 → 3 3 . 4 = (33.4)₈
🔄 Binary (with Fraction) → Hexadecimal

Group integer part from right to left (4 bits); group fractional part from left to right (4 bits). Pad with 0s as needed.

Example 1: (110101.011011)₂ → Hex
Example 2: (1011111011.1011010)₂ → Hex
0010 1111 1011 . 1011 0100
2     F     B  .  B    4 = (2FB.B4)₁₆
⚡ Quick Revision — Important One-Liners
  • 🔡 ASCII = 7-bit = 128 chars (English only) | ISCII = 8-bit = 256 chars (10 Indian languages) | Unicode = superset of ASCII
  • 🔢 Decimal base 10 | Binary base 2 | Octal base 8 | Hex base 16 (0–9, A–F)
  • ➗ Decimal → Other: Division method, remainders read bottom to top
  • ✖️ Decimal fraction → Other: Multiplication method, integer parts read top to bottom
  • 🔢 Binary → Octal: Group 3 bits | Binary → Hex: Group 4 bits (right to left)
  • 🔡 ASCII: A=65, a=97 (gap of 32). 0=48. Z=90, z=122
  • 💡 UTF-8 = most common Unicode encoding; used on the Internet
📚 Chapters
💻 CS Class 11
Ch 1 – Computer System Ch 2 – Encoding & Number System Ch 3 – Problem Solving Ch 4 – Getting Started with Python Ch 5 – Flow of Control Ch 6 – Functions Ch 7 – Strings Ch 8 – Lists Ch 9 – Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 10 – Society, Law & Ethics Ch 11 – Emerging Trends

🖥️ CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 1 – Computer System & Organisation

🖥️
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 1

Computer System & Organisation

Comprehensive notes covering Computer System basics, Block Diagram, CPU, Memory (RAM, ROM, Cache), Secondary Memory, Software types, Operating System, Language Translators and more — as per CBSE syllabus.

📚 7 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💡 Diagrams Included 📊 Tables & Examples

Topic 1 What is a Computer System?

▼
📖 Computer A Computer is basically an electronic machine meant for computing. It can be programmed to take data as input, process it and generate information as output.
📖 Computer System A Computer System is an ideal combination of Hardware and Software. Hardware refers to physical parts; Software refers to programs that run on hardware.
⭐ Forms & Sizes of Computer Systems
  • Desktop — Personal computer used on a desk.
  • Laptop — Portable personal computer.
  • Tablet — Touch-based portable device.
  • Mainframe — Large computer for bulk data processing in organisations.
  • Supercomputer — Extremely fast computer for complex scientific calculations.
  • Smartphone — Mobile phone with computing capabilities.
Image 1: Computer System and Organization

Topic 2 Basic Structure & Block Diagram of Computer System

▼

The basic structure of a computer system shows how its components are connected. The directed lines in the block diagram represent the flow of data and signals between components.

Image 2: Computer System Block Diagram
⭐ Five Main Components of a Computer System
  1. Input Devices
  2. Output Devices
  3. CPU (Central Processing Unit)
  4. Memory (Primary)
  5. Storage Devices (Secondary Memory)
⌨️ Input Devices Devices used to enter data into the computer. They convert input data into digital signals understood by the computer system.
Key Points – Input Devices
  • Convert human-readable data into machine-readable digital signals.
  • Input data is temporarily stored in Primary Memory (RAM).
  • Examples: Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner, Touch Screen, Microphone, Joystick, Webcam.
🖥️ Output Devices Devices responsible for displaying or producing information. They convert digital signals into human-understandable form.
Key Points – Output Devices
  • Convert digital signals back into human-readable form.
  • Output data is temporarily stored in primary memory and can be permanently saved in secondary memory.
  • Examples: Monitor, Printer, Speaker, Plotter, Projector.
⚙️ CPU – Central Processing Unit The CPU is called the Brain of the Computer. It is responsible for all processing done on data. It performs arithmetic and logical operations on data as per the instructions fetched from memory.
⭐ Three Components of CPU
ComponentFull FormFunction
Registers—Small, fast storage inside CPU. Temporarily store data and instructions during processing.
ALUArithmetic & Logic UnitPerforms all arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, comparison) operations.
CUControl UnitControls the flow of data between memory, input & output devices; manages sequential execution of instructions.

Topic 3 Computer Memory

▼
📖 Computer Memory Computer Memory is used to store data and instructions required for processing.
Memory Hierarchy
Diagram: Types of Computer Memory
🟦 Primary Memory (Main Memory) Stores data and programs temporarily during operation. CPU can read/write directly to primary memory. It is volatile in nature.
⭐ RAM vs ROM — Comparison Table
FeatureRAMROM
Full FormRandom Access MemoryRead Only Memory
NatureVolatile (data lost on power off)Non-volatile (data retained)
PurposeStores data & programs temporarily during operationStores startup programs (Bootstrap loader, BIOS)
Modifiable?Yes — read & write bothNo — read only
Also CalledMain Memory / Working MemoryFirmware
🟣 Cache Memory A very high-speed type of primary memory placed between CPU and RAM. Its main purpose is to store copies of frequently accessed data from RAM, reducing the time taken to access that data by the CPU.
⭐ Cache Memory — Key Points
  • Faster than RAM but smaller in size.
  • Located between CPU and Primary Memory (RAM).
  • Stores frequently used data/instructions for quick access.
  • Reduces CPU wait time — improves overall system speed.
  • Types: L1 Cache (inside CPU core), L2 Cache, L3 Cache.
🟠 Secondary Memory (Storage) Stores data and programs permanently. It is non-volatile. CPU cannot access secondary memory directly — data must first be loaded into primary memory.
⭐ Secondary Memory — Key Points
  • Non-volatile — data retained even after power off.
  • Larger storage capacity than primary memory.
  • Slower access speed compared to primary memory.
  • CPU cannot directly access it; data must be copied to RAM first.
  • Examples: SSD (Solid State Drive), HDD (Hard Disk Drive), CD/DVD, Pen Drive/Flash Drive, Memory Card.
📊 Units of Memory

The basic unit of memory is Byte. 8 bits = 1 Byte. 4 bits = 1 Nibble. 2 Nibbles = 1 Byte.

UnitFull FormValue
1 KBKiloByte1024 Bytes
1 MBMegaByte1024 KB
1 GBGigaByte1024 MB
1 TBTeraByte1024 GB
1 PBPetaByte1024 TB
1 EBExaByte1024 PB
1 ZBZettaByte1024 EB
1 YBYottaByte1024 ZB

Topic 4 Software

▼
📖 Software Software refers to a set of programs that instruct hardware what to do and how to do it. It comprises instructions and data to be processed using computer hardware. Softwares are intangible (cannot be touched).
⭐ Need of Software
  • Software acts as an interface between user and hardware.
  • It makes computer hardware operational and useful.
  • Each software is written for a specific computational purpose.
Types of Software
Diagram: Classification of Software

Topic 5 Types of Software — System Software & Application Software

▼
📖 System Software Software that manages computer hardware by interacting directly with it. Provides services to users and resources to other software.
⭐ Types of System Software
TypeDescriptionExamples
Operating SystemInterface between user and hardware; manages all resources.Windows, Linux, macOS, Ubuntu, MS-DOS
System UtilitiesTools to maintain, improve performance and security of the computer.Disk Defragmenter, Antivirus, Backup Tools, Formatter
Device DriversPrograms that help hardware devices communicate with the OS.Printer driver, Graphics driver, Sound driver
📖 Operating System (OS) The OS acts as an interface between the user and computer hardware. Every computer must have at least one OS installed.
⭐ Functions of Operating System
FunctionDescription
Communication ManagementEstablishes communication between peripheral devices (printer, keyboard, mouse) using drivers.
Resource ManagementManages allocation and priority of CPU time, memory, files, I/O devices to different processes.
Process ManagementManages, controls, and schedules different operations being executed in CPU. Decides which process gets the processor and for how long.
Memory ManagementDynamically allocates memory to processes being executed and releases it when not needed.
File ManagementManages all files and folders — creating, deleting, moving, copying, renaming.
📖 OS User Interface The medium through which a user interacts with an Operating System.
⭐ Types of OS User Interface — Comparison
Interface TypeKey FeatureInput DeviceExamples
CUI (Character/Command)Text commands to interact; user must remember all commands.KeyboardMS-DOS, UNIX
GUI (Graphical)Icons, images, buttons, menus for interaction; multitasking supported.MouseWindows, macOS, Ubuntu, Fedora
Touch-based InterfaceInteraction via touch input; mainly smartphones & tablets.Finger/StylusAndroid, iOS, Windows (touch)
Voice-based InterfaceInteraction using human voice; designed for accessibility.MicrophoneSiri (iOS), Google Assistant, Cortana (Win-10)
Gesture-based InterfaceInteraction using gestures like waving, tilting, eye motion.Camera/SensorsGaming consoles, Medical devices, Smartphones
📖 Application Software Software developed for a specific purpose like drawing, presentation, documentation, calculation. Always runs on top of system software.
⭐ Types of Application Software
TypeDescriptionExamples
General Purpose SoftwareDesigned for generic applications; meets requirements of mass users.MS Word, Excel, Photoshop, Chrome, VLC Player
Customized SoftwareDesigned to meet requirements of a specific individual or organisation.School Management System, Hospital Management, Inventory Software, Payroll Software

Topic 6 Programming Tools & Programming Languages

▼
📖 Programming Tools Tools used to write instructions, convert high-level to machine language, and develop software. Includes: Programming Languages, Language Translators, and Program Development Tools.
📖 Programming Languages Languages used to write instructions for computer systems. Classified into:
⭐ Types of Programming Languages — Comparison
TypeSub-TypeKey FeatureExample
Low Level LanguageMachine LanguageUses binary (0s and 1s); directly executed by CPU; no translator needed; very difficult to write/debug.Binary Code: 01010101
Assembly LanguageUses English-like mnemonics (ADD, MOV, SUB); platform dependent; needs Assembler to convert to machine code.MOV AX, BX
High Level Language—Uses English-like syntax; platform independent; easy to write; needs Compiler/Interpreter to convert to machine code.Python, C++, Java, C#

Topic 7 Language Translators & Program Development Tools

▼
📖 Language Translators Programs that convert instructions written in assembly or high-level language into machine language. The converted code is called Object Code.
Diagram: How Language Translators Work
⭐ Three Types of Language Translators — Comparison
FeatureAssemblerCompilerInterpreter
Input LanguageAssembly LanguageHigh-Level LanguageHigh-Level Language
ConversionConverts assembly to machine codeConverts entire source code at once to machine codeConverts source code line by line to machine code
Error ReportingReports errors after full translationShows all errors after scanning full programStops at first error found
SpeedFastFaster execution (compiled once)Slower (translates every time)
Translator needed again?No (after translation)No (after successful compilation)Yes (every time program runs)
PortabilityNot portable (CPU-specific)Portable (once compiled for platform)Portable
ExamplesNASM, MASMC, C++, Java (javac)Python, Ruby, JavaScript
📖 Program Development Tools (IDE) Also called Integrated Development Environment (IDE). Provides all resources needed for software development including: Text Editor, Debugger, Compiler, GUI Editor.
⭐ Examples of IDEs / Program Development Tools
  • Python IDLE — Default IDE for Python programming.
  • PyCharm — Advanced Python IDE by JetBrains.
  • NetBeans — IDE for Java and other languages.
  • Eclipse — Open-source IDE for Java, C++, Python.
  • Visual Studio Code — Lightweight, powerful IDE by Microsoft.
  • Atom — Open-source text editor / IDE.
⚡ Quick Revision — Important One-Liners
  • 🖥️ CPU = Brain of Computer | ALU + CU + Registers
  • 🟦 RAM = Volatile, Temporary | ROM = Non-Volatile, Read-Only
  • 🟣 Cache Memory = Between CPU & RAM, stores frequently accessed data
  • 💾 Secondary Memory = Permanent, Non-Volatile (HDD, SSD, Pen Drive)
  • 🔧 Assembler → Assembly to Machine | Compiler → HLL at once | Interpreter → HLL line by line
  • 🖱️ GUI uses Mouse | CUI uses Keyboard | Touch uses Finger | Voice uses Mic
  • 📏 1 KB = 1024 Bytes | 1 MB = 1024 KB | 1 GB = 1024 MB | 1 TB = 1024 GB

🧠 CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 2: Chapter 3 – Introduction to Problem Solving

🧠
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 2 · Chapter 3

Introduction to Problem Solving

Algorithm, Flowchart, Pseudocode, Steps of Problem Solving, Flow of Control, and Decomposition — complete notes as per CBSE syllabus.

📚 5 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💡 Examples Included

Topic 1 Problem Solving — Definition & Steps

▼
📖 Problem Solving Problem solving is the process of identifying a problem, analyzing it, developing an algorithm, and finally implementing the algorithm to develop a program.
⭐ 4 Steps of Problem Solving
StepNameDescription
1Analyze the ProblemUnderstand the problem clearly — identify inputs, outputs and process required
2Developing an AlgorithmCreate a step-by-step plan to solve the problem
3CodingWrite the algorithm in a Programming Language
4Testing & DebuggingTest the program and find and fix errors

Topic 2 Algorithm

▼
📖 Algorithm A set of precise, finite and sequential steps written to solve a problem and get the desired output. It has a definite beginning and a definite end.
⭐ Characteristics of a Good Algorithm
  • Precision — Steps are precisely stated/defined.
  • Uniqueness — Results of each step are uniquely defined.
  • Finiteness — Algorithm always stops after a finite number of steps.
  • Input — Algorithm receives some input.
  • Output — Algorithm produces some output.
⭐ Why do we need an Algorithm?
  • Helps programmers clearly visualize the instructions to be written.
  • Enhances the reliability, accuracy and efficiency of obtaining solution.
  • Easiest way to describe a problem without going into too much detail.
  • Lets the programmer understand the flow of the problem concisely.

Topic 3 Flowchart and Pseudocode

▼
📖 Flowchart A visual/diagrammatic representation of an algorithm using boxes, diamonds and arrows. Each shape represents a step of the solution process.
⭐ Flowchart Symbols
SymbolShapeUse
TerminalOval / Rounded RectangleStart / End
ProcessRectangleComputation / Assignment
DecisionDiamondCondition check (Yes/No)
Input/OutputParallelogramTaking input / Showing output
Flow LinesArrowShows flow between steps
📖 Pseudocode An informal way to represent an algorithm in plain English-like language. It is not real code and cannot be executed directly by a computer. Keywords used: INPUT, COMPUTE, PRINT, IF/ELSE, WHILE, TRUE/FALSE.
💡 Example — Find the larger of two numbers
INPUT num1, num2
IF num1 > num2 THEN
  PRINT "num1 is largest"
ELSE
  PRINT "num2 is largest"
ENDIF

Topic 4 Flow of Control — Selection & Repetition

▼
⭐ Types of Flow of Control
TypeMeaningKeyword
SequenceSteps execute one after another in order—
SelectionSteps execute based on a condition (True/False)IF / ELSE
RepetitionSteps execute repeatedly (in a loop)WHILE
💡 Repetition Example — Print "Hello" 10 times
SET count = 0
WHILE count < 10
  PRINT "Hello"
  count = count + 1
END WHILE

Topic 5 Decomposition

▼
📖 Decomposition Breaking a complex problem into smaller sub-problems so that each can be solved conveniently. The solutions of all sub-problems are then combined to get the final solution.
⭐ Advantages of Decomposition
  • Makes a complex problem easier to handle.
  • Reduces time and effort required.
  • Different sub-problems can be assigned to different experts.
  • Makes it easier to locate and fix errors.
📚 Chapters
💻 CS Class 11

🐍 CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 2: Chapter 4 – Getting Started with Python

🐍
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 2 · Chapter 4

Getting Started with Python

Features of Python, Keywords, Identifiers, Variables, Data Types, Operators, Input/Output, Type Conversion and Errors.

📚 4 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💻 Code Examples

Topic 1 Python — Introduction & Features

▼
📖 Python Python is a General Purpose, High Level Programming Language used for developing application software.
⭐ Features of Python
  • High Level — Human-readable language.
  • Free and Open Source — Available at no cost.
  • Case Sensitive — Name and name are different identifiers.
  • Interpreted — Executes line by line.
  • Platform Independent — Runs on any Operating System.
  • Rich Library — Large collection of built-in functions and modules.
  • Dynamic — Variable type can change at runtime.
  • General Purpose — Used for web, AI, data science, automation, etc.
⭐ Execution Modes
ModeFeature
Interactive ModeExecutes one statement at a time instantly; statements cannot be saved.
Script ModeWrite complete program and save as .py file; run later as needed.

Topic 2 Keywords, Identifiers & Variables

▼
📖 Keywords Predefined reserved words with a specific meaning to the Python interpreter. They are case-sensitive. Examples: if, else, for, while, def, True, False, None
📖 Identifier A name given to a variable, function or any other entity in a program.
⭐ Rules for Naming Identifiers
  • Must begin with a letter (a–z, A–Z) or underscore (_).
  • Can contain letters, digits (0–9) or underscores.
  • Cannot be a Python keyword.
  • Cannot contain special symbols like !, @, #, $, %.
  • Case-sensitive — Name and name are different identifiers.
📖 Variable A named memory location that stores a value. Created using an assignment statement. Example: x = 10

Topic 3 Data Types & Operators

▼
⭐ Python Data Types
Data TypeExampleDescription
int10, -5, 0Whole numbers
float3.14, -2.5Decimal numbers
complex3+4jComplex numbers
str'Hello', "World"Text / String
boolTrue, FalseBoolean values
list[1, 2, 3]Mutable ordered sequence
tuple(1, 2, 3)Immutable ordered sequence
dict{'a': 1}Key-value pairs
⭐ Python Operators
Operator TypeSymbols
Arithmetic+   -   *   /   //   %   **
Relational==   !=   >   <   >=   <=
Assignment=   +=   -=   *=   /=   //=   %=
Logicaland   or   not
Identityis   is not
Membershipin   not in
⭐ Operator Precedence (High to Low)

** → *, /, //, % → +, - → Relational → not → and → or

Topic 4 Input/Output, Type Conversion & Errors

▼
⭐ Input & Output Functions
FunctionUseExample
input()Takes input from user (always returns a string)x = input("Enter: ")
print()Displays output on screenprint("Hello", x)
⭐ Type Conversion
TypeDescriptionExample
Explicit (Type Casting)Programmer manually converts the typeint("10"), float("3.5")
Implicit (Coercion)Python automatically converts smaller type to wider type10 + 3.4 = 13.4 (int → float)
⭐ Types of Errors
ErrorWhen it occursExample
Syntax ErrorCode written against Python rules; program cannot runWrong function name, wrong indentation
Logical ErrorProgram runs but produces incorrect outputWrong condition written
Runtime ErrorProgram crashes during executionDivision by Zero

🔀 CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 2: Chapter 5 – Flow of Control

🔀
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 2 · Chapter 5

Flow of Control

if, if-else, if-elif-else, for loop, while loop, break, continue and pass — complete flow control in Python.

📚 2 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💻 Code Examples

Topic 1 Conditional Statements — if, if-else, if-elif-else

▼
📖 Conditional Statement Statements that execute different blocks of code based on whether a condition is True or False.
⭐ if Statement
if condition:
  statement(s)
⭐ if-else Statement
if condition:
  statement(s)  # executes when True
else:
  statement(s)  # executes when False
⭐ if-elif-else Statement
if condition1:
  statement(s)
elif condition2:
  statement(s)
else:
  statement(s)

Topic 2 Loops — for & while, break, continue, pass

▼
⭐ for Loop — with range()
for variable in range(start, stop, step):
  statement(s)

# Example: Print 1 to 5
for i in range(1, 6):
  print(i)
⭐ while Loop
while condition:
  statement(s)

# Example: Print 1 to 5
i = 1
while i <= 5:
  print(i)
  i = i + 1
⭐ break, continue & pass
StatementFunction
breakTerminates the loop immediately
continueSkips the current iteration and moves to the next
passDoes nothing — used as a placeholder

⚙️ CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 2: Chapter 6 – Functions

⚙️
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 2 · Chapter 6

Functions in Python

User-defined Functions, Built-in Functions, Parameters, Arguments, return statement and Scope of Variables.

📚 3 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💻 Code Examples

Topic 1 Functions — Definition and Types

▼
📖 Function A named block of code that performs a specific task and can be called multiple times. Functions increase code reusability and reduce repetition.
⭐ Types of Functions
TypeDescriptionExample
Built-in FunctionsAlready defined in Pythonprint(), input(), len(), range(), type()
User-defined FunctionsDefined by the programmerdef myFunc():
Library FunctionsImported from modulesmath.sqrt(), random.randint()

Topic 2 User-Defined Function — Syntax & Examples

▼
⭐ Defining and Calling a Function
# Defining a function
def functionName(parameters):
  statement(s)
  return value  # optional

# Calling a function
functionName(arguments)
💡 Example — Addition of two numbers
def add(a, b):
  result = a + b
  return result

total = add(5, 3)
print(total)  # Output: 8

Topic 3 Scope of Variables — Local & Global

▼
⭐ Variable Scope
TypeDefined WhereAccessible Where
Local VariableInside a functionOnly within that function
Global VariableOutside all functions (main program)Throughout the entire program
x = 10  # Global variable

def myFunc():
  y = 20  # Local variable
  print(x)  # Accessible: 10
  print(y)  # Accessible: 20

myFunc()
# print(y)  # Error! y is local to myFunc

🔤 CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 2: Chapter 7 – Strings

🔤
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 2 · Chapter 7

Strings in Python

Indexing, Slicing, String Operations, Traversing and String Functions — all in one place.

📚 3 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💻 Code Examples

Topic 1 String — Definition, Indexing & Immutability

▼
📖 String A sequence of one or more UNICODE characters enclosed in single, double or triple quotes. Strings are Immutable — their content cannot be changed after creation.
⭐ Indexing — Accessing Characters in a String
TypeStarts AtDirectionExample (s = "Hello")
Positive Index0Left to Rights[0] = 'H', s[1] = 'e'
Negative Index-1Right to Lefts[-1] = 'o', s[-2] = 'l'

💡 IndexError occurs when the index is out of range.

Topic 2 String Operations & Slicing

▼
⭐ String Operations
OperationOperatorExampleOutput
Concatenation+"Hello" + " World""Hello World"
Repetition*"Hi" * 3"HiHiHi"
Membership (in)in"He" in "Hello"True
Membership (not in)not in"Bye" not in "Hello"True
⭐ String Slicing — string[start : end : step]
# end index is always excluded
s = "Python"
s[0:3]   → "Pyt"
s[::2]   → "Pto"
s[::-1] → "nohtyP"  (reverse string)

Topic 3 String Functions

▼
⭐ Important String Functions
FunctionPurposeExample
len(s)Returns number of characterslen("Hello") → 5
s.upper()Converts to uppercase"hello".upper() → "HELLO"
s.lower()Converts to lowercase"HELLO".lower() → "hello"
s.strip()Removes leading/trailing whitespace" hi ".strip() → "hi"
s.replace(a,b)Replaces a with b"Hello".replace("l","r") → "Herro"
s.find(sub)Returns index of first occurrence"Hello".find("ll") → 2
s.count(sub)Counts occurrences of sub"hello".count("l") → 2
s.split(sep)Splits string into a list"a,b,c".split(",") → ['a','b','c']
s.isdigit()Returns True if all digits"123".isdigit() → True
s.isalpha()Returns True if all alphabets"abc".isalpha() → True

📋 CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 2: Chapter 8 – Lists

📋
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 2 · Chapter 8

Lists in Python

List creation, Indexing, Slicing, List Operations, List Methods, Traversing and Nested List.

📚 3 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💻 Code Examples

Topic 1 List — Definition, Indexing & Mutability

▼
📖 List A built-in sequence data type in Python that stores multiple values inside square brackets [ ]. Lists are Mutable — their content can be changed after creation.
⭐ Features of List
  • Stores multiple values.
  • Items can be of different data types.
  • Items are comma-separated and enclosed in square brackets.
  • Index always begins from 0.
  • Mutable — values can be changed after creation.
L = [10, "Hello", 3.14, True]
print(L[0])  # 10
print(L[-1]) # True
L[0] = 99    # Mutable — value can be changed

Topic 2 List Operations & Slicing

▼
⭐ List Operations
OperationExampleOutput
Concatenation (+)[1,2] + [3,4][1, 2, 3, 4]
Repetition (*)[1,2] * 3[1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2]
Membership (in)2 in [1,2,3]True
Slicing [s:e:step][1,2,3,4,5][1:4][2, 3, 4]

Topic 3 List Methods & Nested List

▼
⭐ Important List Methods
MethodPurposeExample
append(x)Adds element x at the endL.append(5)
insert(i, x)Inserts x at position iL.insert(1, 99)
remove(x)Removes first occurrence of xL.remove(3)
pop(i)Removes and returns element at index iL.pop(0)
sort()Sorts list in ascending orderL.sort()
reverse()Reverses the listL.reverse()
len(L)Returns length of listlen(L)
index(x)Returns index of first occurrence of xL.index(3)
count(x)Returns count of x in listL.count(2)
extend(list)Appends all items of another listL.extend([6,7])
📖 Nested List A list inside another list. Example: L = [[1,2], [3,4], [5,6]] → L[0][1] = 2

📦 CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 2: Chapter 9 – Tuples & Dictionaries

📦
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 2 · Chapter 9

Tuples & Dictionaries in Python

Tuple (Immutable sequence), Dictionary (Key-Value mapping), their methods and operations.

📚 2 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💻 Code Examples

Topic 1 Tuple — Definition & Features

▼
📖 Tuple A built-in sequence data type in Python written inside parentheses ( ). Tuples are Immutable — their content cannot be changed after creation.
⭐ List vs Tuple — Comparison
FeatureListTuple
Brackets[ ]( )
MutabilityMutableImmutable
SpeedSlowerFaster
Methods AvailableMany (append, remove, sort...)Only count() and index()
⭐ Tuple Operations
OperationExampleOutput
Concatenation(1,2) + (3,4)(1, 2, 3, 4)
Repetition(1,2) * 2(1, 2, 1, 2)
Membership3 in (1,2,3)True
Slicing(1,2,3,4)[1:3](2, 3)

Topic 2 Dictionary — Definition & Methods

▼
📖 Dictionary An unordered collection of Key:Value pairs stored in curly brackets { }. Keys must be unique and immutable. Values can be of any data type.
⭐ Creating and Accessing a Dictionary
d = {'name': 'Deepak', 'age': 25, 'city': 'Delhi'}
print(d['name'])   # Deepak
d['age'] = 26      # Modify existing item
d['email'] = 'x@x' # Add new item
⭐ Dictionary Methods
MethodPurpose
d.keys()Returns all keys
d.values()Returns all values
d.items()Returns all key-value pairs
d.get(key)Returns value of key; no KeyError if missing
d.pop(key)Removes and returns the specified key-value pair
d.update(d2)Merges another dictionary into d
len(d)Returns number of items
📚 Chapters
💻 CS Class 11
Ch 1 – Computer System Ch 2 – Encoding & Number System Ch 3 – Problem Solving Ch 4 – Getting Started with Python Ch 5 – Flow of Control Ch 6 – Functions Ch 7 – Strings Ch 8 – Lists Ch 9 – Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 10 – Society, Law & Ethics Ch 11 – Emerging Trends

⚖️ CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 3: Chapter 10 – Society, Law and Ethics

⚖️
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 3 · Chapter 10

Society, Law and Ethics

Digital Footprint, Net Etiquettes, Intellectual Property Rights, Cyber Crime, IT Act 2000 and E-Waste.

📚 4 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus ⚖️ Law & Ethics

Topic 1 Digital Footprint & Net Etiquettes

▼
📖 Digital Footprint The collection of data or records generated after any activity performed by an individual on the internet. Also called Digital Tattoos — they are permanent.
⭐ Types of Digital Footprint
TypeDescriptionExample
ActiveData shared intentionally by the userSocial media posts, filling online forms
PassiveData recorded without the user's direct knowledgeGeo-location tracking, auto-installed cookies
⭐ Net Etiquettes — Do's & Don'ts
✅ Do's❌ Don'ts
Respect others' privacyDo not violate copyright laws
Avoid cyber bullyingDo not spam
Update credentials regularlyDo not share personal/sensitive information
Be aware of your digital footprintDo not use sarcasm in communication

Topic 2 Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)

▼
📖 Intellectual Property Rights The rights granted to a person or organization over their intellectual creations such as literary works, inventions, artwork, software, etc. IPR enables creators to earn recognition or financial benefit from their work.
⭐ Types of IPR
TypeWhat it ProtectsKey Feature
CopyrightLiterary, artistic, musical and creative worksLasts 60 years; granted automatically
PatentInventions, machines and processesMust be registered with government agencies
TrademarkBrand name, symbol or logo identifying a productMust be unique; distinguishes products
⭐ IPR Violations
  • Plagiarism — Copying someone else's work and presenting it as one's own.
  • Copyright Infringement — Using copyrighted material without permission.
  • Trademark Infringement — Unauthorized use of another's trademark.
⭐ Licensing
LicenseDescription
GNU GPL (Open Source)Free to use, modify and distribute; source code is provided
Creative Commons (CC)Free license for creative works — 6 types available
ProprietaryPaid software; source code is not available

Topic 3 Cyber Crime & Indian IT Act 2000

▼
📖 Cyber Crime Any criminal offence involving the use of a computer, internet or digital technology. Can target a computer system (e.g. hacking) or use it as a tool (e.g. identity theft).
⭐ Types of Cyber Crime
CrimeDefinition
HackingGaining unauthorized access to a computer system. Ethical hacking (White hat) vs Non-ethical hacking (Black hat).
PhishingStealing sensitive information (passwords, bank details) by impersonating a legitimate entity via email or message.
Identity TheftStealing personal information (bank details, voter ID, PAN) to misuse in illegal activities.
Cyber BullyingOnline harassment or intimidation of individuals.
DoS AttackOverwhelming a server with requests to make it unavailable to users.
📖 Indian IT Act 2000 India's law governing cyber crime and e-commerce. It came into force on October 27, 2009. Key inclusions: Digital Signature, Electronic Governance, Offences and Penalties.

Topic 4 E-Waste

▼
📖 E-Waste All electrical and electronic gadgets and components that are no longer in use. E-waste contains harmful elements like cadmium, beryllium and mercury. Globally, e-waste constitutes more than 5% of municipal solid waste.
⭐ E-Waste Hazards
  • Soil acidification and air pollution.
  • Groundwater pollution with lead and heavy metals.
  • Health risks: Lung cancer, asthma, kidney and heart damage.
⭐ E-Waste Management — 3R
RMeaning
ReduceMinimize consumption of electronic gadgets; purchase only when needed.
ReuseDonate or resell electronic goods instead of discarding them.
RecycleConvert old electronic devices into something reusable.

💻 CS Class 11
Ch 1 – Computer System Ch 2 – Encoding & Number System Ch 3 – Problem Solving Ch 4 – Getting Started with Python Ch 5 – Flow of Control Ch 6 – Functions Ch 7 – Strings Ch 8 – Lists Ch 9 – Tuples & Dictionaries Ch 10 – Society, Law & Ethics Ch 11 – Emerging Trends

🚀 CS Class 11 (083) — Unit 3: Chapter 11 – Emerging Trends

🚀
CBSE Class XI · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 3 · Chapter 11

Emerging Trends in Technology

Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, NLP, IoT, Cloud Computing, Big Data, Blockchain, AR/VR and more.

📚 3 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 🌐 Latest Tech

Topic 1 Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning

▼
📖 Artificial Intelligence (AI) The technology that enables computers to simulate human-like intelligence — thinking, learning, decision-making and problem solving.
⭐ Branches of AI
BranchDescriptionExample
Machine Learning (ML)Systems that learn from data to improve performanceSpam filter, Netflix recommendations
Natural Language Processing (NLP)Enables computers to understand and process human languageSiri, Google Assistant, Chatbots
Computer VisionExtracting information from images and videosFace recognition, Medical imaging
RoboticsBuilding intelligent machines that can perform tasksIndustrial robots, Surgical robots

Topic 2 IoT, Cloud Computing & Big Data

▼
📖 Internet of Things (IoT) Connecting physical devices (sensors, gadgets) to the Internet to collect and share data without human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction. Examples: Smart Home, Smart City, Wearable devices.
📖 Cloud Computing Delivering computing services (storage, servers, databases, software) over the Internet without owning physical hardware. Examples: Google Drive, AWS, Microsoft Azure.
⭐ Cloud Service Models
ModelFull FormExample
IaaSInfrastructure as a ServiceAWS EC2, Google Compute Engine
PaaSPlatform as a ServiceGoogle App Engine, Heroku
SaaSSoftware as a ServiceGmail, Google Docs, Zoom
📖 Big Data Extremely large and complex datasets that cannot be processed by traditional tools. Characterized by the 5 V's: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity and Value.

Topic 3 Blockchain, AR/VR & Other Emerging Trends

▼
📖 Blockchain A distributed, decentralized digital ledger that records transactions in a secure and transparent manner. Each record (block) is linked to the previous one forming a chain. Used in Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies.
⭐ Immersive Technology (AR / VR / MR)
TechnologyFull FormDescription
VRVirtual RealityFully artificial simulated environment experienced through a headset
ARAugmented RealityDigital elements overlaid on the real world (e.g. Pokémon GO)
MRMixed RealityReal and virtual objects interact with each other in real time
⭐ Other Emerging Trends
  • 3D Printing — Creating physical objects directly from a digital design.
  • Smart Cities — Using IoT and AI to make cities more intelligent and efficient.
  • 5G Technology — Ultra-fast wireless connectivity for next-generation networks.
  • Cyber Security — Protecting digital systems, networks and data from cyberattacks.

🐍 CS Class 12 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 1 – Python Revision Tour

🐍
CBSE Class XII · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 1

Python Revision Tour

Variables, Operators, Expressions, Type Casting, Strings, List, Tuples, Dictionary, Flow of Control — complete Python revision as per CBSE Class 12 syllabus.

📚 4 Topics ✅ Full Syllabus 💻 Code Examples

Topic 1 Python Basics — Tokens, Variables & Operators

▼
📖 Token The smallest individual unit in a Python program. Types: Keywords, Identifiers, Literals, Operators, Punctuators.
⭐ Types of Operators
TypeOperatorsExample
Arithmetic+ - * / // % **10//3 = 3, 10%3 = 1, 2**3 = 8
Relational== != > < >= <=5 > 3 → True
Logicaland, or, notTrue and False → False
Assignment= += -= *= /=x += 5 → x = x + 5
Identityis, is notx is y
Membershipin, not in3 in [1,2,3] → True
⭐ Type Casting Functions
FunctionConverts toExample
int(x)Integerint("25") → 25
float(x)Floatfloat("3.5") → 3.5
str(x)Stringstr(10) → "10"
bool(x)Booleanbool(0) → False

Topic 2 Strings — Revision

▼
📖 String A sequence of characters enclosed in quotes. Strings are immutable and support indexing, slicing and various built-in methods.
⭐ Key String Operations
s = "Python"
s[0] → 'P'        # indexing
s[-1] → 'n'        # negative index
s[1:4] → 'yth'     # slicing
s[::-1] → 'nohtyP'  # reverse
len(s) → 6
s.upper() → 'PYTHON'
s.find('t') → 2

Topic 3 List & Tuple — Revision

▼
⭐ List vs Tuple
FeatureListTuple
Syntax[ ]( )
MutabilityMutableImmutable
Methodsappend, remove, sort, reverse...count(), index() only
⭐ Key List Methods
MethodPurpose
append(x)Add x at end
insert(i,x)Insert x at index i
remove(x)Remove first x
pop(i)Remove and return element at i
sort()Sort in ascending order
reverse()Reverse the list
extend(L)Merge another list

Topic 4 Dictionary & Flow of Control — Revision

▼
⭐ Dictionary — Key Points
  • Stores data as key:value pairs in curly braces { }.
  • Keys must be unique and immutable.
  • Methods: keys(), values(), items(), get(), pop(), update()
d = {'name':'Raj', 'age':17}
d['age'] = 18     # update
d['city'] = 'Delhi' # add new key
del d['age']      # delete key
⭐ Flow of Control — Quick Revision
StatementUse
if / elif / elseConditional execution
for var in range()Definite loop
while conditionIndefinite loop
breakExit loop immediately
continueSkip current iteration
passPlaceholder; does nothing

⚠️ CS Class 12 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 2 – Exception Handling in Python

⚠️
CBSE Class XII · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 2

Exception Handling in Python

Types of Errors, Exceptions, try-except-else-finally, raising exceptions — complete notes.

Topic 1 Errors & Exceptions

▼
📖 Exception An error that occurs during program execution (runtime) and disrupts the normal flow. Python generates an exception object when it encounters such an error.
⭐ Types of Errors
Error TypeWhenExample
Syntax ErrorBefore execution — code violates grammar rulesMissing colon, wrong indentation
Runtime Error (Exception)During executionZeroDivisionError, NameError
Logical ErrorWrong output, no crashWrong formula used
⭐ Common Built-in Exceptions
ExceptionCause
ZeroDivisionErrorDivision or modulo by zero
NameErrorVariable or function not defined
TypeErrorWrong data type used in operation
ValueErrorCorrect type but inappropriate value
IndexErrorList/string index out of range
KeyErrorDictionary key not found
FileNotFoundErrorFile does not exist
ImportErrorModule not found
IOErrorFile input/output failure

Topic 2 try-except-else-finally

▼
⭐ Exception Handling Syntax
try:
  # code that may raise exception
except ExceptionType:
  # code to handle exception
else:
  # runs if NO exception occurred
finally:
  # always runs (with or without exception)
⭐ Clauses — Purpose
ClauseExecutes When
tryAlways — contains risky code
exceptOnly when an exception is raised in try
elseOnly when try completes without exception
finallyAlways — used for cleanup (closing files, etc.)
💡 Example
try:
  a = int(input("Enter number: "))
  print(10 / a)
except ZeroDivisionError:
  print("Cannot divide by zero!")
except ValueError:
  print("Please enter a valid number!")
else:
  print("Division successful!")
finally:
  print("Program ended.")
⭐ raise Statement

Used to manually raise/force an exception.

def check_age(age):
  if age < 0:
    raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative")
  print("Valid age:", age)

📁 CS Class 12 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 3 – File Handling in Python

📁
CBSE Class XII · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 3

File Handling in Python

Text Files, Binary Files, CSV Files — open, read, write, seek, tell, pickle module.

Topic 1 File Types & Opening a File

▼
⭐ Types of Files
TypeDescriptionExtension
Text FileStores data as readable characters.txt, .py, .csv
Binary FileStores data in binary (0s and 1s).jpg, .mp3, .dat
CSV FileComma-separated values; used for tabular data.csv
⭐ File Open Modes
ModePurpose
'r'Read only (default); error if file not found
'w'Write; creates new or overwrites existing file
'a'Append; adds to end of existing file
'r+'Read and write; file must exist
'rb'Read binary file
'wb'Write binary file
# Using with clause (auto closes file)
with open("data.txt", "w") as f:
  f.write("Hello World")

Topic 2 Text File Operations

▼
⭐ Reading Functions
FunctionPurpose
read()Reads entire file as a single string
readline()Reads one line at a time
readlines()Reads all lines and returns a list
⭐ Writing Functions
FunctionPurpose
write(str)Writes a single string to the file
writelines(list)Writes a list of strings to the file
⭐ seek() and tell()
FunctionPurpose
tell()Returns current position of file pointer (in bytes)
seek(offset)Moves file pointer to the given offset position

Topic 3 Binary Files & CSV Files

▼
⭐ Binary File — pickle Module
import pickle

# Write to binary file
with open("data.dat", "wb") as f:
  pickle.dump(obj, f)

# Read from binary file
with open("data.dat", "rb") as f:
  obj = pickle.load(f)
  • dump(obj, file) — Serializes and writes object to binary file.
  • load(file) — Reads and deserializes object from binary file.
⭐ CSV File — csv Module
import csv

# Write CSV
with open("data.csv","w",newline='') as f:
  w = csv.writer(f)
  w.writerow(["Name","Age"])

# Read CSV
with open("data.csv","r") as f:
  r = csv.reader(f)
  for row in r:
    print(row)

📚 CS Class 12 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 4 – Stack

📚
CBSE Class XII · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 4

Stack

Stack definition, LIFO principle, Applications, Push, Pop, Peek, isEmpty, isFull operations with Python implementation.

Topic 1 Stack — Definition, Terminologies & Applications

▼
📖 Stack A linear data structure that follows the LIFO (Last In First Out) principle — the last element inserted is the first one to be removed. Like a stack of plates.
⭐ Stack Terminologies
TermMeaning
TOPIndex of the topmost element in the stack
PushInsert an element at the TOP of the stack
PopRemove the element from TOP of the stack
Peek/TopView the TOP element without removing it
OverflowPushing into a full stack
UnderflowPopping from an empty stack
⭐ Applications of Stack
  • Undo/Redo operations in text editors
  • Browser Back button (navigation history)
  • Reversing a string or sequence
  • Expression evaluation (infix to postfix conversion)
  • Function call management (call stack)

Topic 2 Stack Operations — Python Implementation

▼
⭐ Stack Implementation using List
stack = []

# Push
def push(stack, item):
  stack.append(item)
  print("Pushed:", item)

# Pop
def pop(stack):
  if stack == []:
    print("Stack Underflow!")
  else:
    return stack.pop()

# Peek
def peek(stack):
  if stack == []:
    return None
  return stack[-1]

# isEmpty
def isEmpty(stack):
  return stack == []

🐍 CS Class 12

🔢 CS Class 12 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 5 – Queue

🔢
CBSE Class XII · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 5

Queue

FIFO principle, Enqueue, Dequeue, Applications of Queue with Python implementation.

Topic 1 Queue — Definition, Types & Applications

▼
📖 Queue A linear data structure that follows the FIFO (First In First Out) principle — the first element inserted is the first one to be removed. Like a line at a ticket counter.
⭐ Queue Terminologies
TermMeaning
FRONTIndex pointing to the first element (for deletion)
REARIndex pointing to the last element (for insertion)
EnqueueInsert element at the REAR end
DequeueDelete element from the FRONT end
OverflowEnqueue into a full queue
UnderflowDequeue from an empty queue
⭐ Applications of Queue
  • CPU scheduling (process queue)
  • Print spooling (printer queue)
  • Keyboard input buffer
  • BFS (Breadth First Search) in graphs

Topic 2 Queue Operations — Python Implementation

▼
⭐ Queue Implementation using List
queue = []

# Enqueue
def enqueue(queue, item):
  queue.append(item)
  print("Enqueued:", item)

# Dequeue
def dequeue(queue):
  if queue == []:
    print("Queue Underflow!")
  else:
    return queue.pop(0)

# Peek (Front element)
def peek(queue):
  if queue == []:
    return None
  return queue[0]

# isEmpty
def isEmpty(queue):
  return queue == []
⭐ Stack vs Queue
FeatureStackQueue
PrincipleLIFOFIFO
Insert atTOPREAR
Delete fromTOPFRONT
Python insertappend()append()
Python deletepop()pop(0)
📚 Chapters
🐍 CS Class 12
Ch 1 – Python Revision Tour Ch 2 – Exception Handling Ch 3 – File Handling Ch 4 – Stack Ch 5 – Queue Ch 6 – Sorting Algorithms Ch 7 – Searching Algorithms Ch 8 – Computer Networks Ch 9 – Data Communication Ch 10 – Security Aspects Ch 11 – Understanding Data Ch 12 – Database Concepts Ch 13 – SQL

🔃 CS Class 12 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 6 – Sorting Algorithms

🔃
CBSE Class XII · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 6

Sorting Algorithms

Bubble Sort and Insertion Sort — algorithm, dry run and Python code.

Topic 1 Bubble Sort

▼
📖 Bubble Sort A simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. After each pass, the largest unsorted element "bubbles up" to its correct position.
⭐ Bubble Sort Algorithm
  • Perform N-1 passes for an array of N elements.
  • In each pass, compare adjacent pairs and swap if needed.
  • After each pass, the largest element settles at the end.
def bubbleSort(arr):
  n = len(arr)
  for i in range(n-1):
    for j in range(n-1-i):
      if arr[j] > arr[j+1]:
        arr[j], arr[j+1] = arr[j+1], arr[j]
  return arr

Topic 2 Insertion Sort

▼
📖 Insertion Sort Builds the sorted array one element at a time by picking each element and inserting it at the correct position in the already-sorted portion. Works like sorting playing cards in hand.
⭐ Insertion Sort Algorithm
def insertionSort(arr):
  for i in range(1, len(arr)):
    key = arr[i]
    j = i - 1
    while j >= 0 and arr[j] > key:
      arr[j+1] = arr[j]
      j -= 1
    arr[j+1] = key
  return arr
⭐ Bubble Sort vs Insertion Sort
FeatureBubble SortInsertion Sort
MethodCompare and swap adjacent elementsPick element and insert at correct position
Best CaseO(n²)O(n) — already sorted list
EfficiencySlowerBetter for small/nearly sorted data
📚 Chapters
🐍 CS Class 12
Ch 1 – Python Revision Tour Ch 2 – Exception Handling Ch 3 – File Handling Ch 4 – Stack Ch 5 – Queue Ch 6 – Sorting Algorithms Ch 7 – Searching Algorithms Ch 8 – Computer Networks Ch 9 – Data Communication Ch 10 – Security Aspects Ch 11 – Understanding Data Ch 12 – Database Concepts Ch 13 – SQL

🔍 CS Class 12 (083) — Unit 1: Chapter 7 – Searching Algorithms

🔍
CBSE Class XII · Computer Science Code 083 · Unit 1 · Chapter 7

Searching Algorithms

Linear Search and Binary Search — algorithm, working, Python code and comparison.

Topic 1 Linear Search

▼
📖 Linear Search Searches for an element by checking each element of the list one by one from left to right until the element is found or the list ends. Works on both sorted and unsorted lists.
⭐ Linear Search — Python Code
def linearSearch(arr, key):
  for i in range(len(arr)):
    if arr[i] == key:
      return i  # returns index
  return -1  # not found

Topic 2 Binary Search

▼
📖 Binary Search An efficient search algorithm that works on sorted lists only. It repeatedly divides the search space in half by comparing the target with the middle element.
⭐ Binary Search — Python Code
def binarySearch(arr, key):
  low = 0
  high = len(arr) - 1
  while low <= high:
    mid = (low + high) // 2
    if arr[mid] == key:
      return mid
    elif arr[mid] < key:
      low = mid + 1
    else:
      high = mid - 1
  return -1
⭐ Linear vs Binary Search
FeatureLinear SearchBinary Search
Works onSorted and UnsortedSorted only
MethodChecks each element one by oneDivides list in half each time
SpeedSlower — O(n)Faster — O(log n)
Best forSmall listsLarge sorted lists

🐍 CS Class 12
